Method: Proposes SIF with two key components: 1) Semantic-Aligned Fingerprint Distillation (SAFD) transfers text watermarking signals to visual modality for semantically coherent fingerprinted responses, and 2) Robust-Fingerprint Optimization (RFO) enhances robustness by simulating worst-case representation perturbations against model modifications.

Result: Extensive experiments on LLaVA-1.5 and Qwen2.5-VL demonstrate SIF achieves strong stealthiness and robustness, providing practical copyright protection against model modifications like fine-tuning and quantization.

Conclusion: SIF offers a non-intrusive, semantically-preserving fingerprinting solution that addresses vulnerabilities in existing LVLM ownership verification methods, enabling effective copyright protection without model parameter modifications.

Abstract: The public accessibility of large vision-language models (LVLMs) raises serious concerns about unauthorized model reuse and intellectual property infringement. Existing ownership verification methods often rely on semantically abnormal queries or out-of-distribution responses as fingerprints, which can be easily detected and removed by adversaries. We expose this vulnerability through a Semantic Divergence Attack (SDA), which identifies and filters fingerprint queries by measuring semantic divergence between a suspect model and a reference model, showing that existing fingerprints are not semantic-preserving and are therefore easy to detect and bypass. To address these limitations, we propose SIF (Semantically In-Distribution Fingerprints), a non-intrusive ownership verification framework that requires no parameter modification. SIF introduces Semantic-Aligned Fingerprint Distillation (SAFD), which transfers text watermarking signals into the visual modality to produce semantically coherent yet fingerprinted responses. In addition, Robust-Fingerprint Optimization (RFO) enhances robustness by simulating worst-case representation perturbations, making the fingerprints resilient to model modifications such as fine-tuning and quantization. Extensive experiments on LLaVA-1.5 and Qwen2.5-VL demonstrate that SIF achieves strong stealthiness and robustness, providing a practical solution for LVLM copyright protection. Code is available at https://github.com/UCF-ML-Research/SIF-VLM-Fingerprint

</details>


### [110] [A Real-Time Bike-Pedestrian Safety System with Wide-Angle Perception and Evaluation Testbed for Urban Intersections](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17046)
*Mehmet Kerem Turkcan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Real-time collision warning system using fisheye camera on edge device detects cyclist-pedestrian conflicts at intersections with 93.3% sensitivity and 3.3s warning time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cyclist-pedestrian collisions at urban intersections cause persistent injuries, but few systems provide real-time warnings to unequipped road users using affordable hardware.

Method: Four-part system: 1) fisheye calibration pipeline with perspective remapping and bundle adjustment, 2) fisheye-aware object detection with ground-plane projection via lookup table, 3) conformance simulation with hazard scenarios and latency analysis, 4) auditable decision layer with deployment gates and risk management.

Result: System achieves 93.3% sensitivity and 92.3% specificity with 3.3s mean warning budget, running at 30fps on edge device. Community workshops informed design.

Conclusion: Prototype demonstrates feasible real-time collision warning using commodity hardware with robust performance metrics and safety engineering principles.

Abstract: Collisions between cyclists and pedestrians at urban intersections remain a persistent source of injuries, yet few systems attempt real-time warnings to unequipped road users using commodity hardware. We present a prototype collision warning system that runs on a single edge device with a wide-angle fisheye camera, producing audible and visual alerts at 30\,fps. The system makes four contributions. First, we develop a calibration pipeline for ultra-wide fisheye lenses that overcomes corner-detection failure and optimizer divergence through perspective remapping and direct bundle adjustment. Second, we combine fisheye-aware object detection with a closed-form ground-plane projection via a precomputed lookup table. Third, we introduce a design-time conformance simulation with 24 scripted hazard scenarios, stochastic size-aware detection failures, and a latency sweep showing that a first-order kinematic predictor maintains the mean warning budget above the distracted-pedestrian reaction time across realistic camera latencies. Fourth, we formalize the decision layer as a separable, auditable testbench with explicit deployment gates, contestability mechanisms, and a residual risk register. Under conformance testing with fisheye localization error, the selected pipeline configuration achieves 93.3\% sensitivity and 92.3\% specificity, with a mean warning budget of 3.3\,s. The system design was informed by community-aided design workshops. Code and replication scripts are available at https://github.com/mkturkcan/bikeped.

</details>


### [111] [OASIS: On-Demand Hierarchical Event Memory for Streaming Video Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17052)
*Zhijia Liang,Jiaming Li,Weikai Chen,Yanhao Zhang,Haonan Lu,Guanbin Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: OASIS is a streaming video reasoning framework that uses hierarchical event organization and intent-driven retrieval to efficiently find relevant information in long video streams without excessive memory usage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Streaming video reasoning faces the challenge of growing history with scarce meaningful evidence - relevant signal is small and easily lost in redundancy. Traditional approaches of enlarging memory or aggressive compression both fail to address the core problem of discovering where to look.

Method: Organizes streaming history into hierarchical events and performs reasoning as controlled refinement: short-context inference first, followed by semantically grounded retrieval only when uncertainty arises. Uses high-level intent-driven retrieval rather than embedding similarity, making it plug-and-play and training-free.

Result: Achieves strong gains in long-horizon accuracy and compositional reasoning across multiple benchmarks and backbones, with bounded token cost and low request delay.

Conclusion: OASIS effectively addresses the streaming video reasoning challenge by focusing on structured, on-demand retrieval that finds relevant information efficiently without excessive memory consumption.

Abstract: Streaming video reasoning requires models to operate in a setting where history grows without bound while meaningful evidence remains scarce. In such a landscape, relevant signal is like an oasis-small, critical, and easily lost in a desert of redundancy. Enlarging memory only widens the desert; aggressive compression dries up the oasis. The real difficulty lies in discovering where to look, not how much to remember. We therefore introduce OASIS, a novel framework for streaming video reasoning that tackles this challenge through structured, on-demand retrieval. It organizes streaming history into hierarchical events and performs reasoning as controlled refinement-short-context inference first, followed by semantically grounded retrieval only when uncertainty arises. As the retrieval is driven by high-level intent rather than embedding similarity, the retrieved memory is substantially more accurate and less noisy. Additionally, the mechanism is plug-and-play, training-free, and readily attaches to different streaming MLLM backbones. Experiments across multiple benchmarks and backbones show that OASIS achieves strong gains in long-horizon accuracy and compositional reasoning with bounded token cost and low request delay. Code is available at https://github.com/Solus-sano/OASIS.

</details>


### [112] [mEOL: Training-Free Instruction-Guided Multimodal Embedder for Vector Graphics and Image Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17054)
*Kyeong Seon Kim,Baek Seong-Eun,Lee Jung-Mok,Tae-Hyun Oh*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Training-free multimodal embedding framework using MLLM to align text, images, and SVG code through instruction guidance, outperforming trained baselines on SVG retrieval.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SVGs contain rich structural information that gets lost when rasterized, while text embedding methods don't extend well to visual/structured modalities. Need a way to align multimodal representations without extensive training.

Method: Two components: 1) mEOL (Multimodal Explicit One-word Limitation) - instructs MLLM to summarize any input into single token embedding; 2) Semantic SVG rewriting - assigns meaningful identifiers and simplifies nested elements through visual reasoning over rendered images.

Result: Outperforms encoder-based and training-based multimodal baselines on new text-to-SVG retrieval benchmark built from repurposed VGBench, demonstrating prompt-level control as effective alternative to parameter-level training.

Conclusion: Instruction-guided multimodal embedding with MLLMs enables structure-aware retrieval without training, showing prompt-level control can effectively align diverse modalities while preserving SVG structural information.

Abstract: Scalable Vector Graphics (SVGs) function both as visual images and as structured code that encode rich geometric and layout information, yet most methods rasterize them and discard this symbolic organization. At the same time, recent sentence embedding methods produce strong text representations but do not naturally extend to visual or structured modalities. We propose a training-free, instruction-guided multimodal embedding framework that uses a Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) to map text, raster images, and SVG code into an aligned embedding space. We control the direction of embeddings through modality-specific instructions and structural SVG cues, eliminating the need for learned projection heads or contrastive training. Our method has two key components: (1) Multimodal Explicit One-word Limitation (mEOL), which instructs the MLLM to summarize any multimodal input into a single token whose hidden state serves as a compact semantic embedding. (2) A semantic SVG rewriting module that assigns meaningful identifiers and simplifies nested SVG elements through visual reasoning over the rendered image, exposing geometric and relational cues hidden in raw code. Using a repurposed VGBench, we build the first text-to-SVG retrieval benchmark and show that our training-free embeddings outperform encoder-based and training-based multimodal baselines. These results highlight prompt-level control as an effective alternative to parameter-level training for structure-aware multimodal retrieval. Project page: https://scene-the-ella.github.io/meol/

</details>


### [113] [Motion-Guided Semantic Alignment with Negative Prompts for Zero-Shot Video Action Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17062)
*Yiming Wang,Frederick W. B. Li,Jingyun Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Novel CLIP-based framework with disentangled motion features and semantic alignment for zero-shot action recognition, outperforming prior methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Zero-shot action recognition faces challenges due to semantic gap between seen and unseen classes, requiring better feature representation and semantic alignment.

Method: Uses Motion Separation Module to disentangle motion-sensitive and global-static features, Motion Aggregation Block with gated cross-attention for refined motion representation, and semantic alignment between video features and textual prompts (both positive and negative).

Result: Consistently outperforms prior CLIP-based approaches on standard benchmarks, achieving robust zero-shot action recognition across both coarse and fine-grained datasets.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively addresses the semantic gap in zero-shot action recognition through disentangled feature learning and semantic alignment, demonstrating superior generalization to unseen categories.

Abstract: Zero-shot action recognition is challenging due to the semantic gap between seen and unseen classes. We present a novel framework that enhances CLIP with disentangled embeddings and semantic-guided interaction. A Motion Separation Module (MSM) separates motion-sensitive and global-static features, while a Motion Aggregation Block (MAB) employs gated cross-attention to refine motion representation without re-coupling redundant information. To facilitate generalization to unseen categories, we enforce semantic alignment between video features and textual representations by aligning projected embeddings with positive textual prompts, while leveraging negative prompts to explicitly model "non-class" semantics. Experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms prior CLIP-based approaches, achieving robust zero-shot action recognition across both coarse and fine-grained datasets.

</details>


### [114] [BasketHAR: A Multimodal Dataset for Human Activity Recognition and Sport Analysis in Basketball Training Scenarios](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17065)
*Xian Gao,Haoyue Zhang,Zongyun Zhang,Jiacheng Ruan,Ting Liu,Yuzhuo Fu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: BasketHAR: A multimodal basketball training dataset with IMU, physiological, and video data for advanced human activity recognition in sports analytics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing HAR datasets focus on basic activities and lack specialized sports contexts like basketball training, limiting their utility for professional-level performance analysis.

Method: Created BasketHAR dataset with multimodal data including inertial measurement units (accelerometers, gyroscopes), angular velocity, magnetic field, heart rate, skin temperature, and synchronized video recordings for professional basketball actions.

Result: Experimental results demonstrate the dataset's complexity and suitability for advanced HAR tasks, with a baseline multimodal alignment method provided for benchmarking.

Conclusion: BasketHAR represents a valuable resource for HAR and sports analytics research, enabling analysis of basketball training sessions and generation of specialized performance reports, publicly available under Apache License 2.0.

Abstract: Human Activity Recognition (HAR) involves the automatic identification of user activities and has gained significant research interest due to its broad applicability. Most HAR systems rely on supervised learning, which necessitates large, diverse, and well-annotated datasets. However, existing datasets predominantly focus on basic activities such as walking, standing, and stair navigation, limiting their utility in specialized contexts like sports performance analysis. To address this gap, we present BasketHAR, a novel multimodal HAR dataset tailored for basketball training, encompassing a diverse set of professional-level actions. BasketHAR includes comprehensive motion data from inertial measurement units (accelerometers and gyroscopes), angular velocity, magnetic field, heart rate, skin temperature, and synchronized video recordings. We also provide a baseline multimodal alignment method to benchmark performance. Experimental results underscore the dataset's complexity and suitability for advanced HAR tasks. Furthermore, we highlight its potential applications in the analysis of basketball training sessions and in the generation of specialized performance reports, representing a valuable resource for future research in HAR and sports analytics. The dataset are publicly accessible at https://huggingface.co/datasets/Xian-Gao/BasketHAR licensed under Apache License 2.0.

</details>


### [115] [NTIRE 2026 Rip Current Detection and Segmentation (RipDetSeg) Challenge Report](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17070)
*Andrei Dumitriu,Aakash Ralhan,Florin Miron,Florin Tatui,Radu Tudor Ionescu,Radu Timofte,Abdullah Naeem,Anav Katwal,Ayon Dey,Md Tamjidul Hoque,Asuka Shin,Hiroto Shirono,Kosuke Shigematsu,Gaurav Mahesh,Anjana Nanditha,Jiji CV,Akbarali Vakhitov,Sang-Chul Lee,Xinger Li,Chun'an Yu,Junhao Chen,Yang Yang,Gundluri Yuvateja Reddy,Harshitha Palaram,Gejalakshmi N,Jeevitha S,Jiachen Tu,Guoyi Xu,Yaoxin Jiang,Jiajia Liu,Yaokun Shi,Amitabh Tripathi,Modugumudi Mahesh,Santosh Kumar Vipparthi,Subrahmanyam Murala*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: NTIRE 2026 RipDetSeg Challenge for automatic rip current detection/segmentation in images, using diverse RipVIS benchmark with 159 participants and 9 valid submissions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rip currents cause many beach fatalities worldwide but are difficult to identify due to visual variability across beaches, viewpoints, and sea conditions, requiring automated detection methods for safety.

Method: Challenge uses RipVIS benchmark dataset from 10+ countries with 4 camera orientations and diverse conditions. Participants submitted detection and segmentation solutions evaluated using composite score of F1[50], F2[50], F1[40:95], and F2[40:95].

Result: Most solutions used pretrained models with strong augmentation and post-processing. Results show rip current understanding benefits from robust general-purpose vision models but still has room for improvement with specialized methods.

Conclusion: The challenge successfully advanced rip current research, demonstrating current capabilities while highlighting need for future methods tailored to rip currents' unique visual structure.

Abstract: This report presents the NTIRE 2026 Rip Current Detection and Segmentation (RipDetSeg) Challenge, which targets automatic rip current understanding in images. Rip currents are hazardous nearshore flows that cause many beach-related fatalities worldwide, yet remain difficult to identify because their visual appearance varies substantially across beaches, viewpoints, and sea states. To advance research on this safety-critical problem, the challenge builds on the RipVIS benchmark, evaluating both detection and segmentation. The dataset is diverse, sourced from more than $10$ countries, with $4$ camera orientations and diverse beach and sea conditions. This report describes the dataset, challenge protocol, evaluation methodology, final results, and summarizes the main insights from the submitted methods. The challenge attracted $159$ registered participants and produced $9$ valid test submissions across the two tasks. Final rankings are based on a composite score that combines $F_1[50]$, $F_2[50]$, $F_1[40\!:\!95]$, and $F_2[40\!:\!95]$. Most participant solutions relied on pretrained models, combined with strong augmentation and post-processing design. These results suggest that rip current understanding benefits strongly from the robust general-purpose vision models' progress, while leaving ample room for future methods tailored to their unique visual structure.

</details>


### [116] [Comparison Drives Preference: Reference-Aware Modeling for AI-Generated Video Quality Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17074)
*Minghao Zou,Gen Liu,Guanghui Yue,Baoquan Zhao,Zhihua Wang,Paul L. Rosin,Hantao Liu,Wei Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RefVQA: A reference-aware video quality assessment method for AI-generated videos that uses graph-based comparisons between related videos instead of analyzing each video independently.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AI-generated video quality assessment methods analyze each video independently, ignoring relationships between videos. Human perception naturally compares related content, so a reference-aware approach that considers inter-video relationships would better align with how humans assess quality.

Method: Proposes Reference-aware Video Quality Assessment (RefVQA) that uses a query-centered reference graph to organize semantically related samples and performs graph-guided difference aggregation from reference nodes to the query node for quality assessment.

Result: RefVQA outperforms state-of-the-art methods across multiple quality dimensions on existing datasets, with strong generalization ability validated by cross-dataset evaluation.

Conclusion: The reference-based formulation is effective for AI-generated video quality assessment and has potential to advance the field by better aligning with human perception through inter-video comparisons.

Abstract: The rapid advancement of generative models has led to a growing volume of AI-generated videos, making the automatic quality assessment of such videos increasingly important. Existing AI-generated content video quality assessment (AIGC-VQA) methods typically estimate visual quality by analyzing each video independently, ignoring potential relationships among videos. In this work, we revisit AIGC-VQA from an inter-video perspective and formulate it as a reference-aware evaluation problem. Through this formulation, quality assessment is guided not only by intrinsic video characteristics but also by comparisons with related videos, which is more consistent with human perception. To validate its effectiveness, we propose Reference-aware Video Quality Assessment (RefVQA), which utilizes a query-centered reference graph to organize semantically related samples and performs graph-guided difference aggregation from the reference nodes to the query node. Experiments on existing datasets demonstrate that our proposed RefVQA outperforms state-of-the-art methods across multiple quality dimensions, with strong generalization ability validated by cross-dataset evaluation. These results highlight the effectiveness of the proposed reference-based formulation and suggest its potential to advance AIGC-VQA.

</details>


### [117] [D-Prism: Differentiable Primitives for Structured Dynamic Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17082)
*Xingyuan Yu,Yijin Li,Chong Zeng,Yuhang Ming,Hujun Bao,Guofeng Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: D-Prism: First framework for high-fidelity structured dynamic modeling using differentiable primitives with 3DGS binding and adaptive primitive control for articulated objects.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing dynamic methods (deformable meshes, 3DGS) fail to jointly model suitable geometry and articulated motion for structured dynamic objects like multi-part assemblies or jointed mechanisms. Primitive-based methods work well for static scenes but haven't been explored for dynamic scenarios.

Method: Extends differentiable primitives to dynamic domain by binding 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) to primitive surfaces, using a deformation network to control primitive motion, and implementing adaptive control strategy to dynamically adjust primitive counts based on objects' spatial footprint.

Result: Experiments confirm the method excels at structured dynamic modeling, providing both structured geometry and precise motion tracking for articulated objects.

Conclusion: D-Prism successfully addresses the challenge of capturing both geometry and rigid motion for structured dynamic objects, achieving high-fidelity structured dynamic modeling through the novel combination of differentiable primitives, 3DGS binding, and adaptive control.

Abstract: Capturing both geometry and rigid motion for structured dynamic objects, like multi-part assemblies or jointed mechanisms, remains a key challenge. Existing dynamic methods, such as deformable meshes or 3DGS, rely on unstructured representations and fail to jointly model suitable geometry and articulated motion. Primitive-based methods excel at structured static scenes, but their dynamic potential is still unexplored. We propose D-Prism, the first framework to achieve high-fidelity structured dynamic modeling by extending differentiable primitives to the dynamic domain. Specifically, we bind 3DGS to primitive surfaces, leveraging their respective strengths in appearance and geometry. We introduce a deformation network to control primitive motion, ensuring it accurately matches the object's movement. Furthermore, we design a novel adaptive control strategy to dynamically adjust primitive counts, better matching objects' true spatial footprint. Experiments confirm that our method excels at structured dynamic modeling, providing both structured geometry and precise motion tracking.

</details>


### [118] [EvoComp: Learning Visual Token Compression for Multimodal Large Language Models via Semantic-Guided Evolutionary Labeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17087)
*Jiafei Song,Fengwei Zhou,Jin Qu,Wenjin Jason Li,Tong Wu,Gengjian Xue,Zhikang Zhao,Daomin Wei,Yichao Lu,Bailin Na*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EvoComp is a visual token compression framework for MLLMs that reduces token count while preserving accuracy, achieving 99.3% accuracy with 3x compression and 1.6x speedup on mobile devices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MLLMs suffer from inference inefficiency due to large numbers of visual tokens, especially in high-resolution or multi-image scenarios, which hampers practical deployment.

Method: Uses a lightweight encoder-only transformer compressor to select informative, non-redundant tokens by considering visual and textual contexts. Employs evolutionary labeling strategy to find token subsets minimizing MLLM output loss with semantic diversity through vocabulary-based grouping. Uses GHM loss for class/difficulty imbalance and cosine similarity regularization for semantic separation.

Result: Outperforms existing attention/similarity-based methods across multiple vision-language benchmarks. Retains 99.3% original accuracy with 3x token compression and achieves up to 1.6x speedup on mobile devices.

Conclusion: EvoComp effectively addresses visual token redundancy in MLLMs, significantly improving inference efficiency while maintaining task accuracy, making it suitable for practical deployment on resource-constrained devices.

Abstract: Recent Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated strong performance on vision-language understanding tasks, yet their inference efficiency is often hampered by the large number of visual tokens, particularly in high-resolution or multi-image scenarios. To address this issue, we propose EvoComp, a visual token compression framework that significantly reduces token count while preserving task accuracy. EvoComp introduces a lightweight encoder-only transformer-based compressor that selects the most informative and non-redundant visual tokens by jointly considering visual and textual contexts. A core challenge lies in providing effective supervision for training the compressor. To this end, we design an evolutionary labeling strategy that searches for token subsets minimizing the MLLM's output loss, while enforcing semantic diversity through vocabulary-based token grouping. We further train the compressor using a tailored loss function combining the GHM loss to mitigate class and difficulty imbalance, and a cosine similarity regularization to encourage semantic separation between retained and discarded tokens. Extensive experiments across multiple vision-language benchmarks show that EvoComp outperforms existing methods based on attention or similarity heuristics. Notably, it retains 99.3% of the original accuracy under 3x token compression and delivers up to 1.6x speedup on mobile devices.

</details>


### [119] [Marrying Text-to-Motion Generation with Skeleton-Based Action Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17090)
*Jidong Kuang,Hongsong Wang,Jie Gui*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CoAMD is a unified framework for both action recognition and motion generation using skeleton coordinates, achieving SOTA across 13 benchmarks through coarse-to-fine motion synthesis with semantic guidance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing works treat action recognition and motion generation as separate problems, missing their inherent connection where motion generation requires semantic comprehension. The paper aims to unify these tasks to leverage their mutual benefits.

Method: Proposes Coordinates-based Autoregressive Motion Diffusion (CoAMD) with Multi-modal Action Recognizer (MAR) for gradient-based semantic guidance, using skeleton coordinates for coarse-to-fine motion synthesis in absolute coordinates.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on 13 benchmarks across four tasks: skeleton-based action recognition, text-to-motion generation, text-motion retrieval, and motion editing.

Conclusion: CoAMD demonstrates effectiveness and versatility for unified human motion modeling, bridging the gap between understanding and generation while establishing rigorous evaluation on absolute coordinates.

Abstract: Human action recognition and motion generation are two active research problems in human-centric computer vision, both aiming to align motion with textual semantics. However, most existing works study these two problems separately, without uncovering the links between them, namely that motion generation requires semantic comprehension. This work investigates unified action recognition and motion generation by leveraging skeleton coordinates for both motion understanding and generation. We propose Coordinates-based Autoregressive Motion Diffusion (CoAMD), which synthesizes motion in a coarse-to-fine manner. As a core component of CoAMD, we design a Multi-modal Action Recognizer (MAR) that provides gradient-based semantic guidance for motion generation. Furthermore, we establish a rigorous benchmark by evaluating baselines on absolute coordinates. Our model can be applied to four important tasks, including skeleton-based action recognition, text-to-motion generation, text-motion retrieval, and motion editing. Extensive experiments on 13 benchmarks across these tasks demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance, highlighting its effectiveness and versatility for human motion modeling. Code is available at https://github.com/jidongkuang/CoAMD.

</details>


### [120] [Hybrid Multi-Dimensional MRI Prostate Cancer Detection via Hadamard Network-Based Bias Correction and Residual Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17107)
*Emadeldeen Hamdan,Gorkem Durak,Muhammed Enes Tasci,Abel Lorente Campos,Aritrick Chatterjee,Roger Engelmann,Gregory Karczma,Aytekin Oto,Ahmet Enis Cetin,Ulas Bagci*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HBR-Net-18: A two-stage AI framework combining Hadamard U-Net bias field correction with ResNet-18 classification for prostate cancer detection using HM-MRI, outperforming conventional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite advances in Hybrid Multi-dimensional MRI (HM-MRI) for prostate cancer diagnosis, there's a significant need for robust, automated AI-based detection methods to improve diagnostic accuracy and consistency.

Method: Two-stage framework: 1) Hadamard U-Net suppresses intensity inhomogeneities across six parametric HM-MRI maps from Physics-Informed Autoencoder, 2) ResNet-18 performs patch-level classification using overlapping 11×11 patches with both 2D intra-slice and 3D inter-slice information.

Result: HB-Net achieves balanced sensitivity and specificity, significantly outperforming conventional radiomics-based approaches and baseline CNN models.

Conclusion: The proposed HBR-Net-18 framework demonstrates strong potential for clinical deployment in prostate cancer detection, offering improved spatial consistency and diagnostic performance over existing methods.

Abstract: Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) is vital for prostate cancer (PCa) diagnosis. While advanced techniques such as Hybrid Multi-dimensional MRI (HM-MRI) have enhanced diagnostic capabilities, the significant need remains for robust, automated Artificial Intelligence (AI)-based detection methods. In this study, we combine quantitative HM-MRI of tissue composition with an AI-based neural network. We propose the Hadamard-Bias Network plus ResNet18 (HBR-Net-18), a two-stage AI framework for PCa detection. In the first stage, a Hadamard U-Net-based algorithm suppresses intensity inhomogeneities (bias fields) across six parametric HM-MRI maps generated via a Physics-Informed Autoencoder (PIA). In the second stage, a Residual Network (ResNet-18) performs patch-level classification. The framework utilizes overlapping 11-by-11 patches, incorporating both 2D intra-slice and 3D inter-slice (adjacent-slice) information to improve spatial consistency. Our experimental results demonstrate that HB-Net achieves balanced sensitivity and specificity, significantly outperforming conventional radiomics-based approaches and baseline CNN models, highlighting its potential for clinical deployment.

</details>


### [121] [From Clinical Intent to Clinical Model: An Autonomous Coding-Agent Framework for Clinician-driven AI Development](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17110)
*Zihao Zhao,Frederik Hauke,Juliana De Castilhos,Jakob Nikolas Kather,Sven Nebelung,Daniel Truhn*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Autonomous coding agents enable clinicians to independently develop clinical AI models through natural language, reducing dependence on specialized AI teams and communication overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional clinical AI development requires extensive collaboration between clinicians and AI teams, which is time-consuming and prone to misalignment due to expertise gaps. There's a need to empower clinicians to develop AI models independently.

Method: Developed an autonomous prototype system that allows clinicians to create clinical AI models through natural-language interaction. Evaluated on five clinical tasks: dermoscopic lesion classification, melanoma-vs-nevus triage, wrist-fracture detection (including weakly supervised variant with 5% bounding-box annotations), and debiased pneumothorax classification on chest radiographs.

Result: The system consistently developed models from clinician requests with promising performance. Notably, in debiased pneumothorax classification, it successfully mitigated shortcut learning and nearly halved the model's reliance on chest drains as confounders.

Conclusion: Autonomous coding agents can shift clinical AI development toward a clinician-driven paradigm, reducing communication overhead and dependence on specialized AI developers. While further validation is needed, this represents a promising path toward making clinical AI development more accessible.

Abstract: Clinical AI development has traditionally followed a collaborative paradigm that depends on close interaction between clinicians and specialized AI teams. This paradigm imposes a practical challenge: clinicians must repeatedly communicate and refine their requirements with AI developers before those requirements can be translated into executable model development. This iterative process is time-consuming, and even after repeated discussion, misalignment may still exist because the two sides do not fully share each other's expertise. However, autonomous coding agents may change this paradigm, raising the possibility that clinicians could develop clinical AI models independently through natural-language interaction alone. In this study, we present such an autonomous prototype for clinician-driven clinical AI development. We evaluated the system on five clinical tasks spanning dermoscopic lesion classification, melanoma-versus-nevus triage, wrist-fracture detection (including a weakly supervised variant with only 5% bounding-box annotations), and debiased pneumothorax classification on chest radiographs. Across these settings, the system consistently developed models from clinician requests and achieved promising performance. Notably, in a debiased pneumothorax classification task on chest radiographs, where chest drains can act as a major confounder, the system successfully mitigated shortcut learning and nearly halved the model's reliance on chest drains. These findings provide proof of concept that autonomous coding agents may help shift clinical AI development toward a more clinician-driven paradigm, reducing the communication overhead and dependence on specialized AI developers. Although further validation and robustness assessment are needed, this study suggests a promising path toward making clinical AI development more accessible.

</details>


### [122] [Inference-Time Temporal Probability Smoothing for Stable Video Segmentation with SAM2 under Weak Prompts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17115)
*Dawar Jyoti Deka*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes temporal probability smoothing to fix SAM2's flickering video segmentation issues without retraining, using optical flow and uncertainty to blend predictions across frames.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: SAM2 has strong generalization but suffers from temporal instability (flickering boundaries, object dropout, inconsistent extents) under weak user supervision like sparse point prompts, limiting reliability for video applications.

Method: Inference-time temporal probability smoothing that operates on per-frame segmentation probability maps, using optical-flow-based motion warping with pixel-wise uncertainty estimates from segmentation entropy and forward-backwards flow consistency to adaptively blend current predictions with motion-aligned historical estimates.

Result: Consistent improvements in temporal stability over vanilla SAM2 on four diverse video sequences using frame-wise and temporal stability metrics (motion-compensated IoU, boundary consistency, object persistence, area volatility) while preserving spatial accuracy.

Conclusion: The lightweight, model-agnostic framework effectively improves temporal coherence for interactive video segmentation without retraining or architectural changes, making it suitable for real-time applications.

Abstract: Interactive video segmentation models such as SAM2 have demonstrated strong generalization across diverse visual domains. However, under weak user supervision, for example, when sparse point prompts are provided on a single frame, their predictions often suffer from temporal instability, including flickering boundaries, object dropout, and inconsistent object extents across frames. These issues limit their reliability in downstream video understanding and control applications.
  In this paper, we propose an inference-time temporal probability smoothing method that improves the temporal stability of SAM2-based video segmentation without retraining or architectural modification. Our approach operates directly on per-frame segmentation probability maps and leverages optical-flow-based motion warping together with pixel-wise uncertainty estimates derived from segmentation entropy, and forward-backwards flow consistency. These signals are used to adaptively blend current-frame predictions with motion-aligned historical estimates, yielding temporally coherent segmentation outputs under weak prompts.
  We evaluate the proposed method on four diverse video sequences using a comprehensive set of frame-wise and temporal stability metrics, including motion-compensated IoU, boundary consistency, object persistence, and area volatility. Experimental results demonstrate consistent improvements in temporal stability over vanilla SAM2 inference while preserving spatial accuracy. The proposed framework is lightweight, model-agnostic, and well-suited for real-time, interactive video segmentation.

</details>


### [123] [Multimodal Fusion of Histopathology Images and Electronic Health Records for Early Breast Cancer Diagnosis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17122)
*Aditya Shribhagwan Khandelwal,Mohammad Samar Ansari,Asra Aslam*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Multimodal framework combining histopathology images and clinical EHR data outperforms unimodal approaches for breast cancer diagnosis, achieving near-perfect accuracy and improved performance on critical mitosis detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Breast cancer diagnosis requires timely and accurate assessment. While CNNs excel at histopathology image analysis and machine learning models work well with structured EHR data, existing approaches treat these modalities separately, missing potential benefits of multimodal integration.

Method: Systematic multimodal framework integrating patch-level histopathology features from BreCaHAD dataset with structured clinical data from MIMIC-IV. Evaluated unimodal image models (CNN baseline, ResNet-18 with transfer learning), unimodal tabular models (XGBoost, MLP), and intermediate-fusion model concatenating latent representations from both modalities.

Result: ResNet-18 achieved perfect accuracy (1.000) and AUC (1.000) on patch-level classification. XGBoost achieved 98% accuracy on EHR prediction. Intermediate fusion model achieved macro-average AUC of 0.997, outperforming all unimodal baselines, with largest improvements on class-imbalanced mitosis category (AUC 0.994). Grad-CAM and SHAP analyses validated clinical relevance.

Conclusion: Multimodal integration delivers meaningful improvements in both predictive performance and clinical transparency for breast cancer diagnosis, demonstrating the value of combining histopathology and clinical data for enhanced diagnostic accuracy and interpretability.

Abstract: Breast cancer is a leading cause of cancer-related mortality worldwide, and timely accurate diagnosis is critical to improving survival outcomes. While convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have demonstrated strong performance on histopathology image classification, and machine learning models on structured electronic health records (EHR) have shown utility for clinical risk stratification, most existing work treats these modalities in isolation. This paper presents a systematic multimodal framework that integrates patch-level histopathology features from the BreCaHAD dataset with structured clinical data from MIMIC-IV. We train and evaluate unimodal image models (a simple CNN baseline and ResNet-18 with transfer learning), unimodal tabular models (XGBoost and a multilayer perceptron), and an intermediate-fusion model that concatenates latent representations from both modalities. ResNet-18 achieves near-perfect accuracy (1.000) and AUC (1.000) on three-class patch-level classification, while XGBoost achieves 98% accuracy on the EHR prediction task. The intermediate fusion model yields a macro-average AUC of 0.997, outperforming all unimodal baselines and delivering the largest improvements on the diagnostically critical but class-imbalanced mitosis category (AUC 0.994). Grad-CAM and SHAP interpretability analyses validate that model decisions align with established pathological and clinical criteria. Our results demonstrate that multimodal integration delivers meaningful improvements in both predictive performance and clinical transparency.

</details>


### [124] [Prompt Sensitivity in Vision-Language Grounding: How Small Changes in Wording Affect Object Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17126)
*Dawar Jyoti Deka,Amit Sethi,Syed Mohammad Ali*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Vision-language models show inconsistent object grounding for semantically equivalent prompts, with structured variability not explained by text embedding distances alone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To examine the assumption that semantically equivalent descriptions yield consistent object grounding outputs in vision-language models, which is crucial for reliable open-vocabulary object detection.

Method: Used a controlled pipeline combining DETR for object proposals with CLIP for language-conditioned selection on 263 COCO val2017 images, testing overlapping prompts like "a person," "a human," and "a pedestrian." Analyzed variability using PCA and examined prompt ensembling and text embedding proximity.

Result: Found frequent selection of different instances across semantically equivalent prompts (mean instability of 2.11 distinct selections across six prompts). Variability is structured and directional (not random), prompt ensembling doesn't improve quality and often shifts selections toward generic regions, and text embedding proximity explains only 34% of grounding disagreement.

Conclusion: The instability in object grounding for semantically equivalent prompts arises from the argmax selection mechanism rather than text-level distances alone, challenging the assumption of consistent outputs for equivalent descriptions in vision-language models.

Abstract: Vision-language models enable open-vocabulary object grounding through natural language queries, under the implicit assumption that semantically equivalent descriptions yield consistent outputs. We examine this assumption using a controlled pipeline combining DETR for object proposals with CLIP for language-conditioned selection on 263 COCO val2017 images. We find that overlapping prompts such as "a person," "a human," and "a pedestrian" frequently select different instances, with mean instability of 2.11 distinct selections across six prompts. PCA analysis shows this variability is structured and directional, not random. Prompt ensembling does not improve quality and often shifts selections toward generic regions. We further show that text embedding proximity explains only 34% of grounding disagreement (r = -0.58), confirming that instability arises from the argmax selection mechanism rather than text-level distances alone.

</details>


### [125] [OptiMVMap: Offline Vectorized Map Construction via Optimal Multi-vehicle Perspectives](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17135)
*Zedong Dan,Zijie Wang,Wei Zhang,Xiangru Lin,Weiming Zhang,Xiao Tan,Jingdong Wang,Liang Lin,Guanbin Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: OptiMVMap improves multi-vehicle vectorized mapping by strategically selecting helper vehicles to reduce ego-centric uncertainty in occluded regions, achieving better accuracy with fewer views.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Single ego-vehicle trajectories suffer from viewpoint insufficiency and occlusions. While multi-vehicle approaches offer complementary views, naive fusion introduces computational costs, redundancy, and noise from pose errors and occlusion artifacts.

Method: Reformulates multi-vehicle mapping as select-then-fuse: 1) Optimal Vehicle Selection module identifies compact helper subset to reduce ego-centric uncertainty, 2) Cross-Vehicle Attention for pose-tolerant alignment, 3) Semantic-aware Noise Filter for artifact suppression before BEV-level fusion.

Result: Improves MapTRv2 by +10.5 mAP on nuScenes and +9.3 mAP on Argoverse2. Surpasses memory-augmented baselines MVMap and HRMapNet by +6.2 mAP and +3.8 mAP on nuScenes, achieving more complete maps with fewer views.

Conclusion: Uncertainty-guided selection of helper vehicles is essential for efficient and accurate multi-vehicle vectorized mapping, addressing computation, redundancy, and noise challenges in multi-view fusion.

Abstract: Offline vectorized maps constitute critical infrastructure for high-precision autonomous driving and mapping services. Existing approaches rely predominantly on single ego-vehicle trajectories, which fundamentally suffer from viewpoint insufficiency: while memory-based methods extend observation time by aggregating ego-trajectory frames, they lack the spatial diversity needed to reveal occluded regions. Incorporating views from surrounding vehicles offers complementary perspectives, yet naive fusion introduces three key challenges: computational cost from large candidate pools, redundancy from near-collinear viewpoints, and noise from pose errors and occlusion artifacts.
  We present OptiMVMap, which reformulates multi-vehicle mapping as a select-then-fuse problem to address these challenges systematically. An Optimal Vehicle Selection (OVS) module strategically identifies a compact subset of helpers that maximally reduce ego-centric uncertainty in occluded regions, addressing computation and redundancy challenges. Cross-Vehicle Attention (CVA) and Semantic-aware Noise Filter (SNF) then perform pose-tolerant alignment and artifact suppression before BEV-level fusion, addressing the noise challenge. This targeted pipeline yields more complete and topologically faithful maps with substantially fewer views than indiscriminate aggregation. On nuScenes and Argoverse2, OptiMVMap improves MapTRv2 by +10.5 mAP and +9.3 mAP, respectively, and surpasses memory-augmented baselines MVMap and HRMapNet by +6.2 mAP and +3.8 mAP on nuScenes. These results demonstrate that uncertainty-guided selection of helper vehicles is essential for efficient and accurate multi-vehicle vectorized mapping. The code is released at https://github.com/DanZeDong/OptiMVMap.

</details>


### [126] [Instant Colorization of Gaussian Splats](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17155)
*Daniel Lieber,Alexander Mock,Nils Wandel*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Efficient method for mapping 2D image information back onto 3D Gaussian splats using visibility-weighted least squares, enabling scene relighting, stylization, and semantic segmentation with significant speed improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While current Gaussian Splatting frameworks efficiently map 3D scenes to 2D views, there's a need for the opposite direction - mapping 2D image information (color, features, segmentation masks) back onto 3D Gaussian splats to enable applications like scene relighting, stylization, and 3D semantic segmentation.

Method: Uses normal equation to solve a visibility-weighted least squares problem for every Gaussian, implemented efficiently with existing differentiable rasterizers to handle challenges like view-dependent colorization and occlusion.

Result: Demonstrates effectiveness on scene relighting, feature enrichment, and 3D semantic segmentation tasks, achieving up to an order of magnitude speedup compared to gradient descent-based baselines.

Conclusion: Provides an efficient solution for the inverse mapping problem in Gaussian Splatting, enabling practical applications that require transferring 2D image information back to 3D scene representations with significant performance improvements.

Abstract: Gaussian Splatting has recently become one of the most popular frameworks for photorealistic 3D scene reconstruction and rendering. While current rasterizers allow for efficient mappings of 3D Gaussian splats onto 2D camera views, this work focuses on mapping 2D image information (e.g. color, neural features or segmentation masks) efficiently back onto an existing scene of Gaussian splats. This 'opposite' direction enables applications ranging from scene relighting and stylization to 3D semantic segmentation, but also introduces challenges, such as view-dependent colorization and occlusion handling.
  Our approach tackles these challenges using the normal equation to solve a visibility-weighted least squares problem for every Gaussian and can be implemented efficiently with existing differentiable rasterizers. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach on scene relighting, feature enrichment and 3D semantic segmentation tasks, achieving up to an order of magnitude speedup compared to gradient descent-based baselines.

</details>


### [127] [PPEDCRF: Dynamic-CRF-Guided Selective Perturbation for Background-Based Location Privacy in Video Sequences](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17163)
*Bo Ma,Weiqi Yan,Jinsong Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PPEDCRF is a calibrated selective perturbation framework that protects background-based location privacy in video frames by injecting Gaussian noise only into location-sensitive background regions identified via dynamic CRF, achieving better visual quality than global noise at equal privacy protection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Even after removing GPS metadata, adversaries can geolocate video frames by matching background visual cues to geo-tagged reference imagery. This creates a privacy threat where location can be inferred from background content alone.

Method: Uses dynamic conditional random field (DCRF) to estimate location-sensitive background regions, rescales perturbation strength with normalized control penalty (NCP), and injects Gaussian noise only inside inferred regions via DP-style calibration rule.

Result: Reduces ResNet18 Top-1 retrieval accuracy from 0.667 to 0.361±0.127 at σ₀=8 while preserving 36.14dB PSNR - a ≈6dB quality advantage over global Gaussian noise. Shows broad transfer effectiveness across 8 attacker backbones.

Conclusion: PPEDCRF provides spatially concentrated perturbation that preserves higher visual quality at any given noise scale compared to global noise, though matched-operating-point analysis shows similar privacy at equal utility levels.

Abstract: We propose PPEDCRF, a calibrated selective perturbation framework that protects \emph{background-based location privacy} in released video frames against gallery-based retrieval attackers. Even after GPS metadata are stripped, an adversary can geolocate a frame by matching its background visual cues to geo-tagged reference imagery; PPEDCRF mitigates this threat by estimating location-sensitive background regions with a dynamic conditional random field (DCRF), rescaling perturbation strength with a normalized control penalty (NCP), and injecting Gaussian noise only inside the inferred regions via a DP-style calibration rule.
  On a controlled paired-scene retrieval benchmark with eight attacker backbones and three noise seeds, PPEDCRF reduces ResNet18 Top-1 retrieval accuracy from 0.667 to $0.361\pm0.127$ at $σ_0=8$ while preserving $36.14\,$dB PSNR -- an ${\approx}6\,$dB quality advantage over global Gaussian noise. Transfer across the eight-backbone seed-averaged benchmark is broadly supportive (23 of 24 backbone-gallery cells show negative $Δ$), while appendix-scale confirmation identifies MixVPR as a remaining adverse-transfer exception. Matched-operating-point analysis shows that PPEDCRF and global Gaussian noise converge in Top-1 privacy at equal utility, so the practical benefit is spatially concentrated perturbation that preserves higher visual quality at any given noise scale rather than stronger matched-utility privacy. Code: https://github.com/mabo1215/PPEDCRF

</details>


### [128] [LookasideVLN: Direction-Aware Aerial Vision-and-Language Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17190)
*Yuwei Ning,Ganlong Zhao,Yipeng Qin,Si Liu,Yang Liu,Liang Lin,Guanbin Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LookasideVLN improves Aerial VLN by leveraging directional cues in instructions for better spatial reasoning and efficiency, using an Egocentric Lookaside Graph, Spatial Landmark Knowledge Base, and Lookaside MLLM Navigation Agent.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Aerial VLN methods have shallow instruction understanding and high computational cost, primarily relying on landmark descriptions while overlooking directional cues which are crucial for spatial context in human navigation.

Method: Three core components: (1) Egocentric Lookaside Graph (ELG) dynamically encodes instruction-relevant landmarks and directional relationships, (2) Spatial Landmark Knowledge Base (SLKB) provides lightweight memory retrieval from prior navigation experiences, and (3) Lookaside MLLM Navigation Agent aligns multimodal information from instructions, visual observations, and ELG data for path planning.

Result: Extensive experiments show LookasideVLN significantly outperforms state-of-the-art CityNavAgent, even with single-level lookahead, demonstrating directional cues are powerful yet efficient for Aerial VLN.

Conclusion: Leveraging directional cues in natural language instructions enables more accurate spatial reasoning and greater computational efficiency in Aerial VLN, making LookasideVLN a promising new paradigm for UAV navigation in complex urban environments.

Abstract: Aerial Vision-and-Language Navigation (Aerial VLN) enables unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) to follow natural language instructions and navigate complex urban environments. While recent advances have achieved progress through large-scale memory graphs and lookahead path planning, they remain limited by shallow instruction understanding and high computational cost. In particular, existing methods rely primarily on landmark descriptions, overlooking directional cues "a key source of spatial context in human navigation". In this work, we propose LookasideVLN, a new paradigm that exploits directional cues in natural language to achieve both more accurate spatial reasoning and greater computational efficiency. LookasideVLN comprises three core components: (1) an Egocentric Lookaside Graph (ELG) that dynamically encodes instruction-relevant landmarks and their directional relationships, (2) a Spatial Landmark Knowledge Base (SLKB) that provides lightweight memory retrieval from prior navigation experiences, and (3) a Lookaside MLLM Navigation Agent that aligns multimodal information from user instructions, visual observations, and landmark-direction information from ELG for path planning. Extensive experiments show that LookasideVLN significantly outperforms the state-of-the-art CityNavAgent, even with a single-level lookahead, demonstrating that leveraging directional cues is a powerful yet efficient strategy for Aerial VLN.

</details>


### [129] [DreamShot: Personalized Storyboard Synthesis with Video Diffusion Prior](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17195)
*Junjia Huang,Binbin Yang,Pengxiang Yan,Jiyang Liu,Bin Xia,Zhao Wang,Yitong Wang,Liang Lin,Guanbin Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DreamShot is a video diffusion-based storyboard synthesis framework that generates coherent multi-shot sequences with consistent characters and narrative flow, outperforming text-to-image adaptation methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing text-to-image diffusion models adapted for storyboard synthesis struggle with long-range temporal coherence, consistent character identities, and narrative flow across multiple shots.

Method: DreamShot leverages video diffusion priors for controllable multi-shot synthesis, supporting Text-to-Shot and Reference-to-Shot generation with story continuation. It includes a multi-reference role conditioning module with Role-Attention Consistency Loss for identity alignment.

Result: Extensive experiments show DreamShot achieves superior scene coherence, role consistency, and generation efficiency compared to state-of-the-art text-to-image storyboard models.

Conclusion: DreamShot establishes a new direction toward controllable video model-driven visual storytelling by fully exploiting video diffusion priors for coherent storyboard synthesis.

Abstract: Storyboard synthesis plays a crucial role in visual storytelling, aiming to generate coherent shot sequences that visually narrate cinematic events with consistent characters, scenes, and transitions. However, existing approaches are mostly adapted from text-to-image diffusion models, which struggle to maintain long-range temporal coherence, consistent character identities, and narrative flow across multiple shots. In this paper, we introduce DreamShot, a video generative model based storyboard framework that fully exploits powerful video diffusion priors for controllable multi-shot synthesis. DreamShot supports both Text-to-Shot and Reference-to-Shot generation, as well as story continuation conditioned on previous frames, enabling flexible and context-aware storyboard generation. By leveraging the spatial-temporal consistency inherent in video generative models, DreamShot produces visually and semantically coherent sequences with improved narrative fidelity and character continuity. Furthermore, DreamShot incorporates a multi-reference role conditioning module that accepts multiple character reference images and enforces identity alignment via a Role-Attention Consistency Loss, explicitly constraining attention between reference and generated roles. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DreamShot achieves superior scene coherence, role consistency, and generation efficiency compared to state-of-the-art text-to-image storyboard models, establishing a new direction toward controllable video model-driven visual storytelling.

</details>


### [130] [SciDraw-6K: A Multilingual Scientific Illustration Dataset Generated by Google Gemini](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17206)
*Davie Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SciDraw-6K is a multilingual dataset of 6,291 AI-generated scientific illustrations across 8 categories, designed for scientific visualization research and applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a purpose-built dataset for scientific illustration (schematic diagrams, mechanism figures, etc.) that addresses the limitations of general-purpose text-to-image corpora and supports multilingual scientific visualization research.

Method: Curated dataset construction using Google Gemini image-generation models (primarily gemini-2.5-flash-image and gemini-3-pro-image-preview families), with prompts in 11 languages across 8 scientific categories, resulting in 6,291 image-prompt pairs.

Result: Created SciDraw-6K dataset with images spanning biomedical, chemistry, materials, electronics, environment, AI systems, physics, and other categories, deployed as the substrate for sci-draw.com public drawing service.

Conclusion: The dataset supports multilingual text-to-image research, domain-adapted diffusion fine-tuning, and prompt-engineering studies for scientific visualization, addressing a gap in specialized scientific illustration resources.

Abstract: We present SciDraw-6K, a curated dataset of 6,291 scientific illustrations synthesized by Google Gemini image-generation models, each paired with prompts in eleven languages (English, Simplified Chinese, Traditional Chinese, Japanese, Korean, German, French, Spanish, Brazilian Portuguese, Italian, and Russian). Images span eight broad scientific categories -- biomedical, chemistry, materials, electronics, environment, AI systems, physics, and a long "other" tail -- and are produced primarily by the gemini-2.5-flash-image and gemini-3-pro-image-preview model families. In contrast to general-purpose text-to-image corpora that dominate the literature, SciDraw-6K is purpose-built for the scientific illustration genre: schematic diagrams, mechanism figures, table-of-contents graphics, and conceptual posters. We describe the construction pipeline, report dataset statistics, and document its use as the substrate of sci-draw.com, a public scientific drawing service. The dataset is released to support multilingual text-to-image research, domain-adapted diffusion fine-tuning, and prompt-engineering studies for scientific visualization. Dataset: https://huggingface.co/datasets/SciDrawAI/SciDraw-6K Code: https://github.com/SciDrawAI/scidraw-6k

</details>


### [131] [CDSA-Net:Collaborative Decoupling of Vascular Structure and Background for High-Fidelity Coronary Digital Subtraction Angiography](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17208)
*Si Li,Chen-Kai Hu,Zhenhuan Lyu,Yuanqing He*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CDSA-Net is a novel deep learning framework for coronary digital subtraction angiography that decouples vascular structure preservation from realistic background restoration to eliminate boundary artifacts while maintaining diagnostic fidelity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing DSA methods suffer from persistent boundary artifacts and loss of native tissue grayscale fidelity, undermining diagnostic confidence in coronary imaging. Physiological motion forces reliance on raw angiograms cluttered with anatomical noise.

Method: CDSA-Net introduces two core innovations: 1) Hierarchical geometric prior guidance (HGPG) mechanism in CSENet combining integrated geometric prior, gated spatial modulation, and centerline-aware topology loss for structural continuity; 2) Adaptive noise module (ANM) in CBResNet that models clinical X-ray noise to enable seamless background restoration and eliminate boundary artifacts.

Result: Significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods in vascular intensity correlation and perceptual quality. Achieves 25.6% improvement in morphology assessment efficiency and 42.9% gain in hemodynamic evaluation speed while maintaining diagnostic consistency with raw angiograms.

Conclusion: CDSA-Net sets a new benchmark for utility in interventional cardiology by explicitly addressing the dual challenges of vascular structure preservation and realistic background restoration, enabling artifact-free coronary DSA with maintained diagnostic fidelity.

Abstract: Digital subtraction angiography (DSA) in coronary imaging is fundamentally challenged by physiological motion, forcing reliance on raw angiograms cluttered with anatomical noise. Existing deep learning methods often produced images with two critical clinically unacceptable flaws: persistent boundary artifacts and a loss of native tissue grayscale fidelity that undermined diagnostic confidence. We propose a novel framework termed as CDSA-Net that for the first time explicitly decouples and jointly optimizes vascular structure preservation and realistic background restoration. CDSA-Net introduces two core innovations: (i) A hierarchical geometric prior guidance (HGPG) mechanism, embedded in our coronary structure extraction network (CSENet). It synergistically combines integrated geometric prior (IGP) with gated spatial modulation (GSM) and centerline-aware topology (CAT) loss supervision, ensuring structural continuity. (ii) An adaptive noise module (ANM) within our coronary background restoration network (CBResNet). Unlike standard restoration, ANM uniquely models the stochastic nature of clinical X-ray noise, bridging the domain gap to enable seamless background intensity estimation and the complete elimination of boundary artifacts. The final subtraction is obtained by removing the restored background from the raw angiogram. Quantitatively, it significantly outperformed state-of-the-art methods in vascular intensity correlation and perceptual quality. A 25.6% improvement in morphology assessment efficiency and a 42.9% gain in hemodynamic evaluation speed set a new benchmark for utility in interventional cardiology, while maintaining diagnostic results consistent with raw angiograms. The project code is available at https://github.com/DrThink-ai/CDSA-Net.

</details>


### [132] [DREAM: Dynamic Retinal Enhancement with Adaptive Multi-modal Fusion for Expert Precision Medical Report Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17209)
*Nagur Shareef Shaik,Teja Krishna Cherukuri,Dong Hye Ye*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DREAM introduces a two-stage fusion framework for retinal image report generation that integrates visual data with clinical keywords using adaptive multi-modal fusion and contrastive alignment, achieving SOTA results on medical benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LVLMs struggle in specialized medical fields due to data scarcity, leading to overfitting and missed subtle pathologies in retinal image report generation.

Method: DREAM uses a two-stage fusion mechanism: 1) Abstractor maps image and keyword features into shared space, 2) Adaptor performs adaptive multi-modal fusion with learnable weights, plus Contrastive Alignment to ground outputs in clinical reality.

Result: Achieves new SOTA on DeepEyeNet benchmark with BLEU-4 score of 0.241 and demonstrates strong generalization to ROCO dataset.

Conclusion: DREAM successfully addresses data scarcity in medical LVLMs by combining medical expertise with efficient fusion strategies, enabling high-fidelity report generation for retinal images.

Abstract: Automating medical reports for retinal images requires a sophisticated blend of visual pattern recognition and deep clinical knowledge. Current Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) often struggle in specialized medical fields where data is scarce, leading to models that overfit and miss subtle but critical pathologies. To address this, we introduce DREAM (Dynamic Retinal Enhancement with Adaptive Multi-modal Fusion), a novel framework for high-fidelity medical report generation that excels even with limited data. DREAM employs a unique two-stage fusion mechanism that intelligently integrates visual data with clinical keywords curated by ophthalmologists. First, the Abstractor module maps image and keyword features into a shared space, enhancing visual data with pathology-relevant insights. Next, the Adaptor performs adaptive multi-modal fusion, dynamically weighting the importance of each modality using learnable parameters to create a unified representation. To ensure the model's outputs are semantically grounded in clinical reality, a Contrastive Alignment module aligns these fused representations with ground-truth medical reports during training. By combining medical expertise with an efficient fusion strategy, DREAM sets a new state-of-the-art on the DeepEyeNet benchmark, achieving a BLEU-4 score of 0.241, and further demonstrates strong generalization to the ROCO dataset.

</details>


### [133] [EmbodiedHead: Real-Time Listening and Speaking Avatar for Conversational Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17211)
*Yu Zhang,Kaiyuan Shen,Yang Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EmbodiedHead is a real-time talking-head framework that gives LLMs visual avatars using a rectified-flow diffusion transformer and differentiable renderer for high-quality generation in just 4 steps, with single-stream audio processing for natural turn-taking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current talking-head avatars struggle to simultaneously achieve real-time generation, unified listening-speaking behavior, and high visual quality. Existing methods use dual-stream audio that creates look-ahead dependencies incompatible with causal user-LLM interaction.

Method: Uses Rectified-Flow Diffusion Transformer (DiT) with differentiable renderer for 4-step generation. Single-stream audio interface with explicit per-frame listening-speaking state conditioning and Streaming Audio Scheduler. Two-stage training: coefficient-space pretraining followed by joint image-domain refinement.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art visual quality and motion fidelity in both speaking and listening scenarios. Enables real-time generation with seamless turn-taking while suppressing spurious mouth motion during listening.

Conclusion: EmbodiedHead successfully addresses the key challenges of real-time talking-head avatars for LLMs, providing a practical solution that balances generation speed, visual quality, and natural conversational behavior.

Abstract: We present EmbodiedHead, a speech-driven talking-head framework that equips LLMs with real-time visual avatars for conversation. A practical embodied avatar must achieve real-time generation, unified listening-speaking behavior, and high rendered visual quality simultaneously. Our framework couples the first Rectified-Flow Diffusion Transformer (DiT) for this task with a differentiable renderer, enabling diverse, high-fidelity generation in as few as four sampling steps. Prior listening-speaking methods rely on dual-stream audio, introducing an interlocutor look-ahead dependency incompatible with causal user--LLM interaction. We instead adopt a single-stream interface with explicit per-frame listening-speaking state conditioning and a Streaming Audio Scheduler, suppressing spurious mouth motion during listening while enabling seamless turn-taking. A two-stage training scheme of coefficient-space pretraining and joint image-domain refinement further closes the gap between motion-level supervision and rendered quality. Extensive experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art visual quality and motion fidelity in both speaking and listening scenarios.

</details>


### [134] [Cross-Modal Attention Analysis and Optimization in Vision-Language Models: A Study on Visual Reliability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17217)
*Lijie Zhou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Vision-Language Models over-rely on text shortcuts; adversarial evaluation shows optimized LoRA reduces accuracy drop from 27.5% to 9.8% while maintaining 97% normal accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLMs show "text shortcut learning" - they over-rely on textual descriptions while under-utilizing visual evidence, which undermines their cross-modal understanding capabilities.

Method: Adversarial evaluation framework with four strategies (shape/color/position swap, random text) on geometric shapes dataset. Compare Baseline CLIP, LoRA fine-tuning, and optimized LoRA with Hard Negative Mining, Label Smoothing, layer-wise learning rates, Cosine Restarts, curriculum learning, and data augmentation.

Result: Optimized model reduces average accuracy degradation (Drop) from 27.5% to 9.8% (64.4% relative improvement, p<0.001) while maintaining 97% normal accuracy. Attention visualization and embedding analysis confirm better visual attention and cross-modal alignment.

Conclusion: The proposed optimization techniques significantly mitigate text shortcut learning in VLMs, improving visual feature utilization while maintaining strong overall performance.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) achieve strong cross-modal performance, yet recent evidence suggests they over-rely on textual descriptions while under-utilizing visual evidence -- a phenomenon termed ``text shortcut learning.'' We propose an adversarial evaluation framework that quantifies this cross-modal dependency by measuring accuracy degradation (Drop) when semantically conflicting text is paired with unchanged images. Four adversarial strategies -- shape\_swap, color\_swap, position\_swap, and random\_text -- are applied to a controlled geometric-shapes dataset ($n{=}1{,}000$). We compare three configurations: Baseline CLIP (ViT-B/32), LoRA fine-tuning, and LoRA Optimized (integrating Hard Negative Mining, Label Smoothing, layer-wise learning rates, Cosine Restarts, curriculum learning, and data augmentation). The optimized model reduces average Drop from 27.5\% to 9.8\% (64.4\% relative improvement, $p{<}0.001$) while maintaining 97\% normal accuracy. Attention visualization and embedding-space analysis confirm that the optimized model attends more to visual features and achieves tighter cross-modal alignment.

</details>


### [135] [Region-Affinity Attention for Whole-Slide Breast Cancer Classification in Deep Ultraviolet Imaging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17222)
*Nagur Shareef Shaik,Teja Krishna Cherukuri,Dong Hye Ye*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Novel Region-Affinity Attention mechanism for DUV-WSI breast cancer classification achieves 92.67% accuracy without patching, preserving spatial context.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional histopathology is slow for intra-operative use; existing patch-based deep learning methods fragment spatial context and standard attention mechanisms fail to exploit multi-scale regional relationships in DUV-WSI data.

Method: Region-Affinity Attention mechanism processes entire DUV-WSI slides without patching, models local neighbor distances with full affinity matrix to highlight diagnostically relevant regions, and uses contrastive loss for feature discriminability.

Result: Achieved 92.67% ± 0.73% accuracy and 95.97% AUC on 136 DUV-WSI samples, outperforming existing attention methods like Spatial, Squeeze-and-Excitation, Global Context and Guided Context Gating.

Conclusion: Region-Affinity Attention provides superior breast cancer classification from DUV-WSI by preserving spatial integrity and exploiting multi-scale regional relationships, offering clinical utility for rapid intra-operative diagnosis.

Abstract: Breast cancer diagnosis demands rapid and precise tools, yet traditional histopathological methods often fall short in intra-operative settings. Deep Ultraviolet (DUV) fluorescence imaging emerges as a transformative approach, offering high-contrast, label-free visualization of whole-slide images (WSIs) with unprecedented detail, surpassing conventional hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining in speed and resolution. However, existing deep learning methods for breast cancer classification, predominantly patch-based, fragment spatial context and incur significant preprocessing overhead, limiting their clinical utility. Moreover, standard attention mechanisms, such as Spatial, Squeeze-and-Excitation, Global Context and Guided Context Gating, fail to fully exploit the rich, multi-scale regional relationships inherent in DUV-WSI data, often prioritizing generic feature recalibration over diagnostic specificity. This study introduces a novel Region-Affinity Attention mechanism tailored for DUV-WSI breast cancer classification, processing entire slides without patching to preserve spatial integrity. By modeling local neighbor distances and constructing a full affinity matrix, our method dynamically highlights diagnostically relevant regions, augmented by a contrastive loss to enhance feature discriminability. Evaluated on a dataset of 136 DUV-WSI samples, our approach achieves an accuracy of 92.67 +/- 0.73% and an AUC of 95.97%, outperforming existing attention methods.

</details>


### [136] [Enhancing Zero-shot Personalized Image Aesthetics Assessment with Profile-aware Multimodal LLM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17233)
*Chun Wang,Chenfeng Wei,Chenyang Liu,Weihong Deng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: P-MLLM: A profile-aware multimodal LLM for zero-shot personalized image aesthetics assessment using user profiles as contextual signals instead of historical ratings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing PIAA methods rely on historical user ratings and struggle when such data is unavailable (zero-shot setting). Need to model user-specific aesthetic preferences without requiring prior rating data.

Method: Introduce P-MLLM, a profile-aware multimodal LLM that augments a frozen LLM with selective fusion modules. These modules selectively integrate visual information into hidden states during profile-conditioned reasoning, enabling profile-aware visual integration.

Result: Achieves competitive zero-shot performance on recent PIAA benchmarks. Remains effective even with coarse profile information, demonstrating robustness.

Conclusion: Profile-based personalization shows strong potential for zero-shot PIAA, offering an effective alternative to historical rating-based approaches.

Abstract: Personalized image aesthetics assessment (PIAA) aims to predict an individual user's subjective rating of an image, which requires modeling user-specific aesthetic preferences. Existing methods rely on historical user ratings for this modeling and therefore struggle when such data are unavailable. We address this zero-shot setting by using user profiles as contextual signals for personalization and adopting a profile-based personalization paradigm. We introduce P-MLLM, a profile-aware multimodal LLM that augments a frozen LLM with selective fusion modules for controlled visual integration. These modules selectively integrate visual information into the model's evolving hidden states during profile-conditioned reasoning, allowing visual information to be incorporated in a profile-aware manner.
