Experiments on recent PIAA benchmarks show that P-MLLM achieves competitive zero-shot performance and remains effective even with coarse profile information, highlighting the potential of profile-based personalization for zero-shot PIAA.

</details>


### [137] [RemoteShield: Enable Robust Multimodal Large Language Models for Earth Observation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17243)
*Rui Min,Liang Yao,Shiyu Miao,Shengxiang Xu,Yuxuan Liu,Chuanyi Zhang,Shimin Di,Fan Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RemoteShield: A robust Remote Sensing MLLM trained via preference learning to maintain consistent outputs across realistic multimodal perturbations like clouds, fog, and textual variations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current Remote Sensing MLLMs fail to maintain consistent interpretation under realistic input variations, as they're trained on clean datasets and don't generalize to noisy operational conditions like cloud cover and colloquial language.

Method: RemoteShield uses preference learning where clean samples are paired with perturbed variants to form semantic equivalence clusters. The model is optimized to favor stable responses by comparing clean vs. corrupted inputs, focusing on underlying task semantics despite visual/textual noise.

Result: RemoteShield consistently delivers stronger robustness and cross-condition consistency than representative baselines on three Earth Observation tasks under realistic multimodal perturbations.

Conclusion: The proposed RemoteShield framework successfully addresses the vulnerability of current RS MLLMs to realistic input variations through cross-condition alignment and preference learning, enabling more reliable Earth Observation applications.

Abstract: A robust Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) for Earth Observation should maintain consistent interpretation and reasoning under realistic input variations. However, current Remote Sensing MLLMs fail to meet this requirement. Trained on carefully curated clean datasets, they learn brittle mappings that do not generalize to noisy conditions in operational Earth Observation. Consequently, their performance degrades when confronted with imperfect inputs in deployment. To quantify this vulnerability, we construct a realistic set of multimodal perturbations, including visual degradations such as cloud and fog cover, together with diverse human-centric textual variations ranging from colloquialisms to vague or omitted instructions. Empirical evaluations show that these perturbations significantly impair the visual-semantic reasoning capabilities of leading RS foundation models. To address this limitation, we introduce RemoteShield, a robust Remote Sensing MLLM trained to maintain consistent outputs across realistic input variations. During training, each clean sample is paired with its image-text perturbed variants to form a semantic equivalence cluster. Rather than directly fitting noisy samples, RemoteShield is optimized through preference learning over clean and perturbed conditions within the same cluster. By comparing model responses to clean and corrupted inputs, the model is encouraged to favor stable responses over perturbation-induced failures. This cross-condition alignment helps the model focus on underlying task semantics despite visual degradations and textual noise. Experiments on three Earth Observation tasks show that RemoteShield consistently delivers stronger robustness and cross-condition consistency than representative baselines under realistic multimodal perturbations.

</details>


### [138] [Fractal Characterization of Low-Correlation Signals in AI-Generated Image Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17268)
*Wenwei Xie,Jie Yin,Lu Ma,Xuansong Zhang,Wenjing Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes a fractal theory-based method for detecting AI-generated images by analyzing low-correlation signals as distinctive markers, showing superior robustness in open-world scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI-generated imagery poses significant threats to information security and societal trust, and existing deepfake detection methods lack robustness in open-world scenarios.

Method: Analyzes intrinsic signal-level discrepancies between synthetic and authentic images, identifies low-correlation signals as distinctive markers, and introduces a fractal theory-based method to quantify these signals and capture statistical anomalies from the synthesis process.

Result: Extensive experiments demonstrate the method's robustness and superior detection performance, with the approach being applicable to all AI-generated image detection tasks, not just face images.

Conclusion: The work emphasizes shifting research focus to signal-level analysis for deepfake detection, provides a new research direction, and offers a theoretically generalizable approach for detecting AI-generated imagery.

Abstract: AI-generated imagery has reached near-photorealistic fidelity, yet this technology poses significant threats to information security and societal trust. Existing deepfake detection methods often exhibit limited robustness in open-world scenarios. To address this limitation, this paper investigates intrinsic discrepancies between synthetic and authentic images from a signal-level perspective. Our analysis reveals that low-correlation signals serve as distinctive markers for differentiating AI-generated imagery from real photographs. Building on this insight, we introduce a novel method for quantifying these signals based on fractal theory. By analyzing the fractal characteristics of low-correlation signals, our method effectively captures the subtle statistical anomalies inherent to the synthesis process. Extensive experimental results demonstrate the method's robustness and superior detection performance. This work emphasizes the need to shift research focus to a new signal-level direction for deepfake detection. Theoretically, this proposed approach is not limited to face image identification but can be applied to all AI-generated image detection tasks. This study provides a new research direction for deepfake detection.

</details>


### [139] [Instinct vs. Reflection: Unifying Token and Verbalized Confidence in Multimodal Large Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17274)
*Yunkai Dang,Yifan Jiang,Yizhu Jiang,Anqi Chen,Wenbin Li,Yang Gao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper addresses confidence estimation in Multimodal Large Language Models, revealing an instinct-reflection misalignment between token-level support and verbal self-assessment, and proposes a monotone confidence fusion framework to improve reliability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While MLLMs show strong performance, practical deployment requires robust confidence estimation. Prior work focuses on text-only LLMs using expensive self-consistency sampling, leaving a gap for multimodal settings where model's implicit token-level support often diverges from verbal self-assessment confidence.

Method: Proposes a monotone confidence fusion framework to merge dual-channel signals (instinct and reflection) and cross-channel consistency to estimate correctness, followed by order-preserving mean alignment to correct global bias while preserving risk-coverage trade-off for selective prediction.

Result: Experiments on diverse open-source and closed-source MLLMs show the method consistently yields more reliable confidence estimates and improves both calibration and failure prediction across various models.

Conclusion: The proposed approach effectively addresses the instinct-reflection misalignment in MLLMs, providing more reliable confidence estimation that enhances practical deployment reliability while maintaining selective prediction capabilities.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated exceptional capabilities in various perception and reasoning tasks. Despite this success, ensuring their reliability in practical deployment necessitates robust confidence estimation. Prior works have predominantly focused on text-only LLMs, often relying on computationally expensive self-consistency sampling. In this paper, we extend this to multimodal settings and conduct a comprehensive evaluation of MLLMs' response confidence estimation. Our analysis reveals a significant instinct-reflection misalignment: the model's implicit token-level support frequently diverges from its verbal self-assessment confidence. To address this misalignment, we propose a monotone confidence fusion framework to merge dual-channel signals and cross-channel consistency to estimate correctness. Subsequently, an order-preserving mean alignment step is applied to correct global bias, which improves calibration while preserving the risk-coverage trade-off for selective prediction. Experiments on diverse open-source and closed-source MLLMs show that our method consistently yields more reliable confidence estimates and improves both calibration and failure prediction. Code will be available at https://github.com/Yunkaidang/Instinct-vs.-Reflection.

</details>


### [140] [PestVL-Net: Enabling Multimodal Pest Learning via Fine-grained Vision-Language Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17278)
*Xueheng Li,Tao Hu,Ke Cao,Runsheng Qi,Huixin Zhang,Rui Li,Jie Zhang,Chengjun Xie*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PestVL-Net: A vision-language framework using RWKV architecture and MLLM priors for fine-grained pest recognition, addressing challenges in real-world agricultural pest data collection and diverse morphological characteristics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Collecting pest data in real agricultural scenarios is challenging, pests have wide species variety with complex morphological characteristics, and existing techniques struggle to model fine-grained visual and semantic features effectively, hindering practical applications.

Method: PestVL-Net integrates visual and linguistic pathways: visual pathway uses RWKV architecture with saliency-guided adaptive window partitioning for fine-grained pest characteristics; linguistic component generates precise semantic descriptions using MLLM priors informed by agricultural expert knowledge and structured via multimodal Chain-of-Thought reasoning.

Result: Extensive experimental evaluations on multiple pest datasets validate PestVL-Net's superior performance, demonstrating its potential for effective real-world pest management.

Conclusion: The synergistic integration of PestVL-Net with multi-species pest datasets enables fine-grained multimodal pest learning, addressing critical challenges in agricultural pest recognition and management.

Abstract: Effective pest recognition and management are crucial for sustainable agricultural development. However, collecting pest data in real scenarios is often challenging. Compared to other domains, pests exhibit a wide variety of species with complex and diverse morphological characteristics. Existing techniques struggle to effectively model the key visual and high-level semantic features of pests in a fine-grained manner. These limitations hinder the practical application of such methods in real agricultural scenarios. To address these critical challenges, we present a synergistic approach that integrates PestVL-Net, a novel vision-language framework, with two multi-species pest datasets to facilitate fine-grained pest learning. The visual pathway of PestVL-Net utilizes the Recurrent Weighted Key Value (RWKV) architecture, incorporating a saliency-guided adaptive window partitioning scheme to effectively model the fine-grained visual characteristics of pests. Concurrently, the linguistic component generates precise pest semantic descriptions by leveraging Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) priors, critically informed by agricultural expert knowledge and structured via multimodal Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning. The deep fusion of these complementary visual and textual representations enables fine-grained multimodal pest learning. Extensive experimental evaluations on multiple pest datasets validate the superior performance of PestVL-Net, highlighting its potential for effective real-world pest management.

</details>


### [141] [Depth Adaptive Efficient Visual Autoregressive Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17286)
*Chunliang Li,Tianze Cao,Sanyuan Zhao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DepthVAR adaptively allocates computational depth per token instead of pruning tokens, achieving 2.3-3.1× acceleration with minimal quality loss for visual autoregressive models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VAR models inefficiently apply fixed computational depth to all positions, and existing token pruning methods using frequency maps are fundamentally limited and don't improve quality even with better frequency estimation.

Method: DepthVAR introduces adaptive depth scheduling with cyclic rotated schedule for balanced refinement, plus dynamic inference that translates depths into layer-major masks, selectively applies transformer blocks, and blends resulting codes proportionally to processing depth.

Result: Achieves 2.3×-3.1× acceleration with minimal quality loss, offering better compute-performance trade-off than hard-pruning approaches.

Conclusion: DepthVAR's paradigm shift from token pruning to adaptive per-token computational depth allocation provides efficient acceleration for VAR models while maintaining quality.

Abstract: Visual Autoregressive (VAR) modeling inefficiently applies a fixed computational depth to each position when generating high-resolution images. While existing methods accelerate inference by pruning tokens using frequency maps, their binary hard-pruning approach is fundamentally limited and fails to improve quality even with better frequency estimation. Observing that VAR models possess significant depth redundancy, we propose a paradigm shift from pruning entire tokens to adaptively allocating per-token computational depth. To this end, we introduce DepthVAR, a training-free framework that dynamically allocates computation. It integrates an adaptive depth scheduler, which assigns computational depth via a cyclic rotated schedule for balanced, non-static refinement, with a dynamic inference process that translates these depths into layer-major masks, selectively applies transformer blocks, and blends the resulting codes to ensure each token's influence is proportional to its processing depth. Extensive experiments show that DepthVAR achieves 2.3$\times$-3.1$\times$ acceleration with minimal quality loss, offering a competitive compute-performance trade-off compared to existing hard-pruning approaches. Code is available at https://github.com/STOVAGtz/DepthVAR

</details>


### [142] [Spectral Forensics of Diffusion Attention Graphs for Copy-Move Forgery Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17287)
*H. M. Shadman Tabib,Tasriad Ahmed Tias,Nafis Tahmid*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Training-free framework detects copy-move forgery by analyzing spectral structure of attention graphs from Stable Diffusion U-Net, achieving AUROCs of 0.606-0.774 across four benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Copy-move forgery (duplicating image regions to hide/fabricate content) threatens visual media integrity, requiring effective detection methods.

Method: GraphSpecForge analyzes spectral structure of self-attention graphs from pretrained Stable Diffusion U-Net. Copy-move manipulation induces approximate subgraph duplication, leading to measurable spectral redistribution in normalized graph Laplacian. Uses Wasserstein distances between per-image Laplacian spectra and authentic reference distribution for detection.

Result: Achieved AUROC = 0.606 on RecodAI-LUC (5,128 images), with normalized Laplacian outperforming raw attention spectra by +0.057 AUROC. On MICC-F220, CoMoFoD, and COVERAGE, attained AUROCs of 0.752, 0.774, and 0.673 respectively. On CoMoFoD: AUPRC = 0.833, balanced accuracy = 0.712, MCC = 0.499, TPR@1%FPR = 32.5%.

Conclusion: GraphSpecForge provides effective training-free detection of copy-move forgery by leveraging spectral analysis of attention graphs, with ablation experiments confirming signal specificity and sensitivity to manipulation strength.

Abstract: Copy-move forgery, where a region within an image is duplicated to hide or fabricate content, remains a persistent threat to visual media integrity. We introduce GraphSpecForge, a training-free framework that detects copy-move forgery by analysing the spectral structure of attention graphs from a pretrained Stable Diffusion U-Net. Our central insight is that copy-move manipulation induces approximate subgraph duplication in the self-attention graph, leading to measurable spectral redistribution in the normalized graph Laplacian. We formalise this link with perturbation-based arguments and build an image-level anomaly detector using Wasserstein distances between per-image Laplacian spectra and an authentic reference distribution. We evaluate GraphSpecForge on four copy-move benchmarks without forgery-specific retraining. On RecodAI-LUC (5,128 images), our best configuration achieves AUROC = 0.606 (95% CI: 0.580-0.638; permutation p = 0.005), and the normalized Laplacian outperforms raw attention spectra by +0.057 AUROC. On MICC-F220, CoMoFoD, and COVERAGE, the same pipeline attains AUROCs of 0.752, 0.774, and 0.673, respectively; on CoMoFoD it also reaches AUPRC = 0.833, balanced accuracy = 0.712, MCC = 0.499, and TPR@1%FPR = 32.5%. Additional ablation and falsification experiments confirm the signal's specificity and sensitivity to manipulation strength, while null-graph controls rule out trivial-statistic explanations.

</details>


### [143] [Frequency-guided Multi-level Reasoning for Scene Graph Generation in Video](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17298)
*Chenxing Li,Yiping Duan,Xiaoming Tao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FReMuRe model improves video scene graph generation for long-tail relationships using frequency-guided multi-level reasoning, relation-specific branches, and interchangeable classification heads.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing video scene graph generation methods struggle with long-tail distributions of object relationships, limiting their ability to handle rare or infrequent relationships effectively.

Method: Proposes Frequency-guided Relational Multi-level Reasoning (FReMuRe) with: 1) relation-specific branches to handle gradient conflicts, 2) frequency-aware dual-branch predicate embedding network for high/low-frequency relationships, 3) interchangeable relation classification heads (Bayesian Head for uncertainty, Gaussian Mixture Model Head for intra-class diversity).

Result: FReMuRe significantly improves recall rate of long-tail relationships and overall reasoning robustness on the Action Genome dataset.

Conclusion: The proposed frequency-guided approach effectively addresses long-tail distribution challenges in video scene graph generation through specialized modeling of different relationship frequencies and enhanced classification mechanisms.

Abstract: Video Scene Graph Generation aims to obtain structured semantic representations of objects and their relationships in videos for high-level understanding. However, existing methods still have limitations in handling long-tail distributions. This paper proposes the Frequency-guided Relational Multi-level Reasoning (FReMuRe) model, which enhances the modeling ability of long-tail relationships from a mechanism perspective. We introduce relation-specific branches to deal gradient conflicts, yielding more balanced and tail-aware learning. And we design a frequency-aware dual-branch predicate embedding network to model high-frequency and low-frequency relationships separately and improve the recall rate of tail classes through gated fusion. Meanwhile, we propose two types of interchangeable relation classification heads: Bayesian Head for uncertainty estimation and new Gaussian Mixture Model Head to enhance intra-class diversity. Experimental results show that FReMuRe significantly improves the recall rate of long-tail relationships and overall reasoning robustness on the Action Genome dataset.

</details>


### [144] [The First Challenge on Mobile Real-World Image Super-Resolution at NTIRE 2026: Benchmark Results and Method Overview](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17306)
*Jiatong Li,Zheng Chen,Kai Liu,Jingkai Wang,Zihan Zhou,Xiaoyang Liu,Libo Zhu,Jue Gong,Radu Timofte,Yulun Zhang,Congyu Wang,Zihao Wang,Ke Wu,Xinzhe Zhu,Fengkai Zhang,Zhongbao Yang,Long Sun,Jiangxin Dong,Jinshan Pan,Jiachen Tu,Yaokun Shi,Guoyi Xu,Yaoxin Jiang,Jiajia Liu,Renyuan Situ,Yixin Yang,Zhaorun Zhou,Junyang Chen,Yuqi Li,Chuanguang Yang,Weilun Feng,Chuanyue Yan,Yuedong Tan,Yingli Tian,Zhenzhong Chen,Tongqi Guo,Ruhan Liu,Sangzi Shi,Huazhang Deng,Jie Yang,Wenzhuo Ma,Yuantong Zhang,Daiqin Yang,Tianrun Chen,Deyi Ji,Yuxiao Jiang,Qi Zhu,Lanyun Zhu,Yuwen Pan,Runze Tian,Mingyu Shi,Zhanfeng Feng,Yuanfei Bao,Jiaming Guo,Renjing Pei,Xin Di,Long Peng,Linfeng Jiang,Xueyang Fu,Yang Cao,Zhengjun Zha,Choulhyouc Lee,Shyang-En Weng,Yi-Cheng Liao,Jorge Tyrakowski,Yu-Syuan Xu,Wei-Chen Chiu,Ching-Chun Huang,Yoonjin Im,Jihye Park,Hyungju Chun,Hyunhee Park,MinKyu Park,Xiaoxuan Yu,Jianxing Zhang,Yuxuan Jiang,Chengxi Zeng,Tianhao Peng,Fan Zhang,David Bull,Watchara Ruangsang,Supavadee Aramvith,JiaHao Deng,Wei Zhou,Hongyu Huang,Shaohui Lin,Zihan Wang,Yilin Chen,Yunchen Li,Junbo Qiao,Wei Li,Jiao Xie,Gaoqi He,Wenxi Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Review of NTIRE 2026 mobile real-world image super-resolution challenge focusing on efficient x4 upscaling models for mobile devices, evaluating 16 final solutions based on quality-speed tradeoffs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To advance mobile real-world image super-resolution by developing efficient models that can perform x4 upscaling with unknown degradations while maintaining mobile device compatibility, addressing the gap between high-quality super-resolution and mobile deployment constraints.

Method: Challenge-based approach where participants develop network designs or solutions for x4 super-resolution from low-resolution images with unknown degradations, evaluated using weighted combination of image quality assessment scores and speedup ratios on mobile platforms.

Result: The challenge attracted 108 registrants with 16 teams achieving valid scores in final ranking, showcasing various effective and efficient solutions that advance the state-of-the-art in mobile real-world image super-resolution.

Conclusion: The collaborative challenge successfully advanced mobile real-world image super-resolution performance while providing comprehensive insights into current trends and effective approaches for balancing quality and efficiency on mobile devices.

Abstract: This paper provides a review of the NTIRE 2026 challenge on mobile real-world image super-resolution, highlighting the proposed solutions and the resulting outcomes. The challenge aims to recover high-resolution (HR) images from low-resolution (LR) counterparts generated through unknown degradations with a x4 scaling factor while ensuring the models remain executable on mobile devices. The objective is to develop effective and efficient network designs or solutions that achieve state-of-the-art real-world image super-resolution performance. The track of the challenge evaluates performance using a weighted combination of image quality assessment (IQA) score and speedup ratios. The competition attracted 108 registrants, with 16 teams achieving a valid score in the final ranking. This collaborative effort advances the performance of mobile real-world image super-resolution while offering an in-depth overview of the latest trends in the field.

</details>


### [145] [Generalizable Face Forgery Detection via Separable Prompt Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17307)
*Enrui Yang,Yuezun Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SePL uses CLIP's text modality with separable prompt learning to disentangle forgery-specific information for improved deepfake detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CLIP-based deepfake detection methods focus too much on visual encoders while underutilizing the instructive text modality, which could provide valuable guidance for detection.

Method: Proposes Separable Prompt Learning (SePL) with two types of prompts to disentangle forgery-specific and forgery-irrelevant information, using cross-modality alignment and dedicated objectives.

Result: Achieves competitive/superior performance in cross-dataset and cross-method evaluations, demonstrating strong generalizability.

Conclusion: Text modality in CLIP can be effectively leveraged through careful prompt design to create powerful deepfake detectors with excellent generalization capabilities.

Abstract: Detecting face forgeries using CLIP has recently emerged as a promising and increasingly popular research direction. Owing to its rich visual knowledge acquired through large-scale pretraining, most existing methods typically rely on the visual encoder of CLIP, while paying limited attention to the text modality. Given the instructive nature of the text modality, we posit that it can be leveraged to instruct Deepfake detection with meticulous design. Accordingly, we shift the focus from the visual modality to the text modality and propose a new Separable Prompt Learning strategy (SePL) that enables CLIP to serve as an effective face forgery detector. The core idea of SePL is to disentangle forgery-specific and forgery-irrelevant information in images via two types of prompt learning, with the former enhancing detection. To achieve this disentangle, we describe a cross-modality alignment strategy and a set of dedicated objectives. Extensive experiments demonstrate that, with this simple adaptation, our method achieves competitive and even superior performance compared to other methods under both cross-dataset and cross-method evaluation, highlighting its strong generalizability. The codes have been released at https://github.com/OUC-YER/SePL-DeepfakeDetection

</details>


### [146] [When Background Matters: Breaking Medical Vision Language Models by Transferable Attack](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17318)
*Akash Ghosh,Subhadip Baidya,Sriparna Saha,Xiuying Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MedFocusLeak is a black-box multimodal attack that makes VLMs give wrong but clinically plausible diagnoses using imperceptible perturbations in background areas, while distracting attention from pathology.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLMs are increasingly used in clinical diagnostics but their robustness to adversarial attacks is unexplored, posing serious risks. Existing medical attacks focus on secondary objectives or create visible distortions that clinicians can detect.

Method: Inject coordinated perturbations into non-diagnostic background regions and use attention distraction mechanism to shift model's focus away from pathological areas. Creates highly transferable black-box multimodal attack.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance across six medical imaging modalities, generating misleading yet realistic diagnostic outputs across diverse VLMs. Introduces unified evaluation framework with novel metrics capturing attack success and image fidelity.

Conclusion: Reveals critical weakness in reasoning capabilities of modern clinical VLMs, showing they can be manipulated to give incorrect but plausible diagnoses with imperceptible perturbations.

Abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are increasingly used in clinical diagnostics, yet their robustness to adversarial attacks remains largely unexplored, posing serious risks. Existing medical attacks focus on secondary objectives such as model stealing or adversarial fine-tuning, while transferable attacks from natural images introduce visible distortions that clinicians can easily detect. To address this, we propose MedFocusLeak, a highly transferable black-box multimodal attack that induces incorrect yet clinically plausible diagnoses while keeping perturbations imperceptible. The method injects coordinated perturbations into non-diagnostic background regions and employs an attention distraction mechanism to shift the model's focus away from pathological areas. Extensive evaluations across six medical imaging modalities show that MedFocusLeak achieves state-of-the-art performance, generating misleading yet realistic diagnostic outputs across diverse VLMs. We further introduce a unified evaluation framework with novel metrics that jointly capture attack success and image fidelity, revealing a critical weakness in the reasoning capabilities of modern clinical VLMs.

</details>


### [147] [E2E-GMNER: End-to-End Generative Grounded Multimodal Named Entity Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17319)
*Meng Zhang,Jinzhong Ning,Xiaolong Wu,Hongfei Lin,Yijia Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: E2E-GMNER is an end-to-end generative framework for Grounded Multimodal Named Entity Recognition that unifies entity recognition, typing, visual grounding, and knowledge reasoning in a single multimodal LLM, using instruction tuning and noise-aware bounding box supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing GMNER approaches use pipeline architectures that decouple textual entity recognition and visual grounding, leading to error accumulation and suboptimal joint optimization. There's a need for unified end-to-end frameworks that can jointly reason about text, vision, and background knowledge.

Method: Proposes E2E-GMNER: 1) Formulates GMNER as instruction-tuned conditional generation with chain-of-thought reasoning to adaptively use visual evidence or background knowledge; 2) Introduces Gaussian Risk-Aware Box Perturbation (GRBP) that replaces hard box supervision with probabilistically perturbed soft targets to handle annotation noise and discretization errors.

Result: Extensive experiments on Twitter-GMNER and Twitter-FMNERG benchmarks show E2E-GMNER achieves highly competitive performance compared to state-of-the-art methods, validating the effectiveness of unified end-to-end optimization and noise-aware grounding supervision.

Conclusion: The proposed end-to-end generative framework successfully unifies multiple GMNER subtasks, demonstrates the value of adaptive reasoning about when to use visual evidence vs. background knowledge, and shows that noise-aware bounding box supervision improves robustness against annotation noise.

Abstract: Grounded Multimodal Named Entity Recognition (GMNER) aims to jointly identify named entity mentions in text, predict their semantic types, and ground each entity to a corresponding visual region in an associated image. Existing approaches predominantly adopt pipeline-based architectures that decouple textual entity recognition and visual grounding, leading to error accumulation and suboptimal joint optimization. In this paper, we propose E2E-GMNER, a fully end-to-end generative framework that unifies entity recognition, semantic typing, visual grounding, and implicit knowledge reasoning within a single multimodal large language model. We formulate GMNER as an instruction-tuned conditional generation task and incorporate chain-of-thought reasoning to enable the model to adaptively determine when visual evidence or background knowledge is informative, reducing reliance on noisy cues. To further address the instability of generative bounding box prediction, we introduce Gaussian Risk-Aware Box Perturbation (GRBP), which replaces hard box supervision with probabilistically perturbed soft targets to improve robustness against annotation noise and discretization errors. Extensive experiments on the Twitter-GMNER and Twitter-FMNERG benchmarks demonstrate that E2E-GMNER achieves highly competitive performance compared with state of the art methods, validating the effectiveness of unified end-to-end optimization and noise-aware grounding supervision. Code is available at:https://github.com/Finch-coder/E2E-GMNER

</details>


### [148] [Towards Joint Quantization and Token Pruning of Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17320)
*Xinqing Li,Xin He,Xindong Zhang,Ming-Ming Cheng,Lei Zhang,Yun Liu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: QUOTA framework unifies low-bit quantization and visual token pruning for efficient VLM inference, achieving 95.65% accuracy retention with only 30% of visual tokens in W4A4 regime.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision-Language Models suffer from high inference costs due to long visual-token prefixes during prefill and growing KV cache during decoding. While token pruning and quantization can help, naive combinations are brittle due to mismatch between quantization calibration and pruning execution.

Method: Proposes QUOTA (Quantization Unified Offline Token Allocator) - a collaborative framework that unifies low-bit inference and deterministic visual-token pruning. It converts low-bit calibration signals into layer-wise token allocation schedules, evaluating token importance under deployed W4A4 operators using activation magnitude, attention cues, and explicit low-bit risk signals.

Result: Achieves 95.65% average accuracy retention while keeping only 30% of visual tokens in W4A4 regime, outperforming stage-wise baselines that achieve about 94.3% retention under same conditions.

Conclusion: QUOTA provides a robust unified framework for efficient VLM deployment that effectively combines quantization and pruning, addressing the brittleness of stage-wise approaches while maintaining high accuracy with aggressive compression.

Abstract: Deploying Vision-Language Models (VLMs) under aggressive low-bit inference remains challenging because inference cost is dominated by the long visual-token prefix during prefill and the growing KV cache during autoregressive decoding. Token pruning and low-bit quantization are complementary for reducing these costs, yet naive stage-wise combinations are often brittle due to a mismatch between quantization calibration and pruning execution. We present a collaborative quantization-and-pruning framework that unifies low-bit inference and deterministic visual-token pruning in a single deployable pipeline. The framework introduces the \textbf{Q}uantization \textbf{U}nified \textbf{O}ffline \textbf{T}oken \textbf{A}llocator (\textbf{QUOTA}), which converts low-bit calibration signals into a layer-wise token allocation schedule and materializes it as a pruning recipe. Token importance is evaluated under deployed W4A4 operators with a quantized KV cache by combining activation magnitude, attention cues, and an explicit low-bit risk signal, enabling consistent budgeted top-$k$ selection. Experiments on standard VLM benchmarks show improved robustness over stage-wise baselines under the same low-bit regime, achieving 95.65\% average retention while retaining only 30\% of visual tokens, compared with about 94.3\% retention for representative stage-wise combinations. The code will be released.

</details>


### [149] [R-FLoRA: Residual-Statistic-Gated Low-Rank Adaptation for Single-Image Face Morphing Attack Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17321)
*Raghavendra Ramachandra*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A new single-image face morphing attack detection framework using Laplacian residual statistics with frozen vision transformer, achieving state-of-the-art performance with real-time efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Face morphing attacks threaten face recognition systems in passport issuance and border control. Single-image detection is challenging due to lack of reference images and diverse attack methods.

Method: Integrates high-frequency Laplacian residual statistics with frozen foundation-scale vision transformer. Uses residual-statistic-gated low-rank adapters (R-FLoRA) and feature-wise residual fusion (Res-FiLM) to detect local morphing artifacts while preserving semantic context. Includes residual-contrastive alignment loss for better discrimination.

Result: Outperforms nine recent state-of-the-art S-MAD algorithms on four ICAO-compliant datasets with seven morph generation techniques. Achieves superior detection accuracy and cross-domain generalization with minimal trainable parameters and real-time efficiency.

Conclusion: The framework provides effective, efficient, and interpretable face morphing attack detection suitable for real-world biometric verification systems, addressing critical security vulnerabilities in identity verification processes.

Abstract: Face morphing attacks pose a substantial risk to the reliability of face recognition systems used in passport issuance, border control, and digital identity verification. Detecting morphing attacks from a single facial image remains challenging owing to the lack of a trusted reference and the diversity of attack generation methods. This paper presents a new Single-Image Face Morphing Attack Detection (S-MAD) framework that integrates high-frequency Laplacian residual statistics with representations from a frozen, foundation-scale vision transformer. The approach employs residual-statistic-gated low-rank adapters (R-FLoRA) and feature-wise residual fusion (Res-FiLM) to enhance sensitivity to local morphing artefacts while preserving the semantic context of the backbone. A novel residual-contrastive alignment loss further regularises the fused token space, improving discrimination under unseen morphing conditions. Comprehensive experiments on four ICAO-compliant datasets, encompassing seven morph generation techniques, demonstrate that the proposed method consistently surpasses nine recent state-of-the-art S-MAD algorithms in detection accuracy and cross-domain (or dataset) generalisation. With a frozen backbone and minimal trainable parameters, the model achieves real-time efficiency and interpretability, making it suitable for real-life scenarios in biometric verification systems.

</details>


### [150] [Robust Diabetic Retinopathy Grading Using Dual-Resolution Attention-Based Deep Learning with Ordinal Regression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17341)
*Afshan Hashmi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A dual-resolution deep learning framework with attention-based feature fusion and ordinal regression improves cross-dataset generalization for diabetic retinopathy grading.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Diabetic retinopathy is a major cause of vision impairment, but deep learning models often degrade when deployed across datasets with different imaging conditions, highlighting the need for more robust cross-dataset generalization.

Method: Uses two parallel EfficientNet backbones at different spatial resolutions with learnable attention fusion, ordinal regression (CORAL) for ordered severity levels, and preprocessing (circular cropping, contrast enhancement, histogram matching) to mitigate domain discrepancies.

Result: Achieved quadratic weighted kappa of 0.88 on APTOS validation set and 0.68 on unseen Messidor-2 dataset, demonstrating improved cross-dataset robustness.

Conclusion: The proposed framework with dual-resolution feature extraction, attention fusion, and ordinal regression effectively improves generalization across different DR datasets, making it suitable for real-world screening applications.

Abstract: Diabetic retinopathy (DR) is a leading cause of vision impairment worldwide, and automated grading systems play a crucial role in large-scale screening programs. However, deep learning models often exhibit degraded performance when deployed across datasets acquired under different imaging conditions. This study presents a robust dual-resolution deep learning framework for DR grading that integrates attention-based feature fusion with ordinal regression to improve cross-dataset generalization. The proposed method employs two parallel EfficientNet backbones operating at different spatial resolutions to capture complementary retinal features. A learnable attention mechanism adaptively fuses multi-resolution representations, while an ordinal regression formulation based on the cumulative link model (CORAL) explicitly accounts for the ordered nature of DR severity levels. To mitigate domain discrepancies between datasets, a preprocessing strategy combining circular cropping, contrast enhancement, and histogram matching is applied. The model was trained on the APTOS 2019 dataset and evaluated on both an internal validation split and an external Messidor-2 test set. Experimental results demonstrate strong grading performance, achieving a quadratic weighted kappa (QWK) of 0.88 on the APTOS validation set and 0.68 on the unseen Messidor-2 dataset, indicating improved robustness for cross-dataset DR grading applications.

</details>


### [151] [When Text Hijacks Vision: Benchmarking and Mitigating Text Overlay-Induced Hallucination in Vision Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17375)
*Cui Yakun,Xingqun Qi,TianTian Geng,Yuyao Zhang,Sirui Han,Yike Guo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Paper introduces VisualTextTrap benchmark for measuring text overlay-induced hallucination in VLMs, and proposes VTHM-MoE framework to mitigate this issue through vision-text disentanglement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLMs systematically hallucinate when on-screen text contradicts visual scenes, prioritizing text over visual content. This Text Overlay-Induced Hallucination (TOIH) is a critical but overlooked issue in multimodal video understanding.

Method: 1) Creates VisualTextTrap benchmark with 6,057 human-validated samples across 88 attributes and 5-level hallucination scale; 2) Proposes VTHM-MoE framework with dual-encoder architecture, four dimension-specialized experts (Temporal, Action, Object, Spatial), and Adaptive Token Routing Strategy for dynamic expert allocation.

Result: VTHM-MoE outperforms state-of-the-art models on VisualTextTrap benchmark across diverse video QA tasks, demonstrating robust resistance to TOIH while maintaining performance on uncontaminated videos.

Conclusion: The paper addresses a critical hallucination problem in VLMs, provides a comprehensive benchmark for evaluation, and offers an effective solution through vision-text disentanglement that dynamically leverages cross-modal discrepancies.

Abstract: Recent advances in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have substantially enhanced their ability across multimodal video understanding benchmarks spanning temporal, action, object, and spatial understanding. However, we identify a critical yet overlooked issue: when embedded on-screen text contradicts the visual scene, existing VLMs systematically hallucinate, prioritizing overlay textual semantics over the actual visual content. We define this phenomenon as Text Overlay-Induced Hallucination (TOIH). In this work, we propose VisualTextTrap, the first comprehensive benchmark, including large-scale human-validated samples with specifically designed evaluation metrics. In particular, we construct VisualTextTrap from widely-used public datasets using a scalable hybrid pipeline of VLMs assisted text generation and rigorous manual verification. The benchmark features 6,057 samples annotated across 88 fine-grained attributes within four dimensions, with hallucination intensity quantified on a five-level scale (L1--L5) that reflects the semantic contradiction between overlay text and visual reality. Moreover, we propose Visual Text Hallucination Mitigation Mixture-of-Experts (VTHM-MoE), a novel Vision-Text Disentanglement framework that employs a dual-encoder architecture. Concretely, four dimension-specialized expert modules spanning Temporal, Action, Object, and Spatial reasoning are first pre-trained to identify and leverage cross-modal discrepancies between textual semantics and actual video content. We develop an Adaptive Token Routing Strategy to enable dynamic expert allocation, conferring robust resistance to TOIH while preserving performance on uncontaminated videos. Extensive experiments conducted on our VisualTextTrap benchmark verify the effectiveness of VTHM-MoE, outperforming state-of-the-art counterparts with diverse video question answering tasks.

</details>


### [152] [Towards Generalizable Deepfake Image Detection with Vision Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17376)
*Kaliki V Srinanda,M Manvith Prabhu,Hemanth K Mogilipalem,Jayavarapu S Abhinai,Vaibhav Santhosh,Aryan Herur,Deepu Vijayasenan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Ensemble of fine-tuned vision transformers (DINOv2, AIMv2, OpenCLIP) achieves state-of-the-art deepfake detection on DF-Wild dataset, winning IEEE SP Cup 2025 with 96.77% AUC and 9% EER.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Addressing the challenge of detecting deepfake images due to rapid evolution of generative models and poor generalization of existing methods.

Method: Uses ensemble of fine-tuned vision transformers (DINOv2, AIMv2, OpenCLIP's ViT-L/14) trained on DF-Wild dataset, starting with CNN classifiers on spatial features as baseline.

Result: Ensemble outperforms individual models and CNN baselines, achieving 96.77% AUC and 9% EER on DF-Wild test set, beating state-of-the-art Effort by 7.05% AUC and 8% EER improvements.

Conclusion: Vision transformer ensemble approach provides highly generalizable deepfake detection, winning IEEE SP Cup 2025 and demonstrating superior performance over existing methods.

Abstract: In today's day and age, we face a challenge in detecting deepfake images because of the fast evolution of modern generative models and the poor generalization capability of existing methods. In this paper, we use an ensemble of fine-tuned vision transformers like DINOv2, AIMv2 and OpenCLIP's ViT-L/14 to create generalizable method to detect deepfakes. We use the DF-Wild dataset released as part of the IEEE SP Cup 2025, because it uses a challenging and diverse set of manipulations and generation techniques. We started our experiments with CNN classifiers trained on spatial features. Experimental results show that our ensemble outperforms individual models and strong CNN baselines, achieving an AUC of 96.77% and an Equal Error Rate (EER) of just 9% on the DF-Wild test set, beating the state-of-the-art deepfake detection algorithm Effort by 7.05% and 8% in AUC and EER respectively. This was the winning solution for SP Cup, presented at ICASSP 2025.

</details>


### [153] [SpatialImaginer: Towards Adaptive Visual Imagination for Spatial Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17385)
*Yian Li,Yang Jiao,Bin Zhu,Tianwen Qian,Shaoxiang Chen,Jingjing Chen,Yu-Gang Jiang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SpatialImaginer: A multimodal framework combining text reasoning with visual imagination to improve spatial intelligence in MLLMs by preserving geometric details throughout reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current MLLMs struggle with spatial intelligence tasks due to a mismatch between spatial recognition needs and text-only reasoning. Textual representations abstract away critical geometric details needed for consistent spatial state tracking.

Method: Proposes SpatialImaginer framework with divide-and-conquer strategy: text chain-of-thought for high-level semantic planning, visual imagination for geometry-sensitive state transformation and consistency preservation. Uses difficulty-aware data engine with closed-loop verification to train selective visual imagination invocation.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on diverse spatial intelligence benchmarks and substantially improves robustness on complex multi-step spatial reasoning tasks.

Conclusion: Integrating visual imagination with textual reasoning addresses the fundamental limitation of MLLMs in spatial intelligence by preserving low-level geometric structure throughout the reasoning process.

Abstract: Spatial intelligence, which refers to the ability to reason about geometric and physical structure from visual observations, remains a core challenge for multimodal large language models. Despite promising performance, recent multimodal large language models (MLLMs) often exhibit fragile reasoning traces in spatial intelligence tasks that involve consistent spatial state recognition. We argue that these failures stem from a mismatch between the spatial recognition mechanism and the text-only reasoning behavior of these MLLMs. Effective spatial reasoning requires low-level geometric structure to be faithfully preserved and updated throughout the reasoning process, whereas textual representations tend to abstract away precisely these critical details. To address this issue, we propose SpatialImaginer, a unified multimodal generation framework that integrates textual reasoning with visual imagination. Our framework adopts a divide-and-conquer strategy, using text chain-of-thought for high-level semantic planning and the visual imagination for geometry-sensitive state transformation and consistency preservation. To support this capability, we further introduce a difficulty-aware data engine with closed-loop verification to train the model to invoke visual imagination selectively when stable spatial state tracking is required. Extensive experiments on diverse spatial intelligence benchmarks show that SpatialImaginer achieves state-of-the-art performance and substantially improves robustness on complex multi-step spatial reasoning tasks.

</details>


### [154] [Deep learning based Non-Rigid Volume-to-Surface Registration for Brain Shift compensation Using Point Cloud](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17389)
*Eashrat Jahan Muniya,Gernot Kronreif,Ander Biguri,Wolfgang Birkfellner,Sepideh Hatamikia*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Deep learning framework for brain-shift compensation using partial intra-operative surface observations without requiring volumetric imaging or explicit correspondences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Brain shift during neurosurgery causes anatomical deviations from pre-operative images, compromising navigation accuracy. Existing compensation methods using intra-operative MRI/CT/US are disruptive to surgical workflow. Partial cortical surfaces from microscopes/LRS offer a practical alternative but present challenges due to sparsity and noise.

Method: Deep learning-based non-rigid volume-to-surface registration framework using multi-scale point-based feature extraction and hierarchical deformation decoder. Integrates partial intra-operative surface information into full pre-operative point cloud domain for implicit correspondence learning and dense deformation recovery.

Result: Accurate recovery of fine-scale deformations with Endpoint Error (EPE) of 1.13 ± 0.75 mm and RMSE of 1.33 ± 0.81 mm under challenging partial-surface conditions.

Conclusion: The approach enables automatic, workflow-compatible brain-shift compensation from sparse surface observations without disrupting surgery, addressing limitations of existing intra-operative imaging methods.

Abstract: Soft-tissue deformation remains a major limitation in image-guided neurosurgery, where intra-operative anatomy can deviate substantially from pre-operative imaging due to brain shift, compromising navigation accuracy and surgical safety. Existing compensation methods often rely on intra-operative MRI, CT, or ultrasound, which are disruptive and difficult to integrate repeatedly into the surgical workflow. In contrast, partial 3D cortical surfaces can be reconstructed as point clouds from stereoscopic microscopes or laser range scanners (LRS), capturing only a limited portion of the exposed cortex. This makes point cloud registration a practical alternative without interrupting surgery; however, such partial and noisy observations make deformation estimation highly challenging. In this study, we propose a deep learning-based framework for non-rigid volume-to-surface registration, enabling dense displacement field estimation from sparse intra-operative surface observations without explicit point correspondences or volumetric intra-operative imaging. The network leverages multi-scale point-based feature extraction and a hierarchical deformation decoder to capture both global and local deformations. The key contribution lies in integrating partial intra-operative surface information into the full pre-operative point cloud domain, enabling implicit correspondence learning and dense deformation recovery under limited visibility. Quantitative results demonstrate accurate recovery of fine-scale deformations, achieving an Endpoint Error (EPE) of 1.13 +/- 0.75 mm and RMSE of 1.33 +/- 0.81 mm under challenging partial-surface conditions. The proposed approach supports automatic, workflow-compatible brain-shift compensation from sparse surface observations.

</details>


### [155] [MESA: A Training-Free Multi-Exemplar Deep Framework for Restoring Ancient Inscription Textures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17390)
*Vasileios Toulatzis,Ioannis Fudos*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MESA is a novel image restoration method for damaged inscriptions that uses well-preserved exemplar inscriptions to guide reconstruction through multi-exemplar, style-aware synthesis with layer-wise feature matching and OCR-informed weighting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ancient inscriptions often have missing or corrupted regions due to fragmentation and erosion, making them difficult to read and analyze. Existing image restoration methods have limitations when applied to inscription recovery, necessitating a specialized approach.

Method: MESA uses VGG19 convolutional features encoded as Gram matrices to capture exemplar texture, style, and stroke structure. It selects exemplars minimizing Mean-Squared Displacement at each layer, uses OCR-estimated character widths for layer weighting, and employs a training mask to preserve intact regions while synthesizing only damaged areas.

Result: Comparative experiments demonstrate MESA's advantages over prior methods including single-image synthesis, inpainting, and GAN approaches, showing improved restoration of damaged inscriptions.

Conclusion: MESA effectively addresses limitations of previous restoration methods for inscriptions and provides a practical roadmap for choosing appropriate restoration strategies based on available exemplars and metadata.

Abstract: Ancient inscriptions frequently suffer missing or corrupted regions from fragmentation, erosion, or other damage, hindering reading, and analysis. We review prior image restoration methods and their applicability to inscription image recovery, then introduce MESA (Multi-Exemplar, Style-Aware) -an image-level restoration method that uses well-preserved exemplar inscriptions (from the same epigraphic monument, material, or similar letterforms) to guide reconstruction of damaged text. MESA encodes VGG19 convolutional features as Gram matrices to capture exemplar texture, style, and stroke structure; for each neural network layer it selects the exemplar minimizing Mean-Squared Displacement (MSD) to the damaged input. Layer-wise contribution weights are derived from Optical Character Recognition-estimated character widths in the exemplar set to bias filters toward scales matching letter geometry, and a training mask preserves intact regions so synthesis is restricted to damaged areas. We also summarize prior network architectures and exemplar and single-image synthesis, inpainting, and Generative Adversarial Network (GAN) approaches, highlighting limitations that MESA addresses. Comparative experiments demonstrate the advantages of MESA. Finally, we provide a practical roadmap for choosing restoration strategies given available exemplars and metadata.

</details>


### [156] [Speculative Decoding for Autoregressive Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17397)
*Yuezhou Hu,Jintao Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SDVG adapts speculative decoding from LLMs to autoregressive video diffusion by using an image-quality router instead of token verification, achieving 1.59-2.09x speedups while maintaining 95.7-98.1% of target model quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Speculative decoding accelerates LLMs but hasn't been adapted to autoregressive video generation because video blocks are continuous spatiotemporal tensors without token-level distributions for exact rejection sampling.

Method: Uses a 1.3B drafter model to propose candidate blocks via 4 denoising steps, then VAE-decodes and scores blocks with ImageReward using worst-frame aggregation. Blocks above threshold τ are accepted into 14B target's KV cache; others are regenerated by target. First block always force-rejected to anchor composition.

Result: On 1003 MovieGenVideoBench prompts (832x480), SDVG retains 98.1% of target-only VisionReward quality at 1.59x speedup (τ=-0.7), and reaches 2.09x speedup at 95.7% quality retention, outperforming draft-only generation by over +17%.

Conclusion: SDVG successfully brings speculative decoding to block-based autoregressive video diffusion, offering training-free acceleration with a single quality-speed knob τ, seamlessly integrable into existing pipelines without architectural changes.

Abstract: Autoregressive video diffusion is emerging as a promising paradigm for streaming video synthesis, with step distillation serving as the primary means of accelerating inference. Whether speculative decoding, the dominant acceleration strategy for large language models, can be effectively adapted to autoregressive video generation remains an open question, because video blocks are continuous spatiotemporal tensors with no token-level distribution for exact rejection sampling. We introduce SDVG, which brings speculative decoding to block-based autoregressive video diffusion by replacing token verification with an image-quality router. A 1.3B drafter proposes candidate blocks via four denoising steps; each block is VAE-decoded and scored by ImageReward using worst-frame aggregation--taking the minimum per-frame reward to catch single-frame artifacts that averaging would mask. Blocks scoring above a fixed threshold tau are accepted into the 14B target's KV cache; the rest are regenerated by the target. Two additional design choices prove critical: the first block is always force-rejected to anchor scene composition, and tau serves as a single knob that traces a smooth quality-speed Pareto frontier. On 1003 MovieGenVideoBench prompts (832x480), SDVG retains 98.1% of target-only VisionReward quality (0.0773 vs. 0.0788) at a 1.59x speedup with tau=-0.7, and reaches 2.09x at 95.7% quality retention--while consistently outperforming draft-only generation by over +17%. The framework is training-free, requires no architectural changes, and can be seamlessly integrated into existing autoregressive video generation pipelines.

</details>


### [157] [Where to Focus: Query-Modulated Multimodal Keyframe Selection for Long Video Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17422)
*Shaoguang Wang,Weiyu Guo,Ziyang Chen,Xuming Hu,Hui Xiong*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Q-Gate is a training-free framework for long video understanding that dynamically routes keyframe selection through modality-specific experts based on query intent, outperforming SOTA methods by suppressing modal noise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current MLLMs struggle with long videos due to computational costs. Existing keyframe selection methods use either visual-only metrics (ineffective for narrative queries) or static fusion of scores (introduces modal noise for visual tasks), creating a "one-size-fits-all" bottleneck.

Method: Q-Gate decouples retrieval into three expert streams: Visual Grounding (local details), Global Matching (scene semantics), and Contextual Alignment (subtitle narratives). It uses a Query-Modulated Gating Mechanism where an LLM assesses query intent to dynamically allocate attention weights across experts, activating necessary modalities while muting irrelevant ones.

Result: Extensive experiments on LongVideoBench and Video-MME across multiple MLLM backbones show Q-Gate substantially outperforms state-of-the-art baselines by effectively suppressing modality-specific noise.

Conclusion: Q-Gate provides a robust, highly interpretable, plug-and-play solution for scalable video reasoning by treating keyframe selection as a dynamic modality routing problem, maximizing signal-to-noise ratio through intelligent modality activation.

Abstract: Long video understanding remains a formidable challenge for Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) due to the prohibitive computational cost of processing dense frame sequences. Prevailing solutions, which select a keyframe subset, typically rely on either a single visual-centric metric (e.g., CLIP similarity) or a static fusion of heuristic scores. This ``one-size-fits-all'' paradigm frequently fails: visual-only metrics are ineffective for plot-driven narrative queries, while indiscriminately incorporating textual scores introduces severe ``modal noise'' for purely visual tasks. To break this bottleneck, we propose Q-Gate, a plug-and-play and training-free framework that treats keyframe selection as a dynamic modality routing problem. We decouple the retrieval process into three lightweight expert streams: Visual Grounding for local details, Global Matching for scene semantics, and Contextual Alignment for subtitle-driven narratives. Crucially, Q-Gate introduces a Query-Modulated Gating Mechanism that leverages the in-context reasoning of an LLM to assess the query's intent and dynamically allocate attention weights across the experts. This mechanism intelligently activates necessary modalities while ``muting'' irrelevant ones, thereby maximizing the signal-to-noise ratio. Extensive experiments on LongVideoBench and Video-MME across multiple MLLM backbones demonstrate that Q-Gate substantially outperforms state-of-the-art baselines. By effectively suppressing modality-specific noise, it provides a robust, highly interpretable solution for scalable video reasoning.

</details>


### [158] [Long-CODE: Isolating Pure Long-Context as an Orthogonal Dimension in Video Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17428)
*Zhijiang Tang,Jiaxin Qi,Bing Zhao,Jianqiang Huang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes Long-CODE, a specialized framework and benchmark for evaluating long-video generation, addressing limitations of existing short-video metrics that fail to capture narrative richness and global consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current video evaluation metrics are designed for short videos and only assess frame-level visual quality and localized temporal smoothness. As video generation models advance to produce longer videos, these metrics fail to capture essential long-range characteristics like narrative richness and global causal consistency.

Method: 1) Introduces long-video attribute corruption tests to expose limitations of existing short-video metrics. 2) Designs a novel long-video metric based on shot dynamics sensitive to long-range characteristics. 3) Creates Long-CODE dataset with human annotations specifically for genuine long-range video attributes.

Result: The proposed metrics achieve state-of-the-art correlation with human judgments for long-video evaluation. The framework effectively complements existing short-video standards.

Conclusion: Long-video evaluation should be disentangled from short-video assessment as they measure orthogonal dimensions. The proposed Long-CODE framework establishes a holistic and unbiased evaluation paradigm for video generation models by addressing both short-term visual perception and long-context attributes.

Abstract: As video generation models achieve unprecedented capabilities, the demand for robust video evaluation metrics becomes increasingly critical. Traditional metrics are intrinsically tailored for short-video evaluation, predominantly assessing frame-level visual quality and localized temporal smoothness. However, as state-of-the-art video generation models scale to generate longer videos, these metrics fail to capture essential long-range characteristics, such as narrative richness and global causal consistency. Recognizing that short-term visual perception and long-context attributes are fundamentally orthogonal dimensions, we argue that long-video metrics should be disentangled from short-video assessments. In this paper, we focus on the rigorous justification and design of a dedicated framework for long-video evaluation. We first introduce a suite of long-video attribute corruption tests, exposing the critical limitations of existing hort-video metrics from their insensitivity to structural inconsistencies, such as shot-level perturbations and narrative shuffling. To bridge this gap, we design a novel long-video metric based on shot dynamics, which is highly sensitive to the long-range testing framework. Furthermore, we introduce Long-CODE (Long-Context as an Orthogonal Dimension for video Evaluation), a specialized dataset designed to benchmark long-video evaluation, with human annotations isolated specifically to genuine long-range characteristics. Extensive experiments show that our proposed metrics achieve state-of-the-art correlation with human judgments. Ultimately, our metric and benchmark seamlessly complement existing short-video standards, establishing a holistic and unbiased evaluation paradigm for video generation models.

</details>


### [159] [DEM Refinement and Validation on the Lunar Surface Using Shape-from-Shading with Chandrayaan-2 OHRC Imagery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17436)
*Aaranay Aadi,Jai Gopal Singla,Nitant Dube*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Shape from Shading framework enhances lunar DEMs using Chandrayaan-2 OHRC imagery, providing new topographic data beyond stereo limitations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve sub-metre resolution lunar digital elevation models by overcoming stereo baseline limitations and capturing fine-scale topographic features that stereo methods miss.

Method: Apply Shape from Shading to independent OHRC images of lunar regions, using systematic three-stage parameter sweep on SfS smoothness weight across three test sites (Cyrillus crater, Vikram landing region, lunar south pole).

Result: Measurable topographic enhancement, especially in surface slope statistics, revealing previously unresolved fine-scale crater morphology. Identified limitations with large pitch angle separation and partial footprint coverage affecting enhancement quality.

Conclusion: SfS can effectively enhance lunar DEMs as both refinement tool and source of new topographic data, though performance depends on imaging geometry and coverage.

Abstract: This study presents a Shape from Shading (SfS) framework to enhance sub-metre resolution lunar digital elevation models (DEMs) using imagery from the Orbiter High Resolution Camera (OHRC) aboard Chandrayaan-2. The framework applies SfS to an independent OHRC image of the same region, enabling SfS not just as a refinement tool, but as a source of new topographic data, unconstrained by stereo baseline limitations. The method is applied across three lunar sites, including the Cyrillus crater, the Vikram landing region, and the lunar south pole (Mons Mouton), with a systematic three-stage parameter sweep on the SfS smoothness weight. Results show measurable topographic enhancement, particularly in surface slope statistics, revealing fine-scale crater morphology previously unresolved. A limiting case is also characterized, where large pitch angle separation between the shading image and stereo pair reduces SfS sensitivity, and partial footprint coverage of the shading image is identified as a factor influencing spatially variable enhancement quality.

</details>


### [160] [Attention Is not Everything: Efficient Alternatives for Vision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17439)
*Nur Mohammad Kazi,Ibteshum Khaled,Md. Luthful Hasan Galib,Ali Faruk Shihab,Md. Rakibul Islam*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Review paper categorizing non-Transformer computer vision methods (convolution-based, MLP-based, state-space models, etc.) and analyzing their efficiency, scalability, interpretability, and robustness across 40 papers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite recent Transformer dominance in computer vision, many non-Transformer methods remain competitive. This review aims to provide a comprehensive taxonomy of these alternative approaches and understand their current standing in the field.

Method: Systematic review organizing non-Transformer methods into categories (convolution-based, MLP-based, state-space-based, etc.) and analyzing them across four dimensions: efficiency, scalability, interpretability, and robustness. Analysis based on 40 selected papers.

Result: Comprehensive taxonomy of non-Transformer computer vision methods with comparative analysis of their performance characteristics. Identifies strengths and limitations of each category relative to Transformer-based approaches.

Conclusion: Non-Transformer methods remain viable alternatives to Transformers in computer vision, with distinct advantages in certain areas. The review highlights challenges and opportunities for future research in developing efficient, scalable, interpretable, and robust vision models beyond the Transformer paradigm.

Abstract: Recently computer vision has seen advancements mainly thanks to Transformer-based models. However many non-Transformer methods are still doing well being a direct competition of Transformer-based models. This review tries to present a comprehensive taxonomy of such methods and organize these methods into categories like convolution-based models, MLP-based models, state-space-based and more. These methods are looked at in terms of how efficient they are, how well they scale, how easy they are to understand and how robust they are. A total of 40 papers were chosen for this study. The goal is to give a view of non-Transformer methods and find out what challenges and opportunities exist for future computer vision research.

</details>


### [161] [HyKey: Hyperspectral Keypoint Detection and Matching in Minimally Invasive Surgery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17446)
*Alexander Saikia,Chiara Di Vece,Zhehua Mao,Sierra Bonilla,Chloe He,Joao Ramalhinho,Tobias Czempiel,Sophia Bano,Danail Stoyanov*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HyKey: A hyperspectral keypoint detection model for surgical 3D reconstruction that outperforms RGB methods in texture-poor environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional RGB-based keypoint detection struggles in minimally invasive surgery due to poor texture and complex illumination. Hyperspectral imaging may provide better spectral-spatial features for robust 3D reconstruction.

Method: Developed HyKey, a hybrid 3D-2D CNN that extracts spatial-spectral features from hyperspectral images. Trained using synthetic homographic augmentation and epipolar geometry constraints on a dual-camera RGB-HSI laparoscopic dataset.

Result: Outperformed RGB baselines (SuperPoint, ALIKE) with 96.62% mean matching accuracy and 67.18% mean average accuracy at 10 degrees on pose estimation, showing consistent improvements across metrics.

Conclusion: Hyperspectral imaging offers promising improvements for monocular 3D reconstruction in MIS by enhancing spectral-spatial feature discrimination in texture-poor surgical environments.

Abstract: Purpose: 3D reconstruction in minimally invasive surgery (MIS) enables enhanced surgical guidance through improved visualisation, tool tracking, and augmented reality. However, traditional RGB-based keypoint detection and matching pipelines struggle with surgical challenges, such as poor texture and complex illumination. We investigate whether using snapshot hyperspectral imaging (HSI) can provide improved results on keypoint detection and matching surgical scenes. Methods: We developed HyKey, a HYperspectral KEYpoint detection and description model made up of a hybrid 3D-2D convolutional neural network that jointly extracts spatial-spectral features from HSI. The model was trained using synthetic homographic augmentation and epipolar geometry constraints on a robotically-acquired dual-camera RGB-HSI laparoscopic dataset of ex-vivo organs with calibrated camera poses. We benchmarked performance against established RGB-based methods, including SuperPoint and ALIKE. Results: Our HSI-based model outperformed RGB baselines on registered RGB frames, achieving 96.62% mean matching accuracy and 67.18% mean average accuracy at 10 degree on pose estimation, demonstrating consistent improvements across multiple evaluation metrics. Conclusion: Integrating spectral information from an HSI cube offers a promising approach for robust monocular 3D reconstruction in MIS, addressing limitations of texture-poor surgical environments through enhanced spectral-spatial feature discrimination. Our model and dataset are available at https://github.com/alexsaikia/HyKey-Hyperspectral-Keypoint-Detection

</details>


### [162] [SegTTA: Training-Free Test-Time Augmentation for Zero-Shot Medical Imaging Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17451)
*Yihong Yao,Chunlei Li,Canxuan Gang,Wenzhi Hu,Zeyu Zhang,Hao Zhang,Xiaoyan Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SegTTA improves medical image segmentation without retraining by combining four augmentations with weighted voting across MedSAM2 checkpoints, achieving consistent improvements across diverse datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Medical image quality varies due to equipment and operator differences, creating challenges for segmentation models despite their strong generalization abilities. Current models need better adaptation to real-world clinical variations without requiring retraining.

Method: Combines four augmentations (Gamma correction, Contrast enhancement, Gaussian blur, Gaussian noise) with weighted voting across multiple MedSAM2 checkpoints. Uses voting threshold to control coverage-precision trade-off for task-specific optimization.

Result: Consistent improvements across three diverse datasets: healthy uterus segmentation, uterine myoma detection, and multi-class hepatic structure segmentation. On hepatic vessel dataset, achieved 1.6 increase in mIoU, 1.9 increase in aIoU, and ~2.0 reduction in HD95 compared to MedSAM2 baselines.

Conclusion: SegTTA effectively improves medical image segmentation without model retraining, with different augmentation strategies benefiting different organ sizes (large organs need intensity augmentations, small lesions need noise augmentations), enabling task-specific optimization for clinical requirements.

Abstract: Increasingly advanced data augmentation techniques have greatly aided clinical medical research, increasing data diversity and improving model generalization capabilities. Although most current basic models exhibit strong generalization abilities, image quality varies due to differences in equipment and operators. To address these challenges, we present SegTTA, a framework that improves medical image segmentation without model retraining by combining four augmentations (Gamma correction, Contrast enhancement, Gaussian blur, Gaussian noise) with weighted voting across multiple MedSAM2 checkpoints. Experiments demonstrate consistent improvements across three diverse datasets: healthy uterus segmentation, uterine myoma detection, and multi class hepatic structure segmentation. Ablation studies reveal that large organs benefit from intensity augmentations while small lesions require noise augmentations. The voting threshold controls the coverage precision trade off, enabling task specific optimization for different clinical requirements. Ultimately, on a multiclass hepatic vessel dataset, compared to MedSAM2 baselines, our method achieves an increase of 1.6 in mIoU and 1.9 in aIoU, along with a reduction of approximately 2.0 in HD95. Code will be available at https://github.com/AIGeeksGroup/SegTTA.

</details>


### [163] [HSG: Hyperbolic Scene Graph](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17454)
*Liyang Wang,Zeyu Zhang,Hao Tang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HSG learns scene graph embeddings in hyperbolic space to better capture hierarchical relationships, outperforming Euclidean methods on graph-level metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Euclidean geometry doesn't explicitly capture hierarchical entailment relationships between places and objects in scene graphs, limiting structural consistency of learned representations.

Method: Proposes Hyperbolic Scene Graph (HSG) which learns scene graph embeddings in hyperbolic space where hierarchical relationships are naturally encoded through geometric distance.

Result: HSG improves hierarchical structure quality while maintaining strong retrieval performance, achieving PP IoU of 33.17 and Graph IoU of 33.51, outperforming best AoMSG variant (25.37) by 8.14.

Conclusion: Hyperbolic representation learning is effective for scene graph modeling, particularly for capturing hierarchical relationships that Euclidean methods struggle with.

Abstract: Scene graph representations enable structured visual understanding by modeling objects and their relationships, and have been widely used for multiview and 3D scene reasoning. Existing methods such as MSG learn scene graph embeddings in Euclidean space using contrastive learning and attention based association. However, Euclidean geometry does not explicitly capture hierarchical entailment relationships between places and objects, limiting the structural consistency of learned representations. To address this, we propose Hyperbolic Scene Graph (HSG), which learns scene graph embeddings in hyperbolic space where hierarchical relationships are naturally encoded through geometric distance. Our results show that HSG improves hierarchical structure quality while maintaining strong retrieval performance. The largest gains are observed in graph level metrics: HSG achieves a PP IoU of 33.17 and the highest Graph IoU of 33.51, outperforming the best AoMSG variant (25.37) by 8.14, highlighting the effectiveness of hyperbolic representation learning for scene graph modeling. Code: https://github.com/AIGeeksGroup/HSG.

</details>


### [164] [From Adaptation to Generalization: Adaptive Visual Prompting for Medical Image Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17455)
*Evren Çetinkaya,Sangmin Lee,Jung Uk Kim,Hong Joo Lee,Nassir Navab*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: APEX is an adaptive prompting framework that retrieves input-specific prompts from a learnable memory using Fourier domain features, improving generalization in medical segmentation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing visual prompting methods use a single prompt per domain applied uniformly to all inputs, which limits generalization under intra and inter-domain variability, especially critical in medical applications.

Method: APEX retrieves input-specific prompts from a learnable prompt memory using domain features extracted from Fourier spectrum. It uses Low-Frequency Feature Contrastive (LFC) learning to cluster same-domain representations and separate different-domain ones.

Result: Extensive experiments on two medical segmentation tasks show APEX significantly improves generalization across both seen and unseen domains, works as a plug-and-play solution with any backbone, and consistently enhances performance.
