We present a dataset created for this application and outline the training process. We evaluate the performance of YOLO versions 3, 5, and 8 and architecture sizes (nano, extra-large) using Mean Average Precision (mAP) metrics mAP@.5 and mAP@.5:.95. Furthermore, we present two Discrete-Event Simulation (DES) approaches to simulate response times of SRO and UAS-based water rescue. This enables estimation of time savings relative to SRO when selecting the UAS configuration (type, number, and location of UAVs and hangars). Computational experiments for a test area in the Lusatian Lake District, Germany, show that UAS assistance shortens response time. Even a small UAS with two hangars, each containing one UAV, reduces response time by a factor of five compared to SRO.

</details>


### [218] [E3VS-Bench: A Benchmark for Viewpoint-Dependent Active Perception in 3D Gaussian Splatting Scenes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17969)
*Koya Sakamoto,Taiki Miyanishi,Daichi Azuma,Shuhei Kurita,Shu Morikuni,Naoya Chiba,Motoaki Kawanabe,Yusuke Iwasawa,Yutaka Matsuo*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: E3VS-Bench: A benchmark for embodied 3D visual search requiring 5-DoF viewpoint control to gather evidence for question answering, using 3D Gaussian Splatting for photorealistic rendering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing visual search benchmarks rely on static observations or constrained motion, failing to evaluate fine-grained viewpoint-dependent phenomena in real-world 3D environments with unrestricted 5-DoF viewpoint control.

Method: Introduce E3VS-Bench with 99 high-fidelity 3D scenes reconstructed using 3D Gaussian Splatting and 2,014 question-driven episodes. 3D Gaussian Splatting enables photorealistic free-viewpoint rendering that preserves fine-grained visual details.

Result: Evaluation of state-of-the-art VLMs shows substantial performance gap compared to humans, highlighting limitations in active perception and coherent viewpoint planning under full 5-DoF viewpoint changes.

Conclusion: E3VS-Bench addresses limitations of existing benchmarks by enabling evaluation of embodied 3D visual search requiring active viewpoint control, revealing significant challenges for current models in handling viewpoint-dependent evidence gathering.

Abstract: Visual search in 3D environments requires embodied agents to actively explore their surroundings and acquire task-relevant evidence. However, existing visual search and embodied AI benchmarks, including EQA, typically rely on static observations or constrained egocentric motion, and thus do not explicitly evaluate fine-grained viewpoint-dependent phenomena that arise under unrestricted 5-DoF viewpoint control in real-world 3D environments, such as visibility changes caused by vertical viewpoint shifts, revealing contents inside containers, and disambiguating object attributes that are only observable from specific angles. To address this limitation, we introduce {E3VS-Bench}, a benchmark for embodied 3D visual search where agents must control their viewpoints in 5-DoF to gather viewpoint-dependent evidence for question answering. E3VS-Bench consists of 99 high-fidelity 3D scenes reconstructed using 3D Gaussian Splatting and 2,014 question-driven episodes. 3D Gaussian Splatting enables photorealistic free-viewpoint rendering that preserves fine-grained visual details (e.g., small text and subtle attributes) often degraded in mesh-based simulators, thereby allowing the construction of questions that cannot be answered from a single view and instead require active inspection across viewpoints in 5-DoF. We evaluate multiple state-of-the-art VLMs and compare their performance with humans. Despite strong 2D reasoning ability, all models exhibit a substantial gap from humans, highlighting limitations in active perception and coherent viewpoint planning specifically under full 5-DoF viewpoint changes.

</details>


### [219] [Soft Label Pruning and Quantization for Large-Scale Dataset Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18135)
*Xiao Lingao,Yang He*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LPQLD reduces soft label storage by 78-500x while improving accuracy in large-scale dataset distillation by addressing insufficient image and supervision diversity through class-wise batching, label pruning, and quantization techniques.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current large-scale dataset distillation methods require storing auxiliary soft labels that are 30-40x larger than condensed images on ImageNet-1K and 200x larger on ImageNet-21K, which undermines the goal of dataset compression. Two fundamental issues are identified: insufficient image diversity (high within-class similarity requiring extensive augmentation) and insufficient supervision diversity (limited variety in supervisory signals causing performance degradation at high compression rates).

Method: LPQLD (Label Pruning and Quantization for Large-scale Distillation) enhances image diversity via class-wise batching and batch-normalization supervision during synthesis. For supervision diversity, it introduces Label Pruning with Dynamic Knowledge Reuse to improve label-per-augmentation diversity, and Label Quantization with Calibrated Student-Teacher Alignment to improve augmentation-per-image diversity.

Result: The approach reduces soft label storage by 78x on ImageNet-1K and 500x on ImageNet-21K while improving accuracy by up to 7.2% and 2.8%, respectively. Extensive experiments validate superiority across different network architectures and dataset distillation methods.

Conclusion: LPQLD successfully addresses the storage inefficiency of soft labels in large-scale dataset distillation while improving performance, making dataset compression more practical for real-world applications. The method demonstrates significant improvements in both storage reduction and accuracy across multiple datasets and architectures.

Abstract: Large-scale dataset distillation requires storing auxiliary soft labels that can be 30-40x larger on ImageNet-1K and 200x larger on ImageNet-21K than the condensed images, undermining the goal of dataset compression. We identify two fundamental issues necessitating such extensive labels: (1) insufficient image diversity, where high within-class similarity in synthetic images requires extensive augmentation, and (2) insufficient supervision diversity, where limited variety in supervisory signals during training leads to performance degradation at high compression rates. To address these challenges, we propose Label Pruning and Quantization for Large-scale Distillation (LPQLD). We enhance image diversity via class-wise batching and batch-normalization supervision during synthesis. For supervision diversity, we introduce Label Pruning with Dynamic Knowledge Reuse to improve label-per-augmentation diversity, and Label Quantization with Calibrated Student-Teacher Alignment to improve augmentation-per-image diversity. Our approach reduces soft label storage by 78x on ImageNet-1K and 500x on ImageNet-21K while improving accuracy by up to 7.2% and 2.8%, respectively. Extensive experiments validate the superiority of LPQLD across different network architectures and dataset distillation methods. Code is available at https://github.com/he-y/soft-label-pruning-quantization-for-dataset-distillation.

</details>


### [220] [Identifying Ethical Biases in Action Recognition Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17971)
*Ana Baltaretu,Pascal Benschop,Jan van Gemert*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper introduces a framework for auditing bias in Human Action Recognition models using synthetic video data with controlled visual identity attributes like skin color, revealing systematic biases in popular models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: HAR models are deployed in high-stakes environments but their fairness across different human appearances hasn't been analyzed. There's a need to audit bias in these models as they may encode unwanted visual associations, especially with upcoming regulatory standards requiring transparent, accountable systems.

Method: Developed a framework using synthetic video data generated with full control over visual identity attributes (particularly skin color) via the BEDLAM simulation platform. Unlike prior work on static images or pose estimation, this approach preserves temporal consistency to isolate how changes to single attributes affect model predictions through controlled interventions.

Result: Found that some popular HAR models exhibit statistically significant biases based on skin color even when motion remains identical. Provided evidence of systematic errors across groups, demonstrating how models may encode unwanted visual associations.

Conclusion: The work contributes a framework for auditing HAR models and supports development of more transparent, accountable systems in light of upcoming regulatory standards, highlighting the importance of fairness testing in action recognition systems.

Abstract: Human Action Recognition (HAR) models are increasingly deployed in high-stakes environments, yet their fairness across different human appearances has not been analyzed. We introduce a framework for auditing bias in HAR models using synthetic video data, generated with full control over visual identity attributes such as skin color. Unlike prior work that focuses on static images or pose estimation, our approach preserves temporal consistency, allowing us to isolate and test how changes to a single attribute affect model predictions. Through controlled interventions using the BEDLAM simulation platform, we show whether some popular HAR models exhibit statistically significant biases on the skin color even when the motion remains identical. Our results highlight how models may encode unwanted visual associations, and we provide evidence of systematic errors across groups. This work contributes a framework for auditing HAR models and supports the development of more transparent, accountable systems in light of upcoming regulatory standards.

</details>


### [221] [Region-Grounded Report Generation for 3D Medical Imaging: A Fine-Grained Dataset and Graph-Enhanced Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18145)
*Cong Huy Nguyen,Son Dinh Nguyen,Guanlin Li,Tuan Dung Nguyen,Aditya Narayan Sankaran,Mai Huy Thong,Thanh Trung Nguyen,Mai Hong Son,Reza Farahbakhsh,Phi Le Nguyen,Noel Crespi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VietPET-RoI dataset with 600 PET/CT samples and 1,960 annotated RoIs for Vietnamese, plus HiRRA framework using graph-based relational modules to mimic radiologist workflow, achieving SOTA performance with 45.8% improvement in clinical metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current medical report generation methods for 3D PET/CT imaging use black-box approaches that map whole volumes to reports, ignoring the clinical workflow of analyzing localized Regions of Interest (RoIs). There's also a critical scarcity of annotated datasets for low-resource languages.

Method: Introduced VietPET-RoI dataset (600 PET/CT samples, 1,960 annotated RoIs for Vietnamese) and HiRRA framework that mimics radiologist workflow using graph-based relational modules to capture dependencies between RoI attributes, shifting from global pattern matching to localized clinical findings.

Result: Achieved SOTA performance with 19.7% improvement in BLEU and 4.7% in ROUGE-L, plus remarkable 45.8% improvement in new clinical metrics (RoI Coverage and RoI Quality Index) measuring localization accuracy and attribute description fidelity.

Conclusion: The proposed approach enhances clinical reliability and reduces hallucination in automated medical report generation by focusing on localized RoI analysis and introducing specialized clinical evaluation metrics, with code and dataset publicly available.

Abstract: Automated medical report generation for 3D PET/CT imaging is fundamentally challenged by the high-dimensional nature of volumetric data and a critical scarcity of annotated datasets, particularly for low-resource languages. Current black-box methods map whole volumes to reports, ignoring the clinical workflow of analyzing localized Regions of Interest (RoIs) to derive diagnostic conclusions. In this paper, we bridge this gap by introducing VietPET-RoI, the first large-scale 3D PET/CT dataset with fine-grained RoI annotation for a low-resource language, comprising 600 PET/CT samples and 1,960 manually annotated RoIs, paired with corresponding clinical reports. Furthermore, to demonstrate the utility of this dataset, we propose HiRRA, a novel framework that mimics the professional radiologist diagnostic workflow by employing graph-based relational modules to capture dependencies between RoI attributes. This approach shifts from global pattern matching toward localized clinical findings. Additionally, we introduce new clinical evaluation metrics, namely RoI Coverage and RoI Quality Index, that measure both RoI localization accuracy and attribute description fidelity using LLM-based extraction. Extensive evaluation demonstrates that our framework achieves SOTA performance, surpassing existing models by 19.7% in BLEU and 4.7% in ROUGE-L, while achieving a remarkable 45.8% improvement in clinical metrics, indicating enhanced clinical reliability and reduced hallucination. Our code and dataset are available on GitHub.

</details>


### [222] [Mitigating Multimodal Hallucination via Phase-wise Self-reward](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.17982)
*Yu Zhang,Chuyang Sun,Kehai Chen,Xuefeng Bai,Yang Xiang,Min Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PSRD: A self-rewarding framework that dynamically mitigates vision hallucination in LVLMs at inference time without external supervision, reducing hallucination rates by 50% for LLaVA-1.5-7B.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LVLMs suffer from vision hallucination where responses don't match visual input. Existing methods either require expensive fine-tuning with annotated data or use static post-hoc strategies that ignore the dynamic nature of hallucination emergence.

Method: PSRD (Phase-wise Self-Reward Decoding) uses phase-wise self-reward signals for online hallucination correction. Key insights: hallucinations peak at semantic phase onsets. A lightweight reward model is distilled from LVLMs to provide on-the-fly guidance during decoding, enabling precise hallucination suppression without repeated self-evaluation.

Result: PSRD reduces hallucination rate of LLaVA-1.5-7B by 50.0% and consistently outperforms existing post-hoc methods across five hallucination evaluation benchmarks for four LVLMs. Effectively mitigates hallucination propagation and achieves controllable trade-off between performance and inference efficiency.

Conclusion: PSRD provides an effective, efficient framework for dynamic hallucination mitigation at inference time without external supervision, addressing key limitations of existing approaches while maintaining strong performance.

Abstract: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) still struggle with vision hallucination, where generated responses are inconsistent with the visual input. Existing methods either rely on large-scale annotated data for fine-tuning, which incurs massive computational overhead, or employ static post-hoc strategies that overlook the dynamic nature of hallucination emergence. To address these, we introduce a new self-rewarding framework, enabling dynamic hallucination mitigation at inference time without external supervision. On the empirical side, we reveal that visual hallucination exhibits phase-wise dynamic patterns, peaking at the onset of each semantic phase. Drawing on these insights, we propose \textbf{PSRD} (\textbf{Phase-wise \textbf{S}elf-\textbf{R}eward \textbf{D}ecoding) for online hallucination correction guided by phase-wise self-reward signals. To reduce the cost of repeated self-evaluation during decoding, we distill the hallucination guidance signal from LVLMs into a lightweight reward model. The reward model subsequently provides on-the-fly guidance for targeted intervention during the decoding process, enabling precise hallucination suppression. The proposed PSRD significantly reduces the hallucination rate of LLaVA-1.5-7B by 50.0% and consistently outperforms existing post-hoc methods across five hallucination evaluation benchmarks for four LVLMs. Further analysis confirms that PSRD effectively mitigates hallucination propagation and achieves a highly controllable trade-off between strong performance and inference efficiency.

</details>


### [223] [Is SAM3 ready for pathology segmentation?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18225)
*Qiuyu Kong,Shakiba Sharifi,Zanxi Ruan,Yiming Wang,Marco Cristani*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SAM3's Promptable Concept Segmentation shows limited effectiveness for pathology images - text prompts poorly activate nuclear concepts, performance is sensitive to visual prompts, and there's a significant gap compared to task-trained models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Digital pathology segmentation requires methods that reduce annotation costs and improve generalization across tissue and nuclei scales. SAM3's promptable segmentation offers potential for automated pathology analysis via text prompts.

Method: Systematic evaluation protocol assessing SAM3 under different supervision settings (zero-shot, few-shot, supervised) with varying prompting strategies on pathological datasets (NuInsSeg, PanNuke, GlaS).

Result: 1. Text-only prompts poorly activate nuclear concepts. 2. Performance highly sensitive to visual prompt types and budgets. 3. Few-shot learning offers gains but SAM3 lacks robustness against visual prompt noise. 4. Significant gap persists between prompt-based usage and task-trained adapter-based reference.

Conclusion: SAM3 has clear boundaries in pathology image segmentation, demonstrating the necessity of pathology domain adaptation rather than relying solely on prompt-based approaches for medical imaging tasks.

Abstract: Is Segment Anything Model 3 (SAM3) capable in segmenting Any Pathology Images? Digital pathology segmentation spans tissue-level and nuclei-level scales, where traditional methods often suffer from high annotation costs and poor generalization. SAM3 introduces Promptable Concept Segmentation, offering a potential automated interface via text prompts. With this work, we propose a systematic evaluation protocol to explore the capability space of SAM3 in a structured manner. Specifically, we evaluate SAM3 under different supervision settings including zero-shot, few-shot, and supervised with varying prompting strategies. Our extensive evaluation on pathological datasets including NuInsSeg, PanNuke and GlaS, reveals that: 1.text-only prompts poorly activate nuclear concepts. 2.performance is highly sensitive to visual prompt types and budgets. 3.few-shot learning offers gains, but SAM3 lacks robustness against visual prompt noise. and 4.a significant gap persists between prompt-based usage and task-trained adapter-based reference. Our study delineates SAM3's boundaries in pathology image segmentation and provides practical guidance on the necessity of pathology domain adaptation.

</details>


### [224] [Trustworthy Endoscopic Super-Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18001)
*Julio Silva-Rodríguez,Ender Konukoglu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Proposes a framework to make super-resolution systems more trustworthy by predicting reconstruction errors and generating conformal failure masks with theoretical guarantees for safety-critical medical applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Super-resolution models for medical imaging (endoscopic/robotic surgery) can introduce hallucinated structures and amplified noise, raising safety concerns in clinical settings where reliability is critical.

Method: Integrates a lightweight error-prediction network on intermediate representations to estimate pixel-wise reconstruction error, then converts predictions into Conformal Failure Masks (CFM) using conformal risk control principles to localize untrustworthy regions.

Result: Demonstrates effectiveness in detecting unreliable reconstructions in endoscopic and robotic surgery settings for both image and video super-resolution, with computational efficiency suitable for real-time deployment.

Conclusion: First model-agnostic, theoretically grounded approach to improve safety of real-time endoscopic image super-resolution, providing guarantees for error control and failure detection in safety-critical medical applications.

Abstract: Super-resolution (SR) models are attracting growing interest for enhancing minimally invasive surgery and diagnostic videos under hardware constraints. However, valid concerns remain regarding the introduction of hallucinated structures and amplified noise, limiting their reliability in safety-critical settings. We propose a direct and practical framework to make SR systems more trustworthy by identifying where reconstructions are likely to fail. Our approach integrates a lightweight error-prediction network that operates on intermediate representations to estimate pixel-wise reconstruction error. The module is computationally efficient and low-latency, making it suitable for real-time deployment. We convert these predictions into operational failure decisions by constructing Conformal Failure Masks (CFM), which localize regions where the SR output should not be trusted. Built on conformal risk control principles, our method provides theoretical guarantees for controlling both the tolerated error limit and the miscoverage in detected failures. We evaluate our approach on image and video SR, demonstrating its effectiveness in detecting unreliable reconstructions in endoscopic and robotic surgery settings. To our knowledge, this is the first study to provide a model-agnostic, theoretically grounded approach to improving the safety of real-time endoscopic image SR.

</details>


### [225] [Style-Based Neural Architectures for Real-Time Weather Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18251)
*Hamed Ouattara,Pascal Houssam Salmane,Pierre Duthon,Frédéric Bernardin,Omar Ait Aider*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Three neural network architectures for real-time weather classification from images using style-based approaches, with two models outperforming state-of-the-art and showing strong generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop efficient neural network architectures for real-time classification of weather conditions (sunny, rain, snow, fog) from images by capturing stylistic elements, inspired by recent advances in style transfer techniques.

Method: Three architectures: 1) Multi-PatchGAN (adapted from PatchGANs with multiple patch sizes), 2) Truncated ResNet50 (simplified ResNet50 with first 9 layers selected by evolutionary algorithm), 3) Truncated ResNet50 with Gram Matrix and Attention (computes Gram matrices per layer with attention weighting for optimal style feature extraction).

Result: The last two models (Truncated ResNet50 and its enhanced version with Gram Matrix and Attention) outperform state-of-the-art methods and demonstrate remarkable generalization capability on several public databases.

Conclusion: The proposed architectures are effective for weather detection and also suitable for other appearance-based classification tasks including animal species recognition, texture classification, medical imaging, and industrial defect identification.

Abstract: In this paper, we present three neural network architectures designed for real-time classification of weather conditions (sunny, rain, snow, fog) from images. These models, inspired by recent advances in style transfer, aim to capture the stylistic elements present in images. One model, called "Multi-PatchGAN", is based on PatchGANs used in well-known architectures such as Pix2Pix and CycleGAN, but here adapted with multiple patch sizes for detection tasks. The second model, "Truncated ResNet50", is a simplified version of ResNet50 retaining only its first nine layers. This truncation, determined by an evolutionary algorithm, facilitates the extraction of high-frequency features essential for capturing subtle stylistic details. Finally, we propose "Truncated ResNet50 with Gram Matrix and Attention", which computes Gram matrices for each layer during training and automatically weights them via an attention mechanism, thus optimizing the extraction of the most relevant stylistic expressions for classification. These last two models outperform the state of the art and demonstrate remarkable generalization capability on several public databases. Although developed for weather detection, these architectures are also suitable for other appearance-based classification tasks, such as animal species recognition, texture classification, disease detection in medical imaging, or industrial defect identification.

</details>


### [226] [Multi-View Hierarchical Graph Neural Network for Sketch-Based 3D Shape Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18019)
*Hang Cheng,Muyan He,Mingyu Fan,Chengfeng Xie,Xi Cheng,Long Zeng*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: MV-HGNN: A novel graph neural network framework for sketch-based 3D shape retrieval that addresses weak 3D representation and zero-shot limitations through hierarchical graph processing and CLIP-based semantic alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing SBSR methods have two main limitations: 1) They use simplified aggregation strategies for 3D multi-view features that ignore geometric relationships between views and multi-level details, resulting in weak 3D representation. 2) Traditional methods are constrained by visible category limitations and perform poorly in zero-shot scenarios.

Method: Proposes Multi-View Hierarchical Graph Neural Network (MV-HGNN) with: 1) View-level graph construction capturing adjacent geometric dependencies and cross-view message passing via local graph convolution and global attention. 2) View selector for hierarchical graph coarsening to increase receptive field and reduce redundant view interference. 3) CLIP text embeddings as semantic prototypes to project sketch and 3D features into shared semantic space for category-agnostic alignment. 4) Two-stage training for category-level retrieval and one-stage for zero-shot retrieval under same architecture.

Result: Extensive experiments on two public benchmarks show MV-HGNN outperforms state-of-the-art methods under both category-level and zero-shot settings.

Conclusion: MV-HGNN effectively addresses the core challenges of SBSR by providing more discriminative hierarchical 3D representation through graph-based view processing and enabling zero-shot capability via CLIP-based semantic alignment, demonstrating superior performance across different retrieval scenarios.

Abstract: Sketch-based 3D shape retrieval (SBSR) aims to retrieve 3D shapes that are consistent with the category of the input hand-drawn sketch. The core challenge of this task lies in two aspects: existing methods typically employ simplified aggregation strategies for independently encoded 3D multi-view features, which ignore the geometric relationships between views and multi-level details, resulting in weak 3D representation. Simultaneously, traditional SBSR methods are constrained by visible category limitations, leading to poor performance in zero-shot scenarios. To address these challenges, we propose Multi-View Hierarchical Graph Neural Network (MV-HGNN), a novel framework for SBSR. Specifically, we construct a view-level graph and capture adjacent geometric dependencies and cross-view message passing via local graph convolution and global attention. A view selector is further introduced to perform hierarchical graph coarsening, enabling a progressively larger receptive field for graph convolution and mitigating the interference of redundant views, which leads to more discriminate discriminative hierarchical 3D representation. To enable category agnostic alignment and mitigate overfitting to seen classes, we leverage CLIP text embeddings as semantic prototypes and project both sketch and 3D features into a shared semantic space. We use a two-stage training strategy for category-level retrieval and a one-stage strategy for zero-shot retrieval under the same model architecture. Under both category-level and zero-shot settings, extensive experiments on two public benchmarks demonstrate that MV-HGNN outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

</details>


### [227] [Long-Text-to-Image Generation via Compositional Prompt Decomposition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18258)
*Jen-Yuan Huang,Tong Lin,Yilun Du*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PRISM enables pre-trained text-to-image models to handle long descriptive prompts by decomposing them into components, making independent noise predictions, and merging results via energy-based conjunction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current text-to-image models struggle with detailed descriptive paragraphs because they're trained on concise captions. Existing methods either fine-tune poorly on long prompts or compress inputs, losing fidelity.

Method: PRISM uses a lightweight module to extract constituent representations from long prompts. The T2I model makes independent noise predictions for each component, then merges outputs using energy-based conjunction in a single denoising step.

Result: PRISM achieves comparable performance to models fine-tuned on the same training data and shows superior generalization, outperforming baselines by 7.4% on prompts over 500 tokens in challenging benchmarks.

Conclusion: PRISM provides an effective compositional approach for enabling pre-trained T2I models to process long sequence inputs without compromising fidelity or requiring extensive fine-tuning.

Abstract: While modern text-to-image (T2I) models excel at generating images from intricate prompts, they struggle to capture the key details when the inputs are descriptive paragraphs. This limitation stems from the prevalence of concise captions that shape their training distributions. Existing methods attempt to bridge this gap by either fine-tuning T2I models on long prompts, which generalizes poorly to longer lengths; or by projecting the oversize inputs into normal-prompt space and compromising fidelity. We propose Prompt Refraction for Intricate Scene Modeling (PRISM), a compositional approach that enables pre-trained T2I models to process long sequence inputs. PRISM uses a lightweight module to extract constituent representations from the long prompts. The T2I model makes independent noise predictions for each component, and their outputs are merged into a single denoising step using energy-based conjunction. We evaluate PRISM across a wide range of model architectures, showing comparable performances to models fine-tuned on the same training data. Furthermore, PRISM demonstrates superior generalization, outperforming baseline models by 7.4% on prompts over 500 tokens in a challenging public benchmark.

</details>


### [228] [CFSR: Geometry-Conditioned Shadow Removal via Physical Disentanglement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18032)
*Pan Wang,Yihao Hu,Xiujin Liu,Hang Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CFSR is a physics-constrained shadow removal framework that integrates 3D geometric cues with foundation model semantics to bridge the 2D-3D domain gap, achieving state-of-the-art performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional shadow removal networks lack physical interpretability and struggle to balance localized texture recovery with global illumination consistency, treating restoration as unconstrained mapping without physical constraints.

Method: Proposes CFSR with: 1) HVI color space mapping to suppress shadow noise and fuse RGB with depth priors; 2) Geometric & Semantic Dual Explicit Guided Attention using DINO features and 3D normals to modulate attention affinity matrix; 3) Holistic priors injection via frozen CLIP encoder; 4) Frequency Collaborative Reconstruction Module (FCRM) that decouples decoding to harmonize high-frequency boundaries with low-frequency illumination.

Result: Extensive experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance across multiple challenging benchmarks.

Conclusion: CFSR successfully reframes shadow removal as a physics-constrained restoration process by integrating 3D geometric cues with foundation model semantics, effectively bridging the 2D-3D domain gap and achieving optimal balance between texture recovery and illumination consistency.

Abstract: Traditional shadow removal networks often treat image restoration as an unconstrained mapping, lacking the physical interpretability required to balance localized texture recovery with global illumination consistency. To address this, we propose CFSR, a multi-modal prior-driven framework that reframes shadow removal as a physics-constrained restoration process. By seamlessly integrating 3D geometric cues with large-scale foundation model semantics, CFSR effectively bridges the 2D-3D domain gap. Specifically, we first map observations into a custom HVI color space to suppress shadow-induced noise and robustly fuse RGB data with estimated depth priors. At its core, our Geometric & Semantic Dual Explicit Guided Attention mechanism utilizes DINO features and 3D surface normals to directly modulate the attention affinity matrix, structurally enforcing physical lighting constraints. To recover severely degraded regions, we inject holistic priors via a frozen CLIP encoder. Finally, our Frequency Collaborative Reconstruction Module (FCRM) achieves an optimal synthesis by decoupling the decoding process. Conditioned on geometric priors, FCRM seamlessly harmonizes the reconstruction of sharp high-frequency occlusion boundaries with the restoration of low-frequency global illumination. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CFSR achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple challenging benchmarks.

</details>


### [229] [EVE: Verifiable Self-Evolution of MLLMs via Executable Visual Transformations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18320)
*Yongrui Heng,Chaoya Jiang,Han Yang,Shikun Zhang,Wei Ye*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EVE introduces a self-evolution framework for MLLMs that uses executable visual transformations instead of pseudo-labels, with a Challenger-Solver dual-policy architecture that generates diverse, progressively challenging VQA problems with verified ground-truth answers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing self-evolution methods for MLLMs have limitations: pseudo-label-based approaches suffer from quality degradation due to prediction drift, while template-based methods are restricted to static transformations that cannot adapt in difficulty or diversity. Robust continuous self-improvement requires deterministic external feedback and mechanisms to perpetually diversify training distributions.

Method: EVE uses a Challenger-Solver dual-policy architecture. The Challenger maintains and expands a queue of visual transformation code examples, synthesizing novel Python scripts for dynamic visual transformations. Executing these scripts creates VQA problems with execution-verified ground-truth answers. A multi-dimensional reward system with semantic diversity and dynamic difficulty calibration guides the Challenger to enrich its code queue while posing progressively challenging tasks.

Result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that EVE consistently surpasses existing self-evolution methods, establishing a robust and scalable paradigm for verifiable MLLM self-evolution.

Conclusion: EVE provides a novel framework that bypasses pseudo-labels entirely by using executable visual transformations continuously enriched in variety and complexity, enabling robust and continuous self-improvement of MLLMs through verifiable, execution-based supervision.

Abstract: Self-evolution of multimodal large language models (MLLMs) remains a critical challenge: pseudo-label-based methods suffer from progressive quality degradation as model predictions drift, while template-based methods are confined to a static set of transformations that cannot adapt in difficulty or diversity. We contend that robust, continuous self-improvement requires not only deterministic external feedback independent of the model's internal certainty, but also a mechanism to perpetually diversify the training distribution. To this end, we introduce EVE (Executable Visual transformation-based self-Evolution), a novel framework that entirely bypasses pseudo-labels by harnessing executable visual transformations continuously enriched in both variety and complexity. EVE adopts a Challenger-Solver dual-policy architecture. The Challenger maintains and progressively expands a queue of visual transformation code examples, from which it synthesizes novel Python scripts to perform dynamic visual transformations. Executing these scripts yields VQA problems with absolute, execution-verified ground-truth answers, eliminating any reliance on model-generated supervision. A multi-dimensional reward system integrating semantic diversity and dynamic difficulty calibration steers the Challenger to enrich its code example queue while posing progressively more challenging tasks, preventing mode collapse and fostering reciprocal co-evolution between the two policies. Extensive experiments demonstrate that EVE consistently surpasses existing self-evolution methods, establishing a robust and scalable paradigm for verifiable MLLM self-evolution. The code is available at https://github.com/0001Henry/EVE .

</details>


### [230] [HABIT: Chrono-Synergia Robust Progressive Learning Framework for Composed Image Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18037)
*Zixu Li,Yupeng Hu,Zhiwei Chen,Shiqi Zhang,Qinlei Huang,Zhiheng Fu,Yinwei Wei*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HABIT is a robust progressive learning framework for composed image retrieval that addresses the Noise Triplet Correspondence problem through mutual knowledge estimation and dual-consistency progressive learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Composed Image Retrieval faces practical challenges due to the Noise Triplet Correspondence problem caused by expensive and subjective annotation of triplet data, leading to noisy training samples.

Method: Proposes HABIT with two core modules: 1) Mutual Knowledge Estimation Module that quantifies sample cleanliness via Transition Rate of mutual information, and 2) Dual-consistency Progressive Learning Module that uses historical-current model collaboration to simulate habit formation for robust learning.

Result: Extensive experiments on two standard CIR datasets show HABIT significantly outperforms most methods under various noise ratios, demonstrating superior robustness and retrieval performance.

Conclusion: HABIT effectively addresses the NTC problem in CIR through noise-aware sample selection and progressive learning, providing a robust solution for practical applications with noisy training data.

Abstract: Composed Image Retrieval (CIR) is a flexible image retrieval paradigm that enables users to accurately locate the target image through a multimodal query composed of a reference image and modification text. Although this task has demonstrated promising applications in personalized search and recommendation systems, it encounters a severe challenge in practical scenarios known as the Noise Triplet Correspondence (NTC) problem. This issue primarily arises from the high cost and subjectivity involved in annotating triplet data. To address this problem, we identify two central challenges: the precise estimation of composed semantic discrepancy and the insufficient progressive adaptation to modification discrepancy. To tackle these challenges, we propose a cHrono-synergiA roBust progressIve learning framework for composed image reTrieval (HABIT), which consists of two core modules. First, the Mutual Knowledge Estimation Module quantifies sample cleanliness by calculating the Transition Rate of mutual information between the composed feature and the target image, thereby effectively identifying clean samples that align with the intended modification semantics. Second, the Dual-consistency Progressive Learning Module introduces a collaborative mechanism between the historical and current models, simulating human habit formation to retain good habits and calibrate bad habits, ultimately enabling robust learning under the presence of NTC. Extensive experiments conducted on two standard CIR datasets demonstrate that HABIT significantly outperforms most methods under various noise ratios, exhibiting superior robustness and retrieval performance. Codes are available at https://github.com/Lee-zixu/HABIT

</details>


### [231] [AdaCluster: Adaptive Query-Key Clustering for Sparse Attention in Video Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18348)
*Haoyue Tan,Shengnan Wang,Yulin Qiao,Juncheng Zhang,Youhui Bai,Ping Gong,Zewen Jin,Cheng Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AdaCluster accelerates video diffusion transformers via adaptive clustering of attention tokens, achieving 1.67-4.31x speedup with minimal quality loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Video diffusion transformers have prohibitive inference latency due to quadratic attention complexity. Existing sparse attention methods either ignore semantic similarity or fail to adapt to heterogeneous token distributions across layers, causing performance degradation.

Method: AdaCluster is a training-free adaptive clustering framework that uses angle-similarity-preserving clustering for queries and euclidean-similarity-preserving clustering for keys. It includes cluster number assignment, threshold-wise adaptive clustering, and efficient critical cluster selection.

Result: Experiments on CogVideoX-2B, HunyuanVideo, and Wan-2.1 on one A40 GPU show up to 1.67-4.31x speedup with negligible quality degradation.

Conclusion: AdaCluster effectively accelerates video diffusion transformer generation while preserving accuracy through adaptive clustering techniques.

Abstract: Video diffusion transformers (DiTs) suffer from prohibitive inference latency due to quadratic attention complexity. Existing sparse attention methods either overlook semantic similarity or fail to adapt to heterogeneous token distributions across layers, leading to model performance degradation. We propose AdaCluster, a training-free adaptive clustering framework that accelerates the generation of DiTs while preserving accuracy. AdaCluster applies an angle-similarity-preserving clustering method to query vectors for higher compression, and designs a euclidean-similarity-preserving clustering method for keys, covering cluster number assignment, threshold-wise adaptive clustering, and efficient critical cluster selection. Experiments on CogVideoX-2B, HunyuanVideo, and Wan-2.1 on one A40 GPU demonstrate up to 1.67-4.31x speedup with negligible quality degradation.

</details>


### [232] [GS-STVSR: Ultra-Efficient Continuous Spatio-Temporal Video Super-Resolution via 2D Gaussian Splatting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18047)
*Mingyu Shi,Xin Di,Long Peng,Boxiang Cao,Anran Wu,Zhanfeng Feng,Jiaming Guo,Renjing Pei,Xueyang Fu,Yang Cao,Zhengjun Zha*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: GS-STVSR: Ultra-efficient continuous spatio-temporal video super-resolution using 2D Gaussian Splatting instead of dense grid queries, achieving SOTA quality with constant inference time and 3× speedup at extreme scales.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing C-STVSR methods based on Implicit Neural Representations (INR) require dense pixel-wise grid queries, causing computational costs to scale linearly with interpolated frames and severely limiting inference efficiency, especially at high temporal scales.

Method: Proposes GS-STVSR using 2D Gaussian Splatting with continuous motion modeling: 1) exploits temporal stability of covariance for lightweight fitting, 2) optical flow-guided motion module for arbitrary time steps, 3) covariance resampling alignment to prevent drift, 4) adaptive offset window for large-scale motion.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art quality on Vid4, GoPro, and Adobe240 benchmarks. Inference time remains nearly constant at conventional scales (X2-X8) and delivers over 3× speedup at extreme scale X32 compared to previous methods.

Conclusion: GS-STVSR provides an ultra-efficient C-STVSR framework that bypasses dense grid queries entirely, achieving both superior quality and practical efficiency with constant inference time across temporal scales, demonstrating strong real-world applicability.

Abstract: Continuous Spatio-Temporal Video Super-Resolution (C-STVSR) aims to simultaneously enhance the spatial resolution and frame rate of videos by arbitrary scale factors, offering greater flexibility than fixed-scale methods that are constrained by predefined upsampling ratios. In recent years, methods based on Implicit Neural Representations (INR) have made significant progress in C-STVSR by learning continuous mappings from spatio-temporal coordinates to pixel values. However, these methods fundamentally rely on dense pixel-wise grid queries, causing computational cost to scale linearly with the number of interpolated frames and severely limiting inference efficiency. We propose GS-STVSR, an ultra-efficient C-STVSR framework based on 2D Gaussian Splatting (2D-GS) that drives the spatiotemporal evolution of Gaussian kernels through continuous motion modeling, bypassing dense grid queries entirely. We exploit the strong temporal stability of covariance parameters for lightweight intermediate fitting, design an optical flow-guided motion module to derive Gaussian position and color at arbitrary time steps, introduce a Covariance resampling alignment module to prevent covariance drift, and propose an adaptive offset window for large-scale motion. Extensive experiments on Vid4, GoPro, and Adobe240 show that GS-STVSR achieves state-of-the-art quality across all benchmarks. Moreover, its inference time remains nearly constant at conventional temporal scales (X2--X8) and delivers over X3 speedup at extreme scales X32, demonstrating strong practical applicability.

</details>


### [233] [Revisiting Change VQA in Remote Sensing with Structured and Native Multimodal Qwen Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18429)
*Yakoub Bazi,Mohamad M. Al Rahhal,Mansour Zuair,Faroun Mohamed*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Recent VLMs (Qwen3-VL and Qwen3.5) outperform earlier specialized baselines on Change VQA for remote sensing imagery, with native multimodal models being more effective than structured vision-language pipelines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Change VQA (answering questions about semantic changes between bi-temporal remote sensing images) remains underexplored in modern multimodal models, despite recent advances in vision-language models for temporal RS image understanding.

Method: Revisiting the CDVQA benchmark using recent Qwen models under unified LoRA setting: comparing Qwen3-VL (structured vision-language pipeline with multi-depth visual conditioning and full-attention decoder) vs Qwen3.5 (native multimodal model with single-stage alignment and hybrid decoder backbone).

Result: Recent VLMs improve over earlier specialized baselines on CDVQA test splits; performance doesn't scale monotonically with model size; native multimodal models are more effective than structured vision-language pipelines for this task.

Conclusion: Tightly integrated multimodal backbones contribute more to performance than scale or explicit multi-depth visual conditioning for language-driven semantic change reasoning in RS imagery.

Abstract: Change visual question answering (Change VQA) addresses the problem of answering natural-language questions about semantic changes between bi-temporal remote sensing (RS) images. Although vision-language models (VLMs) have recently been studied for temporal RS image understanding, Change VQA remains underexplored in the context of modern multimodal models. In this letter, we revisit the CDVQA benchmark using recent Qwen models under a unified low-rank adaptation (LoRA) setting. We compare Qwen3-VL, which follows a structured vision-language pipeline with multi-depth visual conditioning and a full-attention decoder, with Qwen3.5, a native multimodal model that combines a single-stage alignment with a hybrid decoder backbone. Experimental results on the official CDVQA test splits show that recent VLMs improve over earlier specialized baselines. They further show that performance does not scale monotonically with model size, and that native multimodal models are more effective than structured vision-language pipelines for this task. These findings indicate that tightly integrated multimodal backbones contribute more to performance than scale or explicit multi-depth visual conditioning for language-driven semantic change reasoning in RS imagery.

</details>


### [234] [INTENT: Invariance and Discrimination-aware Noise Mitigation for Robust Composed Image Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18051)
*Zhiwei Chen,Yupeng Hu,Zhiheng Fu,Zixu Li,Jiale Huang,Qinlei Huang,Yinwei Wei*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: INTENT addresses noisy triplet correspondence in composed image retrieval by handling both cross-modal correspondence noise and modality-inherent noise through visual invariant composition and bi-objective discriminative learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world CIR datasets contain annotation errors due to high triplet annotation costs, leading to incorrectly matched triplets. Existing methods assume perfect matches, but noise exists in two forms: cross-modal correspondence noise (mismatches across modalities) and modality-inherent noise (intra-modal background interference).

Method: INTENT has two components: 1) Visual Invariant Composition applies causal intervention via FFT to generate intervened composed features, enforcing visual invariance to ignore modality-inherent noise; 2) Bi-Objective Discriminative Learning uses collaborative optimization with positive/negative samples and constructs scalable decision boundaries based on loyalty degree for robust correspondence discrimination.

Result: Extensive experiments on two widely used benchmark datasets demonstrate the superiority and robustness of INTENT in handling noisy triplet correspondence in composed image retrieval.

Conclusion: INTENT effectively addresses both types of noise in CIR datasets through its dual-component approach, providing a robust solution for noisy triplet correspondence that outperforms existing methods.

Abstract: Composed Image Retrieval (CIR) is a challenging image retrieval paradigm that enables to retrieve target images based on multimodal queries consisting of reference images and modification texts. Although substantial progress has been made in recent years, existing methods assume that all samples are correctly matched. However, in real-world scenarios, due to high triplet annotation costs, CIR datasets inevitably contain annotation errors, resulting in incorrectly matched triplets. To address this issue, the problem of Noisy Triplet Correspondence (NTC) has attracted growing attention. We argue that noise in CIR can be categorized into two types: cross-modal correspondence noise and modality-inherent noise. The former arises from mismatches across modalities, whereas the latter originates from intra-modal background interference or visual factors irrelevant to the coarse-grained modification annotations. However, modality-inherent noise is often overlooked, and research on cross-modal correspondence noise remains nascent. To tackle above issues, we propose the Invariance and discrimiNaTion-awarE Noise neTwork (INTENT), comprising two components: Visual Invariant Composition and Bi-Objective Discriminative Learning, specifically designed to handle the two-aspect noise. The former applies causal intervention on the visual side via Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) to generate intervened composed features, enforcing visual invariance and enabling the model to ignore modality-inherent noise during composition. The latter adopts collaborative optimization with both positive and negative samples, and constructs a scalable decision boundary that dynamically adjusts decisions based on the loyalty degree, enabling robust correspondence discrimination. Extensive experiments on two widely used benchmark datasets demonstrate the superiority and robustness of INTENT.

</details>


### [235] [Progressive Online Video Understanding with Evidence-Aligned Timing and Transparent Decisions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18459)
*Kecheng Zhang,Zongxin Yang,Mingfei Han,Haihong Hao,Yunzhi Zhuge,Changlin Li,Junhan Zhao,Zhihui Li,Xiaojun Chang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A framework for online video understanding that decouples reasoning control from memory integration, enabling transparent decision-making and evidence-aligned responses in streaming video scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Visual agents need to respond precisely when sufficient evidence first appears in video streams, but conventional video LLMs operate offline and lack transparency, proper timing alignment, and efficient global understanding under computational constraints.

Method: Proposes a framework with two components: 1) Active Thinking Decision Maker (ATDM) - transparent reasoning controller using observable progress and confidence metrics to time responses; 2) Hierarchical Progressive Semantic Integration (HPSI) - efficient memory system with learnable multi-level aggregation tokens propagated across clips.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance with 71.6% on StreamingBench (improving from 67.63%) and 46.9% on OVOBench, demonstrating effective evidence-aligned and transparent online video analysis.

Conclusion: The framework successfully addresses challenges of online video understanding by providing transparent decision-making, precise timing alignment with visual evidence, and efficient global memory integration under computational constraints.

Abstract: Visual agents operating in the wild must respond to queries precisely when sufficient evidence first appears in a video stream, a critical capability that is overlooked by conventional video LLMs evaluated in offline settings. The shift to an online, streaming paradigm introduces significant challenges: a lack of decision transparency, the difficulty of aligning response timing with visual evidence, and the need to maintain a global, causally consistent understanding under tight computational budgets. To address these issues, we propose a novel framework that decouples reasoning control from memory integration. We introduce \textbf{\model{}}, an instantiation of this framework with two core components. First, the \emph{Active Thinking Decision Maker (ATDM)} is a transparent reasoning controller that externalizes its decision process using observable progress ($\boldsymbolρ$) and confidence ($\boldsymbol{c}$) metrics. This allows it to precisely time its response $t_r$ to match the first-sufficient-evidence timestamp $t^\star$ while streaming its reasoning to the user. Second, the \emph{Hierarchical Progressive Semantic Integration (HPSI)} module acts as an efficient memory system. It employs a set of learnable, multi-level aggregation tokens that are propagated across clips to build a rich, global cognitive state without exceeding token budgets. %Our approach sets a new standard on key online video understanding benchmarks, achieving strong performance of \textbf{71.6\%} on StreamingBench and \textbf{46.9\%} on OVOBench, demonstrating a robust solution for evidence-aligned and transparent online video analysis. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of ATDM and HPSI, e.g., Thinking-QwenVL improves the accuracy of the previous state-of-the-art from 67.63\% to 71.60\% on the StreamingBench benchmark.

</details>


### [236] [Enhancing Continual Learning of Vision-Language Models via Dynamic Prefix Weighting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18075)
*Hyeonseo Jang,Hyuk Kwon,Kibok Lee*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DPW is a dynamic prefix weighting framework for VLMs that adaptively adjusts prefix weights based on token importance, achieving SOTA in domain-class incremental learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Previous parameter-efficient methods for VLMs use fixed normalization of prefix weights, ignoring that different input tokens require different degrees of adjustment, limiting adaptation effectiveness.

Method: DPW uses 1) a gating module to dynamically weight prefixes based on token importance, and 2) a weighting mechanism that derives adapter output weights as residuals of prefix-tuning weights, ensuring adapters are used only when necessary.

Result: Experimental results show DPW achieves state-of-the-art performance in domain-class incremental learning scenarios for vision-language models.

Conclusion: DPW's dynamic weighting approach effectively addresses the limitations of fixed prefix normalization, enabling better adaptation to downstream tasks in incremental learning settings.

Abstract: We investigate recently introduced domain-class incremental learning scenarios for vision-language models (VLMs). Recent works address this challenge using parameter-efficient methods, such as prefix-tuning or adapters, which facilitate model adaptation to downstream tasks by incorporating task-specific information into input tokens through additive vectors. However, previous approaches often normalize the weights of these vectors, disregarding the fact that different input tokens require different degrees of adjustment. To overcome this issue, we propose Dynamic Prefix Weighting (DPW), a framework that dynamically assigns weights to prefixes, complemented by adapters. DPW consists of 1) a gating module that adjusts the weights of each prefix based on the importance of the corresponding input token, and 2) a weighting mechanism that derives adapter output weights as a residual of prefix-tuning weights, ensuring that adapters are utilized only when necessary. Experimental results demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance in domain-class incremental learning scenarios for VLMs. The code is available at: https://github.com/YonseiML/dpw.

</details>


### [237] [Asset Harvester: Extracting 3D Assets from Autonomous Driving Logs for Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18468)
*Tianshi Cao,Jiawei Ren,Yuxuan Zhang,Jaewoo Seo,Jiahui Huang,Shikhar Solanki,Haotian Zhang,Mingfei Guo,Haithem Turki,Muxingzi Li,Yue Zhu,Sipeng Zhang,Zan Gojcic,Sanja Fidler,Kangxue Yin*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Asset Harvester converts sparse real-world driving data into complete 3D assets for autonomous vehicle simulation through a system combining data curation, geometry-aware preprocessing, sparse-view generation, and 3D Gaussian lifting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Neural scene reconstruction for AV simulation creates interactive 3D environments but lacks complete 3D object assets needed for agent manipulation and large-viewpoint novel-view synthesis from real driving logs.

Method: End-to-end pipeline with system-level design: large-scale curation of object-centric training tuples, geometry-aware preprocessing across heterogeneous sensors, sparse-view-conditioned multiview generation (SparseViewDiT), 3D Gaussian lifting, hybrid data curation, augmentation, and self-distillation.

Result: Enables scalable conversion of sparse AV object observations into reusable, simulation-ready 3D assets that support agent manipulation and large-viewpoint novel-view synthesis.

Conclusion: Asset Harvester addresses the gap in 3D object asset creation from real-world driving data, providing a complete pipeline for generating simulation-ready assets that enhance AV development through scalable testing and validation.

Abstract: Closed-loop simulation is a core component of autonomous vehicle (AV) development, enabling scalable testing, training, and safety validation before real-world deployment. Neural scene reconstruction converts driving logs into interactive 3D environments for simulation, but it does not produce complete 3D object assets required for agent manipulation and large-viewpoint novel-view synthesis. To address this challenge, we present Asset Harvester, an image-to-3D model and end-to-end pipeline that converts sparse, in-the-wild object observations from real driving logs into complete, simulation-ready assets. Rather than relying on a single model component, we developed a system-level design for real-world AV data that combines large-scale curation of object-centric training tuples, geometry-aware preprocessing across heterogeneous sensors, and a robust training recipe that couples sparse-view-conditioned multiview generation with 3D Gaussian lifting. Within this system, SparseViewDiT is explicitly designed to address limited-angle views and other real-world data challenges. Together with hybrid data curation, augmentation, and self-distillation, this system enables scalable conversion of sparse AV object observations into reusable 3D assets.

</details>


### [238] [Back into Plato's Cave: Examining Cross-modal Representational Convergence at Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18572)
*A. Sophia Koepke,Daniil Zverev,Shiry Ginosar,Alexei A. Efros*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper challenges the Platonic Representation Hypothesis, showing cross-modal alignment is fragile, degrades with scale, and reflects coarse semantics rather than fine-grained structure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To critically examine the evidence for the Platonic Representation Hypothesis, which claims neural networks trained on different modalities converge to the same representation of reality, and to test whether modality choice truly matters.

Method: Analyzed cross-modal alignment using mutual nearest neighbors, scaled evaluations from small datasets (~1K samples) to millions of samples, examined one-to-one vs. many-to-many settings, and tested alignment trends across newer models.

Result: Alignment degrades substantially with dataset scaling, remaining alignment reflects coarse semantic overlap rather than fine-grained structure, one-to-one constraint breaks down in realistic many-to-many settings, and reported alignment trends don't hold for newer models.

Conclusion: Current evidence for cross-modal representational convergence is weaker than previously thought; models trained on different modalities may learn equally rich but different representations of the world.

Abstract: The Platonic Representation Hypothesis suggests that neural networks trained on different modalities (e.g., text and images) align and eventually converge toward the same representation of reality. If true, this has significant implications for whether modality choice matters at all. We show that the experimental evidence for this hypothesis is fragile and depends critically on the evaluation regime. Alignment is measured using mutual nearest neighbors on small datasets ($\approx$1K samples) and degrades substantially as the dataset is scaled to millions of samples. The alignment that remains between model representations reflects coarse semantic overlap rather than consistent fine-grained structure. Moreover, the evaluations in Huh et al. are done in a one-to-one image-caption setting, a constraint that breaks down in realistic many-to-many settings and further reduces alignment. We also find that the reported trend of stronger language models increasingly aligning with vision does not appear to hold for newer models. Overall, our findings suggest that the current evidence for cross-modal representational convergence is considerably weaker than subsequent works have taken it to be. Models trained on different modalities may learn equally rich representations of the world, just not the same one.

</details>


### [239] [Decision-Aware Attention Propagation for Vision Transformer Explainability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18094)
*Sehyeong Jo,Gangjae Jang,Haesol Park*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DAP (Decision-Aware Attention Propagation) improves Vision Transformer interpretability by combining gradient-based token importance with attention propagation for more class-sensitive and faithful attribution maps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current ViT explanation methods have limitations: attention-based methods lack class discriminability and don't reflect final decisions, while gradient-based methods don't fully exploit transformers' hierarchical attention propagation mechanism.

Method: DAP integrates decision-relevant priors into attention propagation by estimating token importance through gradient-based localization and injecting it into layer-wise attention rollout, capturing both attention flow and prediction-relevant evidence.

Result: DAP consistently outperforms existing baselines across ViT variants of different scales in both quantitative metrics and qualitative visualizations, producing more class-sensitive, compact, and faithful attribution maps.

Conclusion: Decision-aware propagation is an effective direction for improving ViT interpretability, successfully addressing limitations of both attention-based and gradient-based explanation methods.

Abstract: Vision Transformers (ViTs) have become a dominant architecture in computer vision, yet their prediction process remains difficult to interpret because information is propagated through complex interactions across layers and attention heads. Existing attention based explanation methods provide an intuitive way to trace information flow. However, they rely mainly on raw attention weights, which do not explicitly reflect the final decision and often lead to explanations with limited class discriminability. In contrast, gradient based localization methods are more effective at highlighting class specific evidence, but they do not fully exploit the hierarchical attention propagation mechanism of transformers. To address this limitation, we propose Decision-Aware Attention Propagation (DAP), an attribution method that injects decision-relevant priors into transformer attention propagation. By estimating token importance through gradient based localization and integrating it into layer wise attention rollout, the method captures both the structural flow of attention and the evidence most relevant to the final prediction. Consequently, DAP produces attribution maps that are more class sensitive, compact, and faithful than those generated by conventional attention based methods. Extensive experiments across Vision Transformer variants of different model scales show that DAP consistently outperforms existing baselines in both quantitative metrics and qualitative visualizations, indicating that decision aware propagation is an effective direction for improving ViT interpretability.

</details>


### [240] [Test-Time Perturbation Learning with Delayed Feedback for Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18107)
*Zehua Zang,Xi Wang,Fuchun Sun,Xiao Xu,Lixiang Lium,Jiahuan Zhou,Jiangmeng Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: PDF is a verifier-free test-time adaptation framework that improves Vision-Language-Action model robustness to environmental shifts through uncertainty-based data augmentation, action voting, and delayed feedback correction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: VLAs achieve strong performance but are fragile to subtle environmental changes due to trajectory overfitting, where they over-attend to spurious correlations between actions and entities and reproduce memorized action patterns.

Method: PDF uses uncertainty-based data augmentation and action voting to mitigate spurious correlations, an adaptive scheduler for budget allocation, and learns a lightweight perturbation module that retrospectively adjusts action logits using delayed feedback to correct overconfidence.

Result: Experiments show consistent gains: +7.4% success rate on LIBERO and +10.3 human normalized score on Atari, outperforming vanilla VLA and VLA with test-time adaptation.

Conclusion: PDF establishes a practical path toward reliable test-time adaptation in multimodal decision-making agents without fine-tuning the base model, improving robustness to environmental shifts.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action models (VLAs) achieve remarkable performance in sequential decision-making but remain fragile to subtle environmental shifts, such as small changes in object pose. We attribute this brittleness to trajectory overfitting, where VLAs over-attend to the spurious correlation between actions and entities, then reproduce memorized action patterns. We propose Perturbation learning with Delayed Feedback (PDF), a verifier-free test-time adaptation framework that improves decision performance without fine-tuning the base model. PDF mitigates the spurious correlation through uncertainty-based data augmentation and action voting, while an adaptive scheduler allocates augmentation budgets to balance performance and efficiency. To further improve stability, PDF learns a lightweight perturbation module that retrospectively adjusts action logits guided by delayed feedback, correcting overconfidence issue. Experiments on LIBERO (+7.4\% success rate) and Atari (+10.3 human normalized score) demonstrate consistent gains of PDF in task success over vanilla VLA and VLA with test-time adaptation, establishing a practical path toward reliable test-time adaptation in multimodal decision-making agents. The code is available at \href{https://github.com/zhoujiahuan1991/CVPR2026-PDF}{https://github.com/zhoujiahuan1991/CVPR2026-PDF}.

</details>


### [241] [Can LLM-Generated Text Empower Surgical Vision-Language Pre-training?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18134)
*Chengan Che,Chao Wang,Jiayuan Huang,Xinyue Chen,Luis C. Garcia-Peraza-Herrera*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SurgLIME is a parameter-efficient vision-language pre-training framework that uses LLM-generated surgical narratives to enable scalable multi-modal learning while mitigating noise through confidence-weighted contrastive alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extending surgical vision encoders to multi-modal reasoning is bottlenecked by expensive expert textual annotations. LLM-generated narratives offer scalability but introduce noise and hallucinations that could degrade medical priors in standard contrastive learning pipelines.

Method: Proposes SurgLIME framework with: 1) LoRA-adapted dual-encoder architecture to preserve foundational medical priors, 2) Automated confidence estimation mechanism that dynamically down-weights uncertain text during contrastive alignment, 3) Uses LIME dataset with LLM-generated surgical narratives.

Result: Achieves competitive zero-shot cross-modal alignment on AutoLaparo and Cholec80 benchmarks while preserving robust linear probing performance of the visual foundation model.

Conclusion: SurgLIME enables scalable multi-modal surgical AI by leveraging noisy LLM-generated narratives while protecting against degradation of medical priors through parameter-efficient architecture and confidence-weighted alignment.

Abstract: Recent advancements in self-supervised learning have led to powerful surgical vision encoders capable of spatiotemporal understanding. However, extending these visual foundations to multi-modal reasoning tasks is severely bottlenecked by the prohibitive cost of expert textual annotations. To overcome this scalability limitation, we introduce \textbf{LIME}, a large-scale multi-modal dataset derived from open-access surgical videos using human-free, Large Language Model (LLM)-generated narratives. While LIME offers immense scalability, unverified generated texts may contain errors, including hallucinations, that could potentially lead to catastrophically degraded pre-trained medical priors in standard contrastive pipelines. To mitigate this, we propose \textbf{SurgLIME}, a parameter-efficient Vision-Language Pre-training (VLP) framework designed to learn reliable cross-modal alignments using noisy narratives. SurgLIME preserves foundational medical priors using a LoRA-adapted dual-encoder architecture and introduces an automated confidence estimation mechanism that dynamically down-weights uncertain text during contrastive alignment. Evaluations on the AutoLaparo and Cholec80 benchmarks show that SurgLIME achieves competitive zero-shot cross-modal alignment while preserving the robust linear probing performance of the visual foundation model. Dataset, code, and models are publicly available at \href{https://github.com/visurg-ai/SurgLIME}{https://github.com/visurg-ai/SurgLIME}.

</details>


### [242] [Attention-ResUNet for Automated Fetal Head Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18148)
*Ammar Bhilwarawala,Mainak Bandyopadhyay*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Attention-ResUNet combines residual learning with multi-scale attention mechanisms to achieve state-of-the-art fetal head segmentation in ultrasound images, outperforming five baseline models with 99.30% Dice score.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automated fetal head segmentation in ultrasound images is critical for accurate biometric measurements, but existing deep learning approaches struggle with low contrast, noise, and complex anatomical boundaries inherent to ultrasound imaging.

Method: Proposes Attention-ResUNet, a novel architecture that synergistically combines residual learning with multi-scale attention mechanisms. Integrates attention gates at four decoder levels to focus selectively on anatomically relevant regions while suppressing background noise, complemented by residual connections that facilitate gradient flow and feature reuse.

Result: Achieves superior performance with mean Dice score of 99.30% ± 0.14% on HC18 Challenge dataset (n=200), significantly outperforming five baseline architectures: ResUNet (99.26%), Attention U-Net (98.79%), Swin U-Net (98.60%), Standard U-Net (98.58%), and U-Net++ (97.46%). Statistical analysis confirms highly significant improvements (p < 0.001) with effect sizes ranging from 0.230 to 13.159 (Cohen's d).

Conclusion: Attention-ResUNet establishes new state-of-the-art performance for automated fetal head segmentation while maintaining computational efficiency (14.7M parameters, 45 GFLOPs inference cost). Saliency map analysis reveals highly concentrated, anatomically consistent activation patterns, demonstrating enhanced interpretability crucial for clinical deployment.

Abstract: Automated fetal head segmentation in ultrasound images is critical for accurate biometric measurements in prenatal care. While existing deep learning approaches have achieved a reasonable performance, they struggle with issues like low contrast, noise, and complex anatomical boundaries which are inherent to ultrasound imaging. This paper presents Attention-ResUNet. It is a novel architecture that synergistically combines residual learning with multi-scale attention mechanisms in order to achieve enhanced fetal head segmentation. Our approach integrates attention gates at four decoder levels to focus selectively on anatomically relevant regions while suppressing the background noise, and complemented by residual connections which facilitates gradient flow and feature reuse. Extensive evaluation on the HC18 Challenge dataset where n = 200 demonstrates that Attention ResUNet achieves a superior performance with a mean Dice score of 99.30 +/- 0.14% against similar architectures. It significantly outperforms five baseline architectures including ResUNet (99.26%), Attention U-Net (98.79%), Swin U-Net (98.60%), Standard U-Net (98.58%), and U-Net++ (97.46%). Through statistical analysis we confirm highly significant improvements (p < 0.001) with effect sizes that range from 0.230 to 13.159 (Cohen's d). Using Saliency map analysis, we reveal that our architecture produces highly concentrated, anatomically consistent activation patterns, which demonstrate an enhanced interpretability which is crucial for clinical deployment. The proposed method establishes a new state of the art performance for automated fetal head segmentation whilst maintaining computational efficiency with 14.7M parameters and a 45 GFLOPs inference cost. Code repository: https://github.com/Ammar-ss

</details>


### [243] [AI-based Waste Mapping for Addressing Climate-Exacerbated Flood Risk](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18151)
*Steffen Knoblauch,Levi Szamek,Iddy Chazua,Benedcto Adamu,Innocent Maholi,Alexander Zipf*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: AI-powered waste mapping using aerial/street-view imagery detects waste patterns in Dar es Salaam, revealing waste accumulation in waterways is 3x higher than adjacent areas, creating flood risk hotspots.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Urban flooding is increasing in African cities due to climate change, and inadequate waste management blocks drainage systems, amplifying flood risks. Traditional manual mapping methods are limited for city-wide monitoring.

Method: AI-powered urban waste mapping workflow using openly available aerial and street-view imagery to detect municipal solid waste at high resolution. Collaborated with local partners for culturally relevant data labeling reflecting real-world waste reuse practices.

Result: Revealed spatial waste patterns linked to informal settlements and socio-economic factors. Found waste accumulation in waterways was up to three times higher than in adjacent urban areas, identifying critical hotspots for climate-exacerbated flooding.

Conclusion: Scalable AI approach enables city-wide monitoring and prioritization of interventions. Results provide actionable insights for urban planning, climate adaptation, and sustainable waste management in flood-prone urban areas.

Abstract: Urban flooding is a growing climate change-related hazard in rapidly expanding African cities, where inadequate waste management often blocks drainage systems and amplifies flood risks. This study introduces an AI-powered urban waste mapping workflow that leverages openly available aerial and street-view imagery to detect municipal solid waste at high resolution. Applied in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania, our approach reveals spatial waste patterns linked to informal settlements and socio-economic factors. Waste accumulation in waterways was found to be up to three times higher than in adjacent urban areas, highlighting critical hotspots for climate-exacerbated flooding. Unlike traditional manual mapping methods, this scalable AI approach allows city-wide monitoring and prioritization of interventions. Crucially, our collaboration with local partners ensured culturally and contextually relevant data labeling, reflecting real-world reuse practices for solid waste. The results offer actionable insights for urban planning, climate adaptation, and sustainable waste management in flood-prone urban areas.

</details>


### [244] [Embedding Arithmetic: A Lightweight, Tuning-Free Framework for Post-hoc Bias Mitigation in Text-to-Image Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18167)
*Venkatesh Thirugnana Sambandham,Torsten Schön*

Main category: cs.CV
