TL;DR: OGER is a novel RL framework that combines offline teacher guidance with online reinforcement learning using entropy-aware reward modeling to improve LLM reasoning exploration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RLVR methods for LLM reasoning struggle with exploring novel trajectories beyond initial latent space, and existing solutions like offline teacher guidance or entropy-driven strategies lack deep integration or are constrained by model capacity.

Method: OGER unifies offline teacher guidance and online RL through specialized reward modeling, employing multi-teacher collaborative training and constructing an auxiliary exploration reward that leverages both offline trajectories and model entropy to incentivize autonomous exploration.

Result: OGER significantly outperforms competitive baselines across mathematical and general reasoning benchmarks, achieving substantial gains in mathematical reasoning while maintaining robust generalization to out-of-domain tasks.

Conclusion: The framework successfully addresses exploration limitations in LLM reasoning through integrated offline-online learning with entropy-aware reward modulation, validated by comprehensive analysis and ablation studies.

Abstract: Recent advancements in Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) have significantly improved Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning, yet models often struggle to explore novel trajectories beyond their initial latent space. While offline teacher guidance and entropy-driven strategies have been proposed to address this, they often lack deep integration or are constrained by the model's inherent capacity. In this paper, we propose OGER, a novel framework that unifies offline teacher guidance and online reinforcement learning through a specialized reward modeling lens. OGER employs multi-teacher collaborative training and constructs an auxiliary exploration reward that leverages both offline trajectories and the model's own entropy to incentivize autonomous exploration. Extensive experiments across mathematical and general reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that OGER significantly outperforms competitive baselines, achieving substantial gains in mathematical reasoning while maintaining robust generalization to out-of-domain tasks. We provide a comprehensive analysis of training dynamics and conduct detailed ablation studies to validate the effectiveness of our entropy-aware reward modulation. Our code is available at https://github.com/ecoli-hit/OGER.git.

</details>


### [407] [ClawEnvKit: Automatic Environment Generation for Claw-Like Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18543)
*Xirui Li,Ming Li,Derry Xu,Wei-Lin Chiang,Ion Stoica,Cho-Jui Hsieh,Tianyi Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ClawEnvKit: Automated pipeline for generating diverse, verified claw-like agent environments from natural language descriptions, enabling large-scale benchmarking and on-demand training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Manual environment creation for claw-like agents is human-intensive and doesn't scale; need automated pipeline for diverse, verified environments on demand.

Method: Three-module pipeline: (1) parser extracts structured parameters from natural language, (2) generator produces task specs/tool interface/scoring config, (3) validator enforces feasibility, diversity, validity, and consistency.

Result: Created Auto-ClawEval benchmark with 1,040 environments across 24 categories at 13,800x lower cost than human curation; harness engineering boosts performance up to 15.7 percentage points; no model saturates benchmark.

Conclusion: ClawEnvKit enables scalable evaluation and on-demand training environment generation, turning evaluation into continuous user-driven process and allowing adaptive training based on agent weaknesses.

Abstract: Constructing environments for training and evaluating claw-like agents remains a manual, human-intensive process that does not scale. We argue that what is needed is not just a dataset, but an automated pipeline capable of generating diverse, verified environments on demand. To this end, we introduce ClawEnvKit, an autonomous generation pipeline that instantiates this formalism from natural language descriptions. The pipeline comprises three modules: (1) a parser that extracts structured generation parameters from natural language input; (2) a generator that produces the task specification, tool interface, and scoring configuration; and (3) a validator that enforces feasibility, diversity, structural validity, and internal consistency across the generated environments. Using ClawEnvKit, we construct Auto-ClawEval, the first large-scale benchmark for claw-like agents, comprising 1,040 environments across 24 categories. Empirically, Auto-ClawEval matches or exceeds human-curated environments on coherence and clarity at 13,800x lower cost. Evaluated across 4 model families and 8 agent harness frameworks, we find that harness engineering boosts performance by up to 15.7 percentage points over a bare ReAct baseline, completion remains the primary axis of variation with no model saturating the benchmark, and automated generation enables evaluation at a scale previously infeasible. Beyond static benchmarking, ClawEnvKit enables live evaluation: users describe a desired capability in natural language and obtain a verified environment on demand, turning evaluation into a continuous, user-driven process. The same mechanism serves as an on-demand training environment generator, producing task distributions that adapt to an agent's current weaknesses rather than being bounded by existing user logs.

</details>


### [408] [Benchmarking System Dynamics AI Assistants: Cloud Versus Local LLMs on CLD Extraction and Discussion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18566)
*Terry Leitch*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Systematic evaluation of LLMs on System Dynamics AI benchmarks shows cloud models lead in causal loop diagram extraction (77-89%), while local models match mid-tier cloud performance but struggle with error fixing due to memory limits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To systematically evaluate and compare proprietary cloud APIs and locally-hosted open-source LLMs on System Dynamics AI assistance tasks, providing practical guidance for practitioners working with large models on consumer hardware.

Method: Created two benchmarks: CLD Leaderboard (53 tests for causal loop diagram extraction) and Discussion Leaderboard (interactive model discussion, feedback explanation, coaching). Evaluated cloud and local models across different architectures, backends (GGUF vs MLX), quantization levels, and parameter sweeps.

Result: Cloud models achieved 77-89% pass rates on CLD extraction; best local model matched mid-tier cloud at 77%. Local models performed well on model building (50-100%) and feedback explanation (47-75%) but poorly on error fixing (0-50%). Backend choice (llama.cpp vs mlx_lm) had larger impact than quantization level.

Conclusion: Local models can match mid-tier cloud performance on structured tasks but face limitations with long-context prompts and error fixing. Backend selection is crucial for practical deployment, with llama.cpp offering reliable JSON handling but memory issues on dense models, while mlx_lm requires explicit JSON instructions.

Abstract: We present a systematic evaluation of large language model families -- spanning both proprietary cloud APIs and locally-hosted open-source models -- on two purpose-built benchmarks for System Dynamics AI assistance: the \textbf{CLD Leaderboard} (53 tests, structured causal loop diagram extraction) and the \textbf{Discussion Leaderboard} (interactive model discussion, feedback explanation, and model building coaching).
  On CLD extraction, cloud models achieve 77--89\% overall pass rates; the best local model reaches 77\% (Kimi~K2.5~GGUF~Q3, zero-shot engine), matching mid-tier cloud performance. On Discussion, the best local models achieve 50--100\% on model building steps and 47--75\% on feedback explanation, but only 0--50\% on error fixing -- a category dominated by long-context prompts that expose memory limits in local deployments.
  A central contribution of this paper is a systematic analysis of \textit{model type effects} on performance: we compare reasoning vs.\ instruction-tuned architectures, GGUF (llama.cpp) vs.\ MLX (mlx\_lm) backends, and quantization levels (Q3 / Q4\_K\_M / MLX-3bit / MLX-4bit / MLX-6bit) across the same underlying model families. We find that backend choice has larger practical impact than quantization level: mlx\_lm does not enforce JSON schema constraints, requiring explicit prompt-level JSON instructions, while llama.cpp grammar-constrained sampling handles JSON reliably but causes indefinite generation on long-context prompts for dense models.
  We document the full parameter sweep ($t$, $p$, $k$) for all local models, cleaned timing data (stuck requests excluded), and a practitioner guide for running 671B--123B parameter models on Apple~Silicon.

</details>


### [409] [Agentic Forecasting using Sequential Bayesian Updating of Linguistic Beliefs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18576)
*Kevin Murphy*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BLF is a Bayesian linguistic forecasting system that achieves SOTA on ForecastBench through structured belief states, multi-trial aggregation, and hierarchical calibration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current forecasting systems often append all evidence to growing context windows without structured representation, and need better methods for aggregating multiple trials and calibrating predictions across different data sources.

Method: Three key innovations: (1) Bayesian linguistic belief state combining numerical probabilities with natural-language evidence summaries, (2) hierarchical multi-trial aggregation using logit-space shrinkage with data-dependent prior, (3) hierarchical calibration with Platt scaling and hierarchical prior to avoid over-shrinking extreme predictions.

Result: Outperforms all top public methods on 400 ForecastBench questions, including Cassi, GPT-5, Grok~4.20, and Foresight-32B. Structured belief state is as impactful as web search access, with shrinkage aggregation and hierarchical calibration providing significant additional gains.

Conclusion: BLF demonstrates that structured belief representation combined with Bayesian statistical methods enables state-of-the-art forecasting performance, with the system also featuring robust backtesting framework and rigorous statistical methodology for method comparison.

Abstract: We present BLF (Bayesian Linguistic Forecaster), an agentic system for binary forecasting that achieves state-of-the-art performance on the ForecastBench benchmark. The system is built on three ideas. (1) A Bayesian linguistic belief state: a semi-structured representation combining numerical probability estimates with natural-language evidence summaries, updated by the LLM at each step of an iterative tool-use loop. This contrasts with the common approach of appending all retrieved evidence to an ever-growing context. (2) Hierarchical multi-trial aggregation: running $K$ independent trials and combining them using logit-space shrinkage with a data-dependent prior. (3) Hierarchical calibration: Platt scaling with a hierarchical prior, which avoids over-shrinking extreme predictions for sources with skewed base rates.
  On 400 backtesting questions from the ForecastBench leaderboard, BLF outperforms all the top public methods, including Cassi, GPT-5, Grok~4.20, and Foresight-32B. Ablation studies show that the structured belief state is as impactful as web search access, and that shrinkage aggregation and hierarchical calibration each provide significant additional gains.
  In addition, we develop a robust back-testing framework with a leakage rate below 1.5\%, and use rigorous statistical methodology to compare different methods while controlling for various sources of noise.

</details>


### [410] [MathNet: a Global Multimodal Benchmark for Mathematical Reasoning and Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.18584)
*Shaden Alshammari,Kevin Wen,Abrar Zainal,Mark Hamilton,Navid Safaei,Sultan Albarakati,William T. Freeman,Antonio Torralba*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MathNet is a large-scale multimodal multilingual dataset of Olympiad math problems with benchmark tasks for evaluating mathematical reasoning and retrieval in AI models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks for mathematical problem solving are limited in size, language coverage, and task diversity, making it difficult to properly evaluate AI models' mathematical reasoning capabilities.

Method: Created MathNet dataset spanning 47 countries, 17 languages, and 20 years of competitions with 30,676 expert-authored problems. Constructed retrieval benchmark with mathematically equivalent and structurally similar problem pairs curated by human experts.

Result: State-of-the-art models remain challenged (78.4% for Gemini-3.1-Pro, 69.3% for GPT-5), embedding models struggle with retrieval, and retrieval-augmented generation shows up to 12% gains (DeepSeek-V3.2-Speciale achieves highest scores).

Conclusion: MathNet provides the largest high-quality Olympiad dataset and first benchmark for mathematical problem retrieval, highlighting significant challenges for current AI models in mathematical reasoning and retrieval tasks.

Abstract: Mathematical problem solving remains a challenging test of reasoning for large language and multimodal models, yet existing benchmarks are limited in size, language coverage, and task diversity. We introduce MathNet, a high-quality, large-scale, multimodal, and multilingual dataset of Olympiad-level math problems together with a benchmark for evaluating mathematical reasoning in generative models and mathematical retrieval in embedding-based systems. MathNet spans 47 countries, 17 languages, and two decades of competitions, comprising 30,676 expert-authored problems with solutions across diverse domains. In addition to the core dataset, we construct a retrieval benchmark consisting of mathematically equivalent and structurally similar problem pairs curated by human experts.
  MathNet supports three tasks: (i) Problem Solving, (ii) Math-Aware Retrieval, and (iii) Retrieval-Augmented Problem Solving. Experimental results show that even state-of-the-art reasoning models (78.4% for Gemini-3.1-Pro and 69.3% for GPT-5) remain challenged, while embedding models struggle to retrieve equivalent problems. We further show that retrieval-augmented generation performance is highly sensitive to retrieval quality; for example, DeepSeek-V3.2-Speciale achieves gains of up to 12%, obtaining the highest scores on the benchmark. MathNet provides the largest high-quality Olympiad dataset together with the first benchmark for evaluating mathematical problem retrieval, and we publicly release both the dataset and benchmark at https://mathnet.mit.edu.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [411] [BrainMem: Brain-Inspired Evolving Memory for Embodied Agent Task Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16331)
*Xiaoyu Ma,Lianyu Hu,Wenbing Tang,Zixuan Hu,Zeqin Liao,Zhizhen Wu,Yang Liu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: BrainMem is a training-free hierarchical memory system for embodied AI agents that combines working, episodic, and semantic memory inspired by human cognition to improve long-horizon task planning without model fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-based embodied planners are stateless and reactive, lacking persistent memory, which causes them to repeat errors and struggle with spatial/temporal dependencies in complex 3D environments.

Method: BrainMem transforms interaction histories into structured knowledge graphs and distilled symbolic guidelines, providing a plug-and-play memory system that integrates with arbitrary multi-modal LLMs without fine-tuning or additional training.

Result: Extensive experiments on four benchmarks (EB-ALFRED, EB-Navigation, EB-Manipulation, EB-Habitat) show significant improvements in task success rates across diverse models, with largest gains on long-horizon and spatially complex tasks.

Conclusion: Evolving memory systems like BrainMem represent a promising and scalable mechanism for achieving generalizable embodied intelligence by enabling agents to learn from past experience without model retraining.

Abstract: Embodied task planning requires agents to execute long-horizon, goal-directed actions in complex 3D environments, where success depends on both immediate perception and accumulated experience across tasks. However, most existing LLM-based planners are stateless and reactive, operating without persistent memory and therefore repeating errors and struggling with spatial or temporal dependencies. We propose BrainMem(Brain-Inspired Evolving Memory), a training-free hierarchical memory system that equips embodied agents with working, episodic, and semantic memory inspired by human cognition. BrainMem continuously transforms interaction histories into structured knowledge graphs and distilled symbolic guidelines, enabling planners to retrieve, reason over, and adapt behaviors from past experience without any model fine-tuning or additional training. This plug-and-play design integrates seamlessly with arbitrary multi-modal LLMs and greatly reduces reliance on task-specific prompt engineering. Extensive experiments on four representative benchmarks, including EB-ALFRED, EB-Navigation, EB-Manipulation, and EB-Habitat, demonstrate that BrainMem significantly enhances task success rates across diverse models and difficulty subsets, with the largest gains observed on long-horizon and spatially complex tasks. These results highlight evolving memory as a promising and scalable mechanism for generalizable embodied intelligence.

</details>


### [412] [Interdisciplinary Workshop on Mechanical Intelligence: Summary Report](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16381)
*Victoria A. Webster-Wood,Nicholas Gravish,Amir Alavi,Andres F Arrieta,Sarah Bergbreiter,Anthony Bloch,Laura Blumenschein,C. Chase Cao,Aja Mia Carter,Paolo Celli,Tony Chen,Margaret Coad,Mark Cutkosky,Michael Dickey,Brian Do,Robert Full,Mahdi Haghshenas-Jaryani,Kaushik Jayaram,Aaron Johnson,Eva Kanso,Emma Lejeune,Chen Li,Suyi Li,Jeffrey Lipton,Rob MacCurdy,Matt McHenry,Jean-Michel Mongeau,Todd Murphey,Mark Plecnik,Jordan Raney,Ryan D. Sochol,Hannah Stuart,Zeynep Temel,Michael Tolley,Barry Trimmer,T. J. Wallin,Kon-Well Wang,Wenzhong Yan,Mark Yim,Wenlong Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Summary of a 2024 NSF workshop on Mechanical Intelligence - the concept that physical structures themselves can encode intelligence through responsiveness, adaptivity, memory, and learning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore and define the emerging field of Mechanical Intelligence (MI), which contrasts with computational intelligence by encoding intelligence directly in physical structures rather than through electrical signaling and code.

Method: Two-day interdisciplinary workshop at NSF headquarters with 38 academic researchers and 8 NSF program officers, using small and large group discussions (4-5 and 9-10 people) to address topical questions on MI, with shared presentation slides and final presentations.

Result: The workshop produced collaborative discussions and shared outcomes on Mechanical Intelligence concepts, though specific technical results aren't detailed in this abstract.

Conclusion: The report summarizes workshop outcomes that advance understanding of Mechanical Intelligence as a distinct paradigm where intelligence emerges from physical structures themselves rather than computational processes.

Abstract: This report provides a summary of the outcomes of the Interdisciplinary Workshop on Mechanical Intelligence held in 2024. Mechanical Intelligence (MI) represents the phenomenon that novel structural features of material/biological/robotic systems can encode intelligence through responsiveness, adaptivity, memory, and learning in the mechanical structure itself. This is in contrast to computational intelligence, wherein the intelligence functions occur through electrical signaling and computer code. The two-day workshop was held at NSF headquarters on May 30-31 and included 38 invited academic researcher participants, and 8 program officers from the NSF. The workshop was structured around active small and large group discussions in groups of 4-5 and 9-10 with the goal of addressing topical questions on MI. Working groups entered notes into shared presentation slides for each discussion session and presented their outcomes in a final presentation on the last day. Here we summarize the overall outcomes of the workshop.

</details>


### [413] [RHINO-AR: An Augmented Reality Exhibit for Teaching Mobile Robotics Concepts in Museums](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16384)
*Nils Dengler,Tim Graf,Leif Van Holland,Patrick Stotko,Reinhard Klein,Maren Bennewitz*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RHINO-AR is an AR museum exhibit that places a virtual reconstruction of the historical RHINO robot into the real museum space, making robotics processes visible and understandable to visitors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Previous VR reconstruction (RHINO-VR) separated visitors from the real exhibition space and physical robot, creating a "reality gap." RHINO-AR aims to bridge this gap by integrating virtual reconstruction with the actual museum environment.

Method: Implemented on Magic Leap 2 headset using Unity, combining real-time environment meshing with interactive visualizations of LiDAR sensing, traversability, and path planning to make invisible robotics processes visible.

Result: Two-day museum study with 22 participants showed RHINO-AR was well received, effectively conveyed key navigation concepts, and generally preferred over VR due to stronger physical grounding and increased realism.

Conclusion: AR exhibits can effectively bridge the reality gap in museum settings by combining virtual reconstructions with physical spaces, enhancing visitor understanding and engagement with historical robotics technology.

Abstract: We present RHINO-AR, an interactive Augmented Reality (AR) museum exhibit that reintroduces the historical mobile robot RHINO into its original exhibition environment at the Deutsches Museum Bonn. The system builds on our previous work RHINO-VR, which reconstructed the robot and the environment in virtual reality. Although this created an engaging experience, it also revealed an important limitation, because visitors were separated from the real exhibition space and from the physical robot on display. RHINO-AR addresses this reality gap by placing a virtual reconstruction of the robot directly into the real museum space. Implemented on a Magic Leap~2 headset using Unity, our system combines real-time environment meshing with interactive visualizations of LiDAR sensing, traversability, and path planning to make otherwise invisible robotics processes understandable to non-expert visitors. We evaluated RHINO-AR in a two-day museum study with 22 participants, assessing usability, technical performance, satisfaction, conceptual understanding, and preference comparison to RHINO-VR. The results show that RHINO-AR was well received, effectively conveyed key navigation concepts, and generally preferred over the VR exhibit due to its stronger physical grounding and increased realism.

</details>


### [414] [Visual-RRT: Finding Paths toward Visual-Goals via Differentiable Rendering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16388)
*Sebin Lee,Jumin Lee,Taeyeon Kim,Younju Na,Woobin Im,Sung-Eui Yoon*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: vRRT enables robot motion planning to visual goals (images/videos) by combining differentiable rendering gradients with RRT sampling, using adaptive frontier exploration and inertial gradient expansion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RRT planners require precise numerical joint angle goals, but real applications often provide visual observations (images/videos) where exact configurations are unknown, creating a gap between sampling-based planning and vision applications.

Method: Visual-RRT (vRRT) unifies gradient-based exploitation from differentiable robot rendering with sampling-based RRT exploration, plus frontier-based exploration-exploitation strategy and inertial gradient tree expansion that inherits optimization states across branches.

Result: Extensive experiments on Franka, UR5e, and Fetch robots show vRRT achieves effective visual-goal planning in both simulation and real-world settings, bridging sampling-based planning with vision applications.

Conclusion: vRRT successfully enables motion planning to visual goals by integrating differentiable rendering with RRTs, addressing the limitation of requiring precise numerical goal configurations in traditional planners.

Abstract: Rapidly-exploring random trees (RRTs) have been widely adopted for robot motion planning due to their robustness and theoretical guarantees. However, existing RRT-based planners require explicit goal configurations specified as numerical joint angles, while many practical applications provide goal specifications through visual observations such as images or demonstration videos where precise goal configurations are unavailable. In this paper, we propose visual-RRT (vRRT), a motion planner that enables visual-goal planning by unifying gradient-based exploitation from differentiable robot rendering with sampling-based exploration from RRTs. We further introduce (i) a frontier-based exploration-exploitation strategy that adaptively prioritizes visually promising search regions, and (ii) inertial gradient tree expansion that inherits optimization states across tree branches for momentum-consistent gradient exploitation. Extensive experiments across various robot manipulators including Franka, UR5e, and Fetch demonstrate that vRRT achieves effective visual-goal planning in both simulated and real-world settings, bridging the gap between sampling-based planning and vision-centric robot applications. Our code is available at https://sgvr.kaist.ac.kr/Visual-RRT.

</details>


### [415] [Disentangled Robot Learning via Separate Forward and Inverse Dynamics Pretraining](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16391)
*Wenyao Zhang,Bozhou Zhang,Zekun Qi,Wenjun Zeng,Xin Jin,Li Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DeFI decouples visual forward and inverse dynamics pretraining to address VLA model limitations, achieving SOTA performance on robot manipulation benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLA models face two key issues: misalignment between 2D image forecasting and 3D action prediction, and limitations from vision-action entangled training that prevents learning from action-free web video data.

Method: Proposes DeFI framework with two components: GFDM (General Forward Dynamics Model) pretrained on diverse videos for future prediction, and GIDM (General Inverse Dynamics Model) trained via self-supervised learning to infer latent actions from unlabeled video transitions. These are integrated for end-to-end finetuning.

Result: Achieves average task length of 4.51 on CALVIN ABC-D, 51.2% success rate on SimplerEnv-Fractal benchmark, and 81.3% success rate in real-world deployment, significantly outperforming prior methods.

Conclusion: Decoupling forward and inverse dynamics pretraining enables better exploitation of respective data sources and leads to superior performance in vision-language-action tasks for robotics.

Abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) models have shown great potential in building generalist robots, but still face a dilemma-misalignment of 2D image forecasting and 3D action prediction. Besides, such a vision-action entangled training manner limits model learning from large-scale, action-free web video data. To address these issues, we propose DeFI, a novel framework that Decouples visual Forward and Inverse dynamics pretraining to exploit respective data sources, wherein video generation and action prediction are disentangled. We introduce the General Forward Dynamics Model (GFDM), pretrained on diverse human and robot videos for future prediction, and the General Inverse Dynamics Model (GIDM), trained via self-supervised learning to infer latent actions from unlabeled video transitions. These models are then integrated into a unified architecture for end-to-end finetuning on downstream tasks. In this manner, GFDM and GIDM first shine separately and then cooperate for mutual benefit. Extensive experiments on CALVIN ABC-D and SimplerEnv demonstrate state-of-the-art performance, with DeFI achieving an average task length of 4.51 for CALVIN, 51.2% success rate on SimplerEnv-Fractal benchmark and 81.3% success rate in real-world deployment, significantly outperforming prior methods.

</details>


### [416] [ICAT: Incident-Case-Grounded Adaptive Testing for Physical-Risk Prediction in Embodied World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16405)
*Zhenglin Lai,Sirui Huang,Yuteng Li,Changxin Huang,Jianqiang Li,Bingzhe Wu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ICAT framework tests video-generative world models' safety by grounding evaluations in real incident reports, revealing models often miss danger cues and underestimate risks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Video-generative world models are increasingly used as neural simulators for embodied AI planning and policy learning, but their ability to predict physical risk and severe consequences is rarely evaluated. Current models often downplay or omit key danger cues and severe outcomes for hazardous actions, which can induce unsafe preferences during planning and training on imagined rollouts.

Method: Propose ICAT framework that grounds testing in real incident reports and safety manuals by building structured risk memories and retrieving/composing them to constrain the generation of risk cases with causal chains and severity labels. Creates a benchmark for evaluating world model safety.

Result: Experiments on the ICAT-based benchmark show that mainstream world models frequently miss mechanisms and triggering conditions and miscalibrate severity, falling short of the reliability required for safety-critical embodied deployment.

Conclusion: Current video-generative world models have significant safety limitations in predicting physical risks and severe consequences, highlighting the need for safety-focused evaluation frameworks like ICAT before deployment in safety-critical embodied AI applications.

Abstract: Video-generative world models are increasingly used as neural simulators for embodied planning and policy learning, yet their ability to predict physical risk and severe consequences is rarely evaluated.We find that these models often downplay or omit key danger cues and severe outcomes for hazardous actions, which can induce unsafe preferences during planning and training on imagined rollouts. We propose ICAT, which grounds testing in real incident reports and safety manuals by building structured risk memories and retrieving/composing them to constrain the generation of risk cases with causal chains and severity labels. Experiments on an ICAT-based benchmark show that mainstream world models frequently miss mechanisms and triggering conditions and miscalibrate severity, falling short of the reliability required for safety-critical embodied deployment.

</details>


### [417] [An Edge-Host-Cloud Architecture for Robot-Agnostic, Caregiver-in-the-Loop Personalized Cognitive Exercise: Multi-Site Deployment in Dementia Care](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16408)
*Wenzheng Zhao,Ruth Palan Lopez,Shu Fen Wung,Fengpei Yuan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Speaking Memories is a distributed robotic platform for personalized cognitive exercise support that integrates caregiver knowledge, edge computing, and robots into a unified socio-technical system with emotion-aware dialogue and automated evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To create a scalable, privacy-preserving robotic interaction platform for cognitive exercise support that goes beyond single robot systems by involving stakeholders (caregivers, family) in the loop and enabling personalized, emotion-aware interactions across heterogeneous robotic embodiments.

Method: A distributed architecture with: 1) caregiver-authored knowledge via secure cloud portal, 2) local edge interaction server for multimodal perception/decoupling from robot hardware, 3) fusion of auditory/visual/textual signals for emotion-aware dialogue, 4) automated multimodal evaluation layer for continuous analysis of user responses and engagement patterns.

Result: Real-world deployments show sub-6-second response latency, robust multimodal synchronization, consistently positive feedback from participants and caregivers, and the ability to share subsets of data (with consent/IRB approval). The platform achieves low-latency, privacy-preserving operation.

Conclusion: Speaking Memories demonstrates a viable distributed robotics architecture for personalized cognitive support that successfully integrates stakeholder knowledge, edge intelligence, and embodied agents while enabling systematic evaluation, data-driven personalization, and scalable deployment across different robotic platforms.

Abstract: We present Speaking Memories, a distributed, stakeholder-in-the-loop robotic interaction platform for personalized cognitive exercise support. Rather than a single robot-centric system, Speaking Memories is designed as a generalizable robotics architecture that integrates caregiver-authored knowledge, local edge intelligence, and embodied robotic agents into a unified socio-technical loop. The platform fuses auditory, visual, and textual signals to enable emotion-aware, personalized dialogue, while decoupling multimodal perception and reasoning from robot-specific hardware through a local edge interaction server. This design achieves low-latency, privacy-preserving operation and supports scalable deployment across heterogeneous robotic embodiments. Caregivers and family members contribute structured biographical knowledge via a secure cloud portal, which conditions downstream dialogue policies and enables longitudinal personalization across interaction sessions. Beyond real-time interaction, the system incorporates an automated multimodal evaluation layer that continuously analyzes user responses, affective cues, and engagement patterns, producing structured interaction metrics at scale. These metrics support systematic assessment of interaction quality, enable data-driven model fine-tuning, and lay the foundation for future clinician- and caregiver-informed personalization and intervention planning. We evaluate the platform through real-world deployments, measuring end-to-end latency, dialogue coherence, interaction stability, and stakeholder-reported usability and engagement. Results demonstrate sub-6-second response latency, robust multimodal synchronization, and consistently positive feedback from both participants and caregivers. Furthermore, subsets of the dataset can be shared upon request, subject to participant consent and IRB constraints.

</details>


### [418] [LatentMimic: Terrain-Adaptive Locomotion via Latent Space Imitation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16440)
*Zhiquan Wang,Yunyu Liu,Dipam Patel,Ayush Kumar,Aniket Bera,Bedrich Benes*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: LatentMimic decouples style from geometry for quadruped locomotion, using latent divergence minimization to preserve gait style while adapting to terrain, achieving better success rates than motion-tracking methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing imitation-based methods face a trade-off: strict adherence to motion capture references penalizes geometric deviations needed for terrain adaptation, while terrain-centric policies compromise stylistic fidelity.

Method: LatentMimic minimizes marginal latent divergence between policy's state-action distribution and learned mocap prior, providing conditional relaxation of rigid pose-tracking. Includes terrain adaptation module with dynamic replay buffer to resolve distribution shifts across terrains.

Result: Validated across four locomotion styles and four terrains, LatentMimic enables effective terrain-adaptive locomotion with higher terrain traversal success rates than state-of-the-art motion-tracking methods while maintaining high stylistic fidelity.

Conclusion: LatentMimic successfully decouples stylistic fidelity from geometric constraints, allowing quadruped robots to adapt to complex terrains while preserving motion style, overcoming the fundamental optimization trade-off in existing methods.

Abstract: Developing natural and diverse locomotion controllers for quadruped robots that can adapt to complex terrains while preserving motion style remains a significant challenge. Existing imitation-based methods face a fundamental optimization trade-off: strict adherence to motion capture (mocap) references penalizes the geometric deviations required for terrain adaptability, whereas terrain-centric policies often compromise stylistic fidelity. We introduce LatentMimic, a novel locomotion learning framework that decouples stylistic fidelity from geometric constraints. By minimizing the marginal latent divergence between the policy's state-action distribution and a learned mocap prior, our approach provides a conditional relaxation of rigid pose-tracking objectives. This formulation preserves gait topology while permitting independent end-effector adaptations for irregular terrains. We further introduce a terrain adaptation module with a dynamic replay buffer to resolve the policy's distribution shifts across different terrains. We validate our method across four locomotion styles and four terrains, demonstrating that LatentMimic enables effective terrain-adaptive locomotion, achieving higher terrain traversal success rates than state-of-the-art motion-tracking methods while maintaining high stylistic fidelity.

</details>


### [419] [Compiling OpenSCENARIO 2.1 for Scenario-Based Testing in CARLA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16452)
*Thoshitha Gamage,Lasanthi Gamage*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A modern compiler architecture that translates OpenSCENARIO 2.1 DSL directly into executable CARLA behaviors using ANTLR4 frontend, semantic middle-end, and py_trees backend, eliminating need for external solvers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While OpenSCENARIO 2.1 DSL enables declarative scenario authoring for testing, its integration into open-source simulators like CARLA is limited by legacy parsers, creating a need for better compilation infrastructure.

Method: Multi-pass compiler architecture with ANTLR4 frontend for AST generation, semantic middle-end, and runtime backend that synthesizes deterministic py_trees behavior trees. Uses custom method registry to map domain ontology to CARLA's procedural API.

Result: Successfully demonstrated with multi-actor cut-in and evasive maneuver scenarios, showing ability to process concurrent actions, dynamic mathematical expressions, and asynchronous signaling. Provides functional baseline for reproducible large-scale testing.

Conclusion: The framework establishes a foundation for reproducible, large-scale scenario-based testing in CARLA, with future work planned for C++ optimizations to address Python computational overhead.

Abstract: While the ASAM OpenSCENARIO 2.1 Domain-Specific Language (DSL) enables declarative, intent-driven authoring for Scenario-Based Testing (SBT), its integration into open-source simulators like CARLA remains limited by legacy parsers. We propose a multi-pass modern compiler architecture that translates the OpenSCENARIO 2.1 DSL directly into executable CARLA behaviors. The pipeline features an ANTLR4 frontend for Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) generation, a semantic middle-end, and a runtime backend that synthesizes deterministic py_trees behavior trees. Mapping the standardized domain ontology directly to CARLA's procedural API via a custom method registry eliminates the need for external logic solvers. A demonstrative multi-actor cut-in and evasive maneuver, selected from a wider suite of validated scenarios, confirms the compiler's ability to process concurrent actions, dynamic mathematical expressions, and asynchronous signaling. This framework establishes a functional baseline for reproducible, large-scale SBT, paving the way for future C++ optimizations to mitigate current Python-based computational overhead.

</details>


### [420] [Learning-Based Sparsification of Dynamic Graphs in Robotic Exploration Algorithms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16509)
*Adithya V. Sastry,Bibek Poudel,Weizi Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Transformer-based RL framework prunes exploration graphs during robotic frontier exploration, reducing graph size by up to 96% while maintaining consistent exploration performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robotic exploration algorithms using graph structures (like RRT) accumulate redundant information as graphs grow rapidly, impacting performance. There's a need to intelligently prune these dynamic graphs during exploration.

Method: Transformer-based framework trained with Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) to prune exploration graphs. Evaluated on robotic agent simulations using Rapidly Exploring Random Trees (RRT) for frontier-based exploration.

Result: Learned policy reduces graph size by up to 96%. Framework learns to associate pruning decisions with exploration outcomes despite sparse, delayed rewards. Intelligent pruning achieves lower but more consistent exploration rates (lowest standard deviation) across varied environments.

Conclusion: First evidence suggesting viability of RL for sparsification of dynamic graphs in robotic exploration algorithms, enabling efficient graph management while maintaining exploration consistency.

Abstract: Many robotic exploration algorithms rely on graph structures for frontier-based exploration and dynamic path planning. However, these graphs grow rapidly, accumulating redundant information and impacting performance. We present a transformer-based framework trained with Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) to prune these graphs during exploration, limiting their growth and reducing the accumulation of excess information. The framework was evaluated on simulations of a robotic agent using Rapidly Exploring Random Trees (RRT) to carry out frontier-based exploration, where the learned policy reduces graph size by up to 96%. We find preliminary evidence that our framework learns to associate pruning decisions with exploration outcomes despite sparse, delayed reward signals. We also observe that while intelligent pruning achieves a lower rate of exploration compared to baselines, it yields the lowest standard deviation, producing the most consistent exploration across varied environments. To the best of our knowledge, these results are the first suggesting the viability of RL in sparsification of dynamic graphs used in robotic exploration algorithms.

</details>


### [421] [On-Orbit Space AI: Federated, Multi-Agent, and Collaborative Algorithms for Satellite Constellations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16518)
*Ziyang Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Survey paper on AI for satellite constellations covering federated learning, multi-agent algorithms, and collaborative sensing for on-orbit autonomy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Satellite constellations are evolving into networked platforms requiring new AI approaches beyond single-satellite inference, addressing challenges like dynamic connectivity, SWaP-C limits, radiation faults, non-IID data, concept drift, and safety constraints.

Method: Three complementary paradigms: 1) Federated learning for cross-satellite training and secure aggregation, 2) Multi-agent algorithms for cooperative planning and control, 3) Collaborative sensing and distributed inference for multi-satellite fusion and split inference. Provides taxonomy unifying collaboration architectures, temporal mechanisms, and trust models.

Result: Consolidates emerging field of on-orbit space AI with system-level view and taxonomy. Maintains continuously curated repository (https://github.com/ziyangwang007/AI4Space) for community development.

Conclusion: Survey establishes foundations for constellation-scale AI autonomy, addressing unique space-specific challenges through integrated federated learning, multi-agent coordination, and collaborative sensing approaches.

Abstract: Satellite constellations are transforming space systems from isolated spacecraft into networked, software-defined platforms capable of on-orbit perception, decision making, and adaptation. Yet much of the existing AI studies remains centered on single-satellite inference, while constellation-scale autonomy introduces fundamentally new algorithmic requirements: learning and coordination under dynamic inter-satellite connectivity, strict SWaP-C limits, radiation-induced faults, non-IID data, concept drift, and safety-critical operational constraints. This survey consolidates the emerging field of on-orbit space AI through three complementary paradigms: (i) {federated learning} for cross-satellite training, personalization, and secure aggregation; (ii) {multi-agent algorithms} for cooperative planning, resource allocation, scheduling, formation control, and collision avoidance; and (iii) {collaborative sensing and distributed inference} for multi-satellite fusion, tracking, split/early-exit inference, and cross-layer co-design with constellation networking. We provide a system-level view and a taxonomy that unifies collaboration architectures, temporal mechanisms, and trust models. To support community development and keep this review actionable over time, we continuously curate relevant papers and resources at https://github.com/ziyangwang007/AI4Space.

</details>


### [422] [Human Cognition in Machines: A Unified Perspective of World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16592)
*Timothy Rupprecht,Pu Zhao,Amir Taherin,Arash Akbari,Arman Akbari,Yumei He,Sean Duffy,Juyi Lin,Yixiao Chen,Rahul Chowdhury,Enfu Nan,Yixin Shen,Yifan Cao,Haochen Zeng,Weiwei Chen,Geng Yuan,Jennifer Dy,Sarah Ostadabbas,Silvia Zhang,David Kaeli,Edmund Yeh,Yanzhi Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper presents a unified cognitive architecture framework for evaluating world models, identifies research gaps in motivation and meta-cognition, and introduces Epistemic World Models for scientific discovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a principled evaluation framework for world models that claim "human-like" cognitive capabilities, grounded in Cognitive Architecture Theory, and to identify research gaps in current approaches.

Method: Develops a conceptual unified framework incorporating all cognitive functions from CAT (memory, perception, language, reasoning, imagining, motivation, meta-cognition), creates a taxonomy applied across video, embodied, and epistemic world models, and proposes directions informed by active inference and global workspace theory.

Result: Identifies that motivation (especially intrinsic motivation) and meta-cognition remain drastically under-researched in current world models, introduces Epistemic World Models as a new category for scientific discovery frameworks, and provides a taxonomy that reveals research directions missed by prior classifications.

Conclusion: The paper establishes a comprehensive cognitive architecture framework for evaluating world models, highlights critical gaps in motivation and meta-cognition research, and proposes concrete future directions including Epistemic World Models for structured knowledge discovery.

Abstract: This comprehensive report distinguishes prior works by the cognitive functions they innovate. Many works claim an almost "human-like" cognitive capability in their world models. To evaluate these claims requires a proper grounding in first principles in Cognitive Architecture Theory (CAT). We present a conceptual unified framework for world models that fully incorporates all the cognitive functions associated with CAT (i.e. memory, perception, language, reasoning, imagining, motivation, and meta-cognition) and identify gaps in the research as a guide for future states of the art. In particular, we find that motivation (especially intrinsic motivation) and meta-cognition remain drastically under-researched, and we propose concrete directions informed by active inference and global workspace theory to address them. We further introduce Epistemic World Models, a new category encompassing agent frameworks for scientific discovery that operate over structured knowledge. Our taxonomy, applied across video, embodied, and epistemic world models, suggests research directions where prior taxonomies have not.

</details>


### [423] [Emergency Stopping for Liquid-manipulating Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16667)
*Samuli Hynninen,Ville Kyrki*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Emergency stop system for robots manipulating liquids in open containers that generates time-optimal, spill-free stopping trajectories using model predictive control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Liquid manipulation is challenging due to sensitivity to accelerations, and while spill-free manipulation has been studied, emergency stopping under unexpected hazards has received little attention despite the risk of hazardous spills from abrupt braking.

Method: Formulate emergency stopping as an optimal control problem and solve it in a model predictive control framework to generate time-optimal, spill-free stopping trajectories. The method operates as a plug-and-play safety layer on top of existing slosh-free motion planning methods.

Result: Demonstrated through simulation and on a 7-DoF Franka Emika Panda robot that the approach achieves fast emergency stopping without spilling.

Conclusion: The proposed emergency stop system enables immediate reaction to detected hazards while accounting for nonlinear liquid dynamics, providing a safety layer for liquid manipulation robots.

Abstract: Manipulating open liquid containers is challenging because liquids are highly sensitive to vessel accelerations and jerks. Although spill-free liquid manipulation has been widely studied, emergency stopping under unexpected hazards has received little attention, despite the fact that abrupt braking may cause hazardous spills. This letter presents an emergency stop system for robots manipulating liquids in open containers. We formulate emergency stopping as an optimal control problem and solve it in a model predictive control framework to generate time-optimal, spill-free stopping trajectories. The method operates as a plug-and-play safety layer on top of existing slosh-free motion planning methods, enabling immediate reaction to detected hazards while accounting for nonlinear liquid dynamics. We demonstrate, through simulation and on a 7-DoF Franka Emika Panda robot, that the proposed approach achieves fast emergency stopping without spilling.

</details>


### [424] [Diffusion-Based Optimization for Accelerated Convergence of Redundant Dual-Arm Minimum Time Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16670)
*Jushan Chen,Jonathan Fried,Santiago Paternain*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Diffusion-based framework for dual-arm robots reduces computation time 35x and Cartesian error 34% compared to prior gradient-based approach.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior gradient-based bi-level optimization for dual-arm path following had large computation overhead and couldn't directly impose L∞ Cartesian error constraints due to sparse gradients.

Method: Novel diffusion-based framework using probabilistic sampling to solve the nonconvex high-level optimization problem for redundant dual-arm robot configuration.

Result: Achieved 35x reduction in runtime and 34% less Cartesian error compared to previous gradient-based approach.

Conclusion: Diffusion-based probabilistic sampling effectively addresses computation and constraint limitations of gradient methods for dual-arm robot path following optimization.

Abstract: We present a framework leveraging a novel variant of the model-based diffusion algorithm to minimize the time required for a redundant dual-arm robot configuration to follow a desired relative Cartesian path. Our prior work proposed a bi-level optimization approach for the dual-arm problem, where we derived the analytical solution to the lower-level convex sub-problem and solved the high-level nonconvex problem using a primal-dual approach. However, the gradient-based nature leads to a large computation overhead, and it prohibits directly imposing an $L_{\infty}$ Cartesian error constraint along the joint trajectory due to the sparsity of the gradient. In this work, we propose a diffusion-based framework that relies on probabilistic sampling to tackle the aforementioned challenges in the nonconvex high-level problem, leading to a 35x reduction in the runtime and 34\% less Cartesian error compared to our prior work.

</details>


### [425] [ReconVLA: An Uncertainty-Guided and Failure-Aware Vision-Language-Action Framework for Robotic Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16677)
*Lingling Chen,Zongyao Lyu,William J. Beksi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ReconVLA applies conformal prediction to VLA models to provide calibrated uncertainty estimates for robotic control, improving failure anticipation and reducing catastrophic errors without retraining the base model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current VLA models lack calibrated confidence measures, limiting their reliability in real-world settings where uncertainty and failures must be anticipated for safe operation.

Method: Apply conformal prediction directly to action token outputs of pretrained VLA policies, and extend it to robot state space for outlier/unsafe state detection before failures occur.

Result: Conformalized action predictions consistently improve failure anticipation, reduce catastrophic errors, and provide calibrated confidence measures in both simulation and real robot experiments across diverse manipulation tasks.

Conclusion: ReconVLA provides a simple yet effective approach to make VLA controllers more reliable through uncertainty quantification and failure detection, without requiring retraining or modification of the underlying VLA model.

Abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) models have emerged as generalist robotic controllers capable of mapping visual observations and natural language instructions to continuous action sequences. However, VLAs provide no calibrated measure of confidence in their action predictions, thus limiting their reliability in real-world settings where uncertainty and failures must be anticipated. To address this problem we introduce ReconVLA, a reliable conformal model that produces uncertainty-guided and failure-aware control signals. Concretely, our approach applies conformal prediction directly to the action token outputs of pretrained VLA policies, yielding calibrated uncertainty estimates that correlate with execution quality and task success. Furthermore, we extend conformal prediction to the robot state space to detect outliers or unsafe states before failures occur, providing a simple yet effective failure detection mechanism that complements the action-level uncertainty. We evaluate ReconVLA in both simulation and real robot experiments across diverse manipulation tasks. Our results show that conformalized action predictions consistently improve failure anticipation, reduce catastrophic errors, and provide a calibrated measure of confidence without retraining or modifying the underlying VLA.

</details>


### [426] [Rewind-IL: Online Failure Detection and State Respawning for Imitation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16683)
*Gehan Zheng,Sanjay Seenivasan,Matthew Johnson-Roberson,Weiming Zhi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Rewind-IL is a training-free online safeguard framework for action-chunked imitation policies that detects failures via temporal discrepancy and recovers by rewinding to verified safe states.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Action-chunked imitation policies often fail to recover from execution drift, continuing to produce plausible actions even when off the demonstration manifold. Existing runtime monitors either need failure data, over-trigger, or lack recovery mechanisms.

Method: Combines zero-shot failure detection using Temporal Inter-chunk Discrepancy Estimate (TIDE) calibrated with conformal prediction, with state-respawning that returns to semantically verified safe intermediate states. Offline, uses vision-language models to identify recovery checkpoints and builds a checkpoint feature database. Online, monitors self-consistency in overlapping action chunks and similarity to checkpoints.

Result: Experiments on real-world and simulated long-horizon manipulation tasks, including transfer to flow-matching action-chunked policies, demonstrate improved reliability through policy-internal consistency and semantically grounded respawning.

Conclusion: Policy-internal consistency coupled with semantically grounded respawning offers a practical route to improved reliability in imitation learning without requiring additional training.

Abstract: Imitation learning has enabled robots to acquire complex visuomotor manipulation skills from demonstrations, but deployment failures remain a major obstacle, especially for long-horizon action-chunked policies. Once execution drifts off the demonstration manifold, these policies often continue producing locally plausible actions without recovering from the failure. Existing runtime monitors either require failure data, over-trigger under benign feature drift, or stop at failure detection without providing a recovery mechanism. We present Rewind-IL, a training-free online safeguard framework for generative action-chunked imitation policies. Rewind-IL combines a zero-shot failure detector based on Temporal Inter-chunk Discrepancy Estimate (TIDE), calibrated with split conformal prediction, with a state-respawning mechanism that returns the robot to a semantically verified safe intermediate state. Offline, a vision-language model identifies recovery checkpoints in demonstrations, and the frozen policy encoder is used to construct a compact checkpoint feature database. Online, Rewind-IL monitors self-consistency in overlapping action chunks, tracks similarity to the checkpoint library, and, upon failure, rewinds execution to the latest verified safe state before restarting inference from a clean policy state. Experiments on real-world and simulated long-horizon manipulation tasks, including transfer to flow-matching action-chunked policies, demonstrate that policy-internal consistency coupled with semantically grounded respawning offers a practical route to improved reliability in imitation learning. Supplemental materials are available at https://sjay05.github.io/rewind-il

</details>


### [427] [Autonomous Vehicle Collision Avoidance With Racing Parameterized Deep Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16702)
*Shathushan Sivashangaran,Vihaan Dutta,Apoorva Khairnar,Sepideh Gohari,Azim Eskandarian*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: DRL-based collision avoidance system outperforms traditional MPC-APF baseline with 31x fewer FLOPS and 64x lower latency, achieving better safety in autonomous vehicle emergency scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Road traffic accidents cause significant fatalities and costs, with human error responsible for 94% of crashes. Autonomous vehicles need emergency collision avoidance systems that work at vehicle dynamics limits while being computationally efficient, especially for adverse weather, cybersecurity threats, and mixed human-AV traffic.

Method: Parameterizes Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) collision avoidance policy using race car overtaking scenarios without explicit trajectory guidance. Uses physics-informed, simulator exploit-aware reward to encode nonlinear vehicle kinodynamics. Evaluates two policies: default uni-direction and reversed heading variant that navigates opposite to other cars.

Result: Both DRL policies outperform MPC-APF baseline, with zero-shot transfer to scaled hardware across three intersection scenarios. Achieves 31x fewer FLOPS and 64x lower inference latency. Reversed heading policy outperforms default by 30% and baseline by 50% in head-to-head collisions, matches default in side collisions, with both DRL policies evading 10% more than numerical optimal control.

Conclusion: DRL-based emergency collision avoidance systems can significantly enhance autonomous vehicle safety while being computationally efficient, outperforming traditional control methods and showing strong transfer capabilities to real-world hardware scenarios.

Abstract: Road traffic accidents are a leading cause of fatalities worldwide. In the US, human error causes 94% of crashes, resulting in excess of 7,000 pedestrian fatalities and $500 billion in costs annually. Autonomous Vehicles (AVs) with emergency collision avoidance systems that operate at the limits of vehicle dynamics at a high frequency, a dual constraint of nonlinear kinodynamic accuracy and computational efficiency, further enhance safety benefits during adverse weather and cybersecurity breaches, and to evade dangerous human driving when AVs and human drivers share roads. This paper parameterizes a Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) collision avoidance policy Out-Of-Distribution (OOD) utilizing race car overtaking, without explicit geometric mimicry reference trajectory guidance, in simulation, with a physics-informed, simulator exploit-aware reward to encode nonlinear vehicle kinodynamics. Two policies are evaluated, a default uni-direction and a reversed heading variant that navigates in the opposite direction to other cars, which both consistently outperform a Model Predictive Control and Artificial Potential Function (MPC-APF) baseline, with zero-shot transfer to proportionally scaled hardware, across three intersection collision scenarios, at 31x fewer Floating Point Operations (FLOPS) and 64x lower inference latency. The reversed heading policy outperforms the default racing overtaking policy in head-to-head collisions by 30% and the baseline by 50%, and matches the former in side collisions, where both DRL policies evade 10% greater than numerical optimal control.

</details>


### [428] [LiDAR-based Crowd Navigation with Visible Edge Group Representation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16741)
*Allan Wang,Aaron Steinfeld*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Using visible edge-based group representation for robot navigation in crowded environments, achieving comparable safety/socialness with faster computation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robot navigation in crowded pedestrian environments is challenging; while group-based representations improve safety and socialness, existing approaches either work only in limited scenarios or depend on noisy external detection modules prone to occlusion errors.

Method: Propose visible edge-based group representation based on observation that group prediction accuracy has marginal effect on navigation performance in crowded environments. Integrate this simplified representation into navigation framework.

Result: Simulation experiments show navigation framework with simplified group representation performs comparably in safety and socialness in dense crowds while achieving faster computation speed. Real robot deployment explores practical benefits.

Conclusion: Visible edge-based group representation enables practical deployment of group-based navigation in real-world crowded environments with efficient computation and comparable performance to more complex approaches.

Abstract: Robot navigation in crowded pedestrian environments is a well-known challenge and we explore the practical deployment of group-based representations in this setting. Pedestrian groups have been empirically shown to enable a mobile robot's navigation behavior to be safer and more social. However, existing approaches either explored groups only in limited scenarios with no high-density crowds or depended on external detection modules to track individuals, which are prone to noise and errors due to occlusions in crowds. We show that group prediction accuracy affects navigation performance only marginally in crowded environments. Based on this observation, we propose the visible edge-based group representation. We additionally demonstrate via simulation experiments that our navigation framework, integrated with the simplified group representation, performs comparatively in terms of safety and socialness in dense crowds, while achieving faster computation speed. Finally, we deploy our navigation framework on a real robot to explore the benefits of practically deploying group-based representations in the real world.

</details>


### [429] [LongBench: Evaluating Robotic Manipulation Policies on Real-World Long-Horizon Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16788)
*Xueyao Chen,Jingkai Jia,Tong Yang,Yibo Fu,Wei Li,Wenqiang Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: LongBench is a real-world benchmark with 1,000+ episodes for evaluating long-horizon robotic manipulation, distinguishing between context-independent (fully observable) and context-dependent (ambiguity-driven) regimes to analyze execution robustness and contextual reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks provide limited insight into why robotic manipulation policies degrade over extended horizons, as most are simulation-based or report only aggregate success, making it difficult to disentangle distinct sources of temporal difficulty in real-world execution.

Method: LongBench consists of over 1,000 real-world episodes organized into two complementary regimes: Context-Independent (fully observable) and Context-Dependent (ambiguity-driven). Tasks are organized into capability- and ambiguity-specific subsets to enable mechanism-aware evaluation of execution robustness, temporal consistency, and context-dependent reasoning.

Result: Evaluation of six state-of-the-art policies reveals that long-horizon performance is not governed by a single factor. Performance in fully observable settings is more strongly associated with execution robustness, while contextual difficulty varies across tasks and is not consistently improved by memory-based methods.

Conclusion: LongBench serves as a useful benchmark for studying long-horizon manipulation and developing policies with stronger robustness across both execution and contextual challenges, providing mechanism-aware evaluation that distinguishes between different sources of temporal difficulty.

Abstract: Robotic manipulation policies often degrade over extended horizons, yet existing benchmarks provide limited insight into why such failures occur. Most prior benchmarks are either simulation-based or report aggregate success, making it difficult to disentangle the distinct sources of temporal difficulty in real-world execution. We introduce LongBench, a real-world benchmark for evaluating long-horizon manipulation. LongBench consists of over 1,000 real-world episodes, covering two complementary regimes: Context-Independent (fully observable) and Context-Dependent (ambiguity-driven). By organizing tasks into capability- and ambiguity-specific subsets, LongBench enables mechanism-aware evaluation of execution robustness, temporal consistency, and context-dependent reasoning. Evaluating six state-of-the-art policies reveals that long-horizon performance is not governed by a single factor. We observe that performance in fully observable settings is more strongly associated with execution robustness, while contextual difficulty varies across tasks and is not consistently improved by memory-based methods. We hope that LongBench serves as a useful benchmark for studying long-horizon manipulation and for developing policies with stronger robustness across both execution and contextual challenges.

</details>


### [430] [Refinement of Accelerated Demonstrations via Incremental Iterative Reference Learning Control for Fast Contact-Rich Imitation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16850)
*Koki Yamane,Cristian C. Beltran-Hernandez,Steven Oh,Masashi Hamaya,Sho Sakaino*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: I2RLC gradually speeds up demonstrations while refining trajectories, enabling 10x faster contact-rich manipulation for imitation learning with better fidelity than direct acceleration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fast demonstrations for contact-rich manipulation are hard to obtain: humans can't demonstrate at high speeds, and naive acceleration alters contact dynamics and causes tracking errors.

Method: Proposes Incremental Iterative Reference Learning Control (I2RLC), which gradually increases speed while updating reference trajectories using tracking errors, improving on direct IRLC application at high speeds.

Result: Achieves 10x faster demonstrations with reduced tracking error; I2RLC improves spatial similarity by 22.5% over IRLC; IL policies trained on refined trajectories achieve 100% success in peg-in-hole tasks with lower contact forces.

Conclusion: Gradual speed scheduling with reference adaptation provides a practical path to fast, contact-rich imitation learning, enabling policies that execute faster than demonstrations with high success rates.

Abstract: Fast execution of contact-rich manipulation is critical for practical deployment, yet providing fast demonstrations for imitation learning (IL) remains challenging: humans cannot demonstrate at high speed, and naively accelerating demonstrations alters contact dynamics and induces large tracking errors. We present a method to autonomously refine time-accelerated demonstrations by repurposing Iterative Reference Learning Control (IRLC) to iteratively update the reference trajectory from observed tracking errors. However, applying IRLC directly at high speed tends to produce larger early-iteration errors and less stable transients. To address this issue, we propose Incremental Iterative Reference Learning Control (I2RLC), which gradually increases the speed while updating the reference, yielding high-fidelity trajectories. We validate on real-robot whiteboard erasing and peg-in-hole tasks using a teleoperation setup with a compliance-controlled follower and a 3D-printed haptic leader. Both IRLC and I2RLC achieve up to 10x faster demonstrations with reduced tracking error; moreover, I2RLC improves spatial similarity to the original trajectories by 22.5% on average over IRLC across three tasks and multiple speeds (3x-10x). We then use the refined trajectories to train IL policies; the resulting policies execute faster than the demonstrations and achieve 100% success rates in the peg-in-hole task at both seen and unseen positions, with I2RLC-trained policies exhibiting lower contact forces than those trained on IRLC-refined demonstrations. These results indicate that gradual speed scheduling coupled with reference adaptation provides a practical path to fast, contact-rich IL.

</details>


### [431] [Greedy Kalman-Swarm: Improving State Estimation in Robot Swarms in Harsh Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16868)
*Phunyapa Suksomboon,Paulo Garcia*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Greedy Kalman-Swarm enables decentralized state estimation for robotic swarms using localized neighbor sensing without global communication, balancing accuracy and robustness in constrained environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional swarm state estimation requires centralized processing or heavy communication protocols that are impractical for real-world deployments, especially in communication-constrained environments like search-and-rescue or space exploration missions.

Method: A localized "greedy" approach to distributed state estimation where individual robots leverage relative inter-robot sensing, integrating all currently available neighbor data at each iteration without requiring full data availability or global communication.

Result: Simulations show robots can effectively refine internal states using neighbor data while remaining robust when data is missing, achieving performance between independent estimation and centralized systems, particularly in harsh/dynamic conditions.

Conclusion: Global state awareness can emerge rather than be enforced, providing a scalable framework for swarm cohesion in unpredictable terrains, with applications in resilient autonomous systems where reliable communication cannot be guaranteed.

Abstract: State estimation is a fundamental requirement in robotics, where the accurate determination of a robot's state is essential for stable operation despite inherent process disturbances and sensor noise. Traditionally, this is achieved through Kalman filtering, providing a statistically optimal estimate by balancing predictive models with noisy measurements. In the context of robotic swarms, the challenge shifts from individual accuracy to collective coordination, where the integration of global dynamics can significantly enhance the precision of the entire group. Existing estimation techniques rely on centralized processing or heavy communication protocols to reach a global consensus, which are frequently impractical in real-world deployments. Here we show that a localized, "greedy" approach to distributed state estimation (termed "Greedy Kalman-Swarm") allows individual robots to leverage relative inter-robot sensing for improved accuracy without requiring full data availability or global communication. Simulations in communication-constrained environments show robots can effectively integrate all currently available neighbor data at each iteration to refine their internal states, yet remain robust and functional even when data is missing. This results in a performance profile that strikes a balance between the low overhead of independent estimation and the high accuracy of centralized systems, specifically under harsh or dynamic environmental conditions. Our results demonstrate that global state awareness can be emergent rather than enforced, providing a scalable framework for maintaining swarm cohesion in unpredictable terrains. We anticipate that this decentralized methodology will serve as a foundation for more resilient autonomous systems, particularly in search-and-rescue or space exploration missions where reliable, high-bandwidth communication cannot be guaranteed.

</details>


### [432] [Chain Of Interaction Benchmark (COIN): When Reasoning meets Embodied Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16886)
*Xianhao Wang,Xiaojian Ma,Haozhe Hu,Rongpeng Su,Yutian Cheng,Zhou Ziheng,Hangxin Liu,Lei Liu,Bin Li,Qing Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: COIN benchmark evaluates interactive reasoning in robotic manipulation through 50 realistic tasks, primitive/composition datasets, and systematic metrics revealing current methods' limitations in visual-motor coordination.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks fail to systematically evaluate interactive reasoning capabilities needed for real-world embodied agents, which require sequential interaction under partial observability to solve long-horizon tasks.

Method: Created COIN-50 benchmark with 50 interactive tasks, COIN-Primitive for causally-dependent tasks, and COIN-Composition for skill generalization. Developed low-cost mobile AR teleoperation system to collect 1,000 demonstrations. Established systematic evaluation metrics for execution stability and generalization robustness.

Result: Comprehensive evaluation of CodeAsPolicy, VLA, and language-conditioned H-VLA approaches reveals critical limitations: models struggle with interactive reasoning due to significant gaps between visual understanding and motor execution.

Conclusion: COIN benchmark systematically assesses interactive reasoning in robotic manipulation, highlighting current methods' deficiencies and providing fine-grained analysis to guide future development of embodied agents.

Abstract: Generalist embodied agents must perform interactive, causally-dependent reasoning, continually interacting with the environment, acquiring information, and updating plans to solve long-horizon tasks before they could be adopted in real-life scenarios. For instance, retrieving an apple from a cabinet may require opening multiple doors and drawers before the apple becomes visible and reachable, demanding sequential interaction under partial observability. However, existing benchmarks fail to systematically evaluate this essential capability. We introduce COIN, a benchmark designed to assess interactive reasoning in realistic robotic manipulation through three key contributions. First, we construct COIN-50: 50 interactive tasks in daily scenarios, and create COIN-Primitive required by causally-dependent tasks, and COIN-Composition with mid-term complexity for skill learning and generalization evaluation. Second, we develop a low-cost mobile AR teleoperation system and collect the COIN-Primitive Dataset with 50 demonstrations per primitive task (1,000 in total). Third, we develop systematic evaluation metrics about execution stability and generalization robustness to evaluate CodeAsPolicy, VLA, and language-conditioned H-VLA approaches. Our comprehensive evaluation reveals critical limitations in current methods: models struggle with interactive reasoning tasks due to significant gaps between visual understanding and motor execution. We provide fine-grained analysis of these limitations.

</details>


### [433] [Time-Division Multiplexing Actuation in Tendon-Driven Arms: Lightweight Design and Fault Tolerance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2604.16887)
*Shoujie Li,Changqing Guo,Jianle Xu,Hong Luo,Xueqian Wang,Wenbo Ding,Bin Liang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: TDMA enables tendon-driven robots to reduce actuators while maintaining torque and fault tolerance, demonstrated by MuxArm with 2.17kg weight, 10kg capacity, and 1% accuracy even under servo failure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Aerospace robotic manipulators need lightweight construction and fault tolerance to meet weight limits and ensure reliability in remote, hazardous environments.

Method: Time-Division Multiplexing Actuation (TDMA) with vertically-stacked rotational selection structure, TDM motors, electromagnetic clutches, worm gear reducer, dual-encoder system, and actuation space trajectory planning algorithm.

Result: MuxArm achieves 2.17kg self-weight, 10kg actuator driving capacity, 1% end-effector accuracy under partial servo failure, and up to 50% tendon load reduction compared to conventional methods.

Conclusion: TDMA provides practical approach for tendon-driven robots to balance lightweight construction with fault tolerance, enabling robust performance in aerospace applications across diverse environments.
